title: DSI Drug and Alcohol Testing Contract One-Year Extension for CDL Compliance
summary: One-year extension of contract with DSI for random drug/alcohol testing and CDL testing to maintain federal compliance. Originally a 2+1+1 year agreement. HR Director confirms satisfactory service. Will go to RFP when contract expires.

title: Resolution Urging New York State Support for NY For All Act
summary: Council member introduces resolution urging NY State Senate, Assembly, and Governor to support the New York For All Act. Seeks co-sponsors from fellow councilors; no immediate co-signers at this session.

title: OCRRA Battery Recycling Site Expansion and Park Memorial Donations
summary: Parks Commissioner presents OCRRA battery recycling program expanding to four community center sites (Southwest, Northeast, Cecil, Magnarelli). Council acknowledges responsiveness to site concerns. Four park donations accepted: $2,000 from Eastwood Neighborhood Association for Cummings Field bench, $1,054 from Syracuse Inner Rotary Club for Kirk Park bench and memorial plaque honoring Al Turner, two $100 TNT donations for Franklin Square plaques honoring Ben Wash and TNT community contributions.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Fire Department Equipment, EMS Ambulance Billing, First Responder Wellness, and Training Center Usage
summary: Multiple fire and police items: (1) SPD Deputy Chief presents CBH Group agreement for first responder resiliency speaker event hosted jointly with Onondaga County Sheriff's Department; (2) Fire Chief requests aerial ladder truck purchase under regular replacement program — 10-truck fleet with 10-year frontline/15-year total lifecycle, rotating by station usage; (3) Multimed third-party ambulance billing contract extension — Fire Dept has operated ambulance service since 1950s, expanded full-time during pandemic due to private ambulance staffing shortages, 2 ambulances with ~40 paramedics and 300+ EMTs; (4) NYSAFC annual conference hosting at training center for $8,500. Council requests apparatus replacement data and training center revenue history.
entities: New York State Association of Fire Chiefs

title: South Geddes Street Lease Amendment to Include Pedestrian-Vehicle Bridge
summary: Department of Assessment requests amending Ordinance 994 to include the bridge connecting properties at 115 and 116-18 South Geddes Street in railroad lease agreement. Bridge provides both pedestrian and vehicle transit between the two properties; separate railroad bridge exists farther north. Item 26 (Parkside Commons) held for committee meeting.

title: Appraisal Authorization for 725 Harrison Street (Former Washington Irving School)
summary: Department of Assessment requests authorization to hire appraiser for 725 Harrison Street, the former Washington Irving School used as school district administrative offices. Building approaching its 100th birthday. Property was part of a 6-parcel RFP package issued approximately two years ago (3 for relocation, 3 for disposition); no transactions closed yet. Johnson Center relocated but redevelopment continues. Council members emphasize the building's historical significance as one of the last 15 historic school buildings in Syracuse.

title: Appraisal Authorization for Grape Alley Right-of-Way
summary: Department of Assessment requests authorization to hire appraiser for Grape Alley, a city right-of-way off Townsend Street south of Erie Boulevard, near the recently demolished Goodyear building site now used as a parking lot. Brief discussion with location clarification (East Fayette area, near former Snowflake Bakery).

title: Northland Pro Fiber Connection Extension for Council Meeting Broadcasting
summary: IT Director requests extension of existing fiber agreement with Northland Pro for the connection between City Hall and WCNY on West Fayette Street used for recording and broadcasting council meetings. Extension needed while full RFP process is prepared. Connection is fiber-only and does not include post-production support. Council raises questions about YouTube live streaming quality; directed to contact WCNY directly.

title: Southwest Community Center Fiber Upgrade for E-Gaming and Property Settlement Ordinance Correction
summary: Two items: (1) IT presents agreement with Spectrum to upgrade Southwest Community Center from coax to fiber connection to support Parks Department e-gaming program, following similar upgrade at Wilson/McBride Street; (2) Department of Assessment corrects settlement ordinance — need to rescind incorrect ordinance reference and amend original Ord. #942 from March 2024 with refund amount of $1,523.89. Assistant Corporation Counsel Trevor McDaniel confirms corrected letter with refund amount being submitted.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Honorary Street Sign for Community Leader Donna Reese
summary: Council member Patrona Jones-Rowser introduces item for honorary street sign recognizing Donna Reese, longtime Syracuse resident with extensive community service across multiple organizations and initiatives. Full history to be presented at the following week's regular meeting. Other council members express immediate support for co-sponsorship.

title: Lot Alteration, CRB Reappointment, and Axon Contract Legal Preparation
summary: Three items: (1) Deputy Commissioner of Code Enforcement presents lot alteration at 153-159 to straighten diagonal property line between occupied house and adjacent Greater Syracuse Land Bank lot for R3 zone compliance, eliminating split-zone and existing fence encroachment; (2) Resolution to reappoint Linda Bats to Citizen Review Board — council notes multiple CRB vacancies from both council and mayoral appointments are overdue per legislation; (3) First Assistant Corporation Counsel announces Law Department and SPD prepared to discuss Axon contract legal aspects in executive session.

title: Emergency Signal Preemption System Contract: GTT to Miovision Name Change and Five-Year Extension
summary: Contract adjustment changes vendor name from Global Traffic Technologies (GTT) to Miovision Technologies (parent company acquisition) and formalizes five-year term with one five-year renewal option. System enables fire trucks to preempt traffic signals for emergency response. Law Department confirmed contract language consistent with prior ordinance.

title: Magnarelli Center Sound System Grant and Items 14-15 Withdrawn
summary: Clarification that Councilor Caldwell's grant item funds a new sound and music system at Magnarelli Community Center (Machznney Park) for youth and senior programming. Items 14 and 15 (bond ordinance and authorization) withdrawn by Parks Commissioner for further work.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Youth Esports Programming, Community Battery Recycling Kiosks, and Annual Music Licensing
summary: Item 12: Agreement with Elite Gaming for instructional e-sports programming at Wilson Park (live) and Southwest Community Center (launching February 2026). Item 13: OCRRA battery recycling kiosk agreement for four city community centers, funded by Department of Energy grant expanding from suburban locations. Council raised safety concerns and suggested City Hall as additional drop-off. Items 14-15 withdrawn. Items 16-17: Annual ASCAP and SESAC music licensing for Parks programming.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Veo Micromobility Contract Extension Held for Committee Meeting Pending Council Questions
summary: Request to extend existing Veo micromobility agreement held for committee meeting at council direction. Council wants Veo representatives to present statistics and answer constituent questions. Discussion about realigning contract dates from February cycle back to January/calendar year. Eric Annis (Chief Development Officer) presented on behalf of the administration.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse Fire Department Wellness and Safety Contracts Introduction
summary: Fire Chief introduces items 19 and 20 as essential and mandatory programs for firefighter wellness, safety, and operational readiness in high-risk environments. Item 22 withdrawn as already approved in prior year. Councilor Moore sponsoring all fire department items.

title: Crouse Health EAP Agreement for Firefighter Counseling, Critical Incident Stress, and Behavioral Health
summary: Two-year agreement with Crouse Health Hospital for fire-department-specific EAP services including confidential counseling, critical incident stress debriefing, and supervisor consultation. Part of three-prong behavioral health program (EAP, critical incident stress, peer support) distinct from citywide ENI program. Fire department previously used Crouse before citywide transition and needs fire-specific services again. Council requests firefighter-specific usage data.

title: WellNow AkMed Fire Physician Contract Extension for Physicals and Medical Program
summary: Extension of existing contract with WellNow AkMed as fire department physician providing workplace safety assessments, annual physicals, and respirator exams. Essential mandatory program for fire department operational readiness per NFPA standards. No council questions raised.

title: ATF Task Force Overtime Reimbursement Agreement Renewal for Two Co-Designated Officers
summary: Five-year renewal of agreement with Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, and Firearms for overtime reimbursement covering two Syracuse Police Department task force officers co-designated with ATF. Reimbursements arrive timely. Council members commend SPD officers for community engagement with youth and procedural justice practices at incidents.
entities: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, and Firearms

title: UKG Payroll Modernization Held for Committee, Assessment Corrections, and Public Safety HQ Parking Lease
summary: Item 23 (Mosaic/UKG Pro payroll modernization contract) held for Finance Committee meeting to brief new council members on project history and trajectory. Item 24: Assessment corrections for properties at 1314 Spring Street and 112-14 BERT. Item 25: Lease of 100 parking spaces at Gettis Street shopping plaza for public safety headquarters relocation; council questions lot capacity and considers Corcoran High School as alternative. Item 26 introduced by Budget Director.

title: IT Position Transfer from Parks to Information Technology Department
summary: Budget transfer moves an IT position from Parks Department to IT Department so the role can support citywide technology needs rather than parks-specific work. Remaining salary funds transfer with the position. Budget Director Evan Loving presents.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: NYS Uniform Building Code Update Held, Housing Technical Corrections, and Land Bank Property Transfers
summary: Item 27 (NYS Uniform Fire Prevention and Building Code update) held pending rescheduled state meeting after prior cancellation. Items 28-29 are technical housing corrections. Items 30-35 request property transfers to Greater Syracuse Property Development Corporation (land bank) following foreclosure. Item 30 withdrawn for internal discussion on long-term land use.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Property Foreclosure Redemption Timelines and Community Outreach for Land Bank Transfers
summary: Councilor Jones-Rowser clarifies property foreclosure redemption rights: vacant lots have 5-6 month window, structures 8-10 months, even after council action. NBD staff and councilors conduct extensive community outreach to property owners in foreclosure. Item 30 withdrawn pending internal discussion on long-term land use plans.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Special Use Permit for Muslim Funeral Home at 4400 South Salina Street with Site Improvements
summary: Special use permit for a Muslim funeral service home at 4400 South Salina Street and West Glen Avenue. Services involve brief family ceremonies usually under one hour. Site improvements include adding sidewalks, curbing, and organized parking to a currently 100% paved vacant building, improving pedestrian and vehicular safety. Applied for by LaBella Associates under the 2023 zoning code.

title: Veo 2025 Performance Review -- Ridership Growth, Investment, and Equity Programs
summary: Chair Nave opens Public Transportation Committee to discuss Veo contract renewal. Veo representatives Jeffrey Hoover, Paul Kalabufo, and Reed Neil present 2025 results: $250K total new investment including $50K city revenue share (15c/trip), $130K in opportunity zone rider rebates, expanded operations staffing, and community grants to Creek Rats. Key growth metrics: 34% ridership increase, 1.7 rides/vehicle/day (nearly double 'good' benchmark), 30K+ unique riders (20% increase), median trip cost down 2%. Veo Access (low-income) memberships up 90%. Tipover detection launched March 2025 with proactive cure time of 4.5 hours (70% improvement). Virtual parking coach prevented 10K bad parking instances since August. Data shows majority of riders are NOT Syracuse University students -- ridership peaks in summer when university is out of session. Significant proportion lack driver's licenses and live in low-income households. Syracuse outperforms peer cities (Rochester, Toledo, Hartford, Cleveland) on Access program.

title: Revenue Share, Automated Tipover Detection, Opportunity Zones, and Virtual Parking Coach
summary: Detailed breakdown of Veo's 2025 operational improvements. Revenue share: 15c/trip yielded ~$50K to city (exempting Access and opportunity zone trips). Automated tipover detection reduced proactive cure time from 16 hours to 4 hours 45 minutes despite 140 more tipped vehicles per day -- unprecedented improvement across all Veo markets. Opportunity zones designed with DPW based on low car ownership, household income, and transit gaps: riders automatically refunded $1 unlock fee when ending in designated areas, $130K+ returned to riders, zones expanded mid-year and flexible to council input. Virtual parking coach (AI-powered, August launch): 10K corrective parking actions in real time. New real-time rider analysis rolling out: detects hard swerves, impacts, and auto-slows speed; vehicle 'perception' system takes damaged vehicles out of service. 311-style complaint response: 92% under 3 hours (up from 77%), median 2hr 11min. Van fleet up 33%, labor hours up 22%, 40+ Syracuse residents employed, 80%+ city proper residents.

title: Inclusive Vehicle Designs, Centro Transit Integration, and Intoxication/Helmet Safety Tools
summary: Veo presents two new vehicle types: three-wheeled trike for riders with mobility limitations (storage capacity for oxygen tanks, walkers, larger bags) and cargo bike targeting food desert areas (zip code 13205 identified). Centro transit integration announced: riders connecting to any Centro bus stop via QR code scan receive $1 rebate -- no cost to Centro or city. Discussion of 44 existing Veo bike racks citywide (25 pre-existing, rest converted from prior bike share program), with council requesting more at bus stops. Nave raises intoxication concerns; Veo developing cognition test tool for spring 2026 to increase friction for impaired riders at targeted times. AI-powered helmet detection tool offers rebates as incentive (carrot approach) since NY state law does not require helmets for riders 18+. Equity concerns raised: helmet mandates and ID requirements could become access barriers. Council presses on minors accessing service despite 18+ terms of service -- transitions to extended discussion in next segment.

title: Battery Management, Mid-Ride Motor Cutoff Safety, and Vehicle Damage Liability
summary: Council questions on practical operations: battery range (45 miles full charge, swap every 10 rides, removed from app at 5%), low-funds notification (verbal alert from bike, $20 pre-authorization to prevent mid-ride cutoff). Council member reports witnessing near-accident when bike motor cut off mid-ride near a bus -- Veo clarifies motor disengages but does not apply brakes. Damage liability: rider bears responsibility; Veo tracks all ride paths via 4-second GPS pings but legally requires subpoena for personally identifiable data (even for SPD). Council member notes bikes left on lawns remain biggest constituent complaint -- Council President Paniagua had one for 4 days. Virtual parking coach penalty: meter runs up to 20 minutes if rider ignores parking instructions. Charging racks technically possible but require grid tie-in; swappable batteries preferred for operational flexibility. DPW partnership sought for additional bike rack deployment.

title: Minor Rider Access, Age Verification Equity, and Multilingual Community Outreach
summary: Extended discussion on minor riders: California requires ID scan by state law but New York does not; parents can enable children using their own accounts. Veo argues mandatory ID verification creates equity barriers for unbanked/undocumented riders and could push minors to unsafe unregulated e-bikes. Council proposes teen rider program (14-17) with speed-limited bikes; Veo says technically possible but requires NY state law change plus new insurance product -- 2-year horizon, parallel effort underway with City of Denver. Access program challenges: income verification friction and language barriers despite app auto-translation to phone language (Arabic specifically confirmed). Paul Kalabufo describes community outreach: partnered with Nicole Watt from Hope Speak Open for 9-language event on north side teaching New Americans to use scooters -- north side subsequently showed highest Access program usage. Veo commits to establishing equity working group with city partnership to reach riders through public housing, libraries, and community organizations.

title: Fleet Maintenance Diagnostics, Lithium Battery Fire Safety, and Rider Violation Enforcement
summary: Paul Kalabufo details four-tier maintenance system: self-diagnostic error mode (removes vehicle from app), customer phone reports, systematic weekly rotation (10 scooters/week through full fleet), and seasonal full-fleet overhaul. All vehicles include self-sealing tires and brake failure detection. Five-step safety field check ('5 S's') performed on every vehicle touch -- given battery swap and tipover retrieval frequency, checks happen multiple times per week on most vehicles. Adding baskets to all scooters for grocery shopping. Lithium battery safety: UL-certified at cell, battery, and vehicle-electrical-system levels; practices aligned with FDNY as national leader on battery fire issues. Damaged battery protocol: visual inspection on every warehouse return, fireproof barrel storage with 'cell block' powder that vitrifies to smother fire, recycling through Redwood Materials. Warehouse electrical work done to city code with local official inspections. Violations up 51% year-over-year (outpacing 34% ride increase): two warnings then monetary fines then suspension requiring rules quiz -- approximately 200 riders suspended in 2025. Double riding prohibited; new two-person tandem vehicle coming. Meeting adjourns.

title: Good Cause Eviction Local Law Defeated 4-4 -- Helen Hudson Appointed At-Large
summary: Syracuse Common Council votes on Good Cause Eviction (GCE) local law (item 8A), applying to landlords owning two or more properties. Vote splits 4-4 (Caldwell, Majok, Ehrenreich, Williams yes; Nave, Moore, Jones-Rowser, Monto no), defeating the measure. Moore cites unanswered questions about code enforcement staffing, housing court capacity, and impact on small two-property landlords. Jones-Rowser delivers extensive data analysis: across 1,525 NY municipalities, only 17 enacted GCE with less than 5% eviction decline; argues GCE would increase vacancy, trigger stricter tenant screening, and only protect 8-9K of Syracuse's 34,575 rental units. Majok counters that protecting even one person justifies the measure and Syracuse's poverty requires protecting 'the bottom half.' Council President Paniagua confirms she cannot break tie on local laws per charter (confirmed by First Assistant Corporation Counsel Joe Barry). Separately, Helen Hudson appointed to vacant at-large council seat 6-2 (Ehrenreich and Monto voting no; Ehrenreich cites insufficient vetting process).

title: Carmelita Sapp-Walker 30-Year Service Resolution
summary: Councilor Nave presents resolution honoring Carmelita Sapp-Walker for 30 years of service to the Common Council office. Walker shares that she treated every call as if it were from her mother, wanting each person to be received, respected, and helped. Resolution passes unanimously.

title: Officer Pastorello and Maria Holmes Compassionate Policing Resolution
summary: Councilor Moore presents resolution honoring Syracuse Police Officer Jamie Pastorello for helping Maria Holmes, a 55-year-old woman sleeping on her late husband's grave at Oakwood Cemetery. Pastorello secured immediate shelter, launched a GoFundMe, and connected Holmes with Tiny Home For Good and Le Moyne College for permanent housing. Moore also shares examples of community policing: officers comforting a grieving widow, Officer Thorrett investigating an illegal eviction saving residents from freezing, and Officer Wheeling escorting a truant student back to school. Police Chief Mark Russen's letter of support read. National news story. Passes unanimously.

title: Dr. Linda LeMura Lane Honorary Street Sign -- Le Moyne College President
summary: Councilor Monto reads tribute to Dr. Linda LeMura, 11-year president of Le Moyne College and first female lay person to lead a Jesuit institution in the US. Achievements include Division 2 to Division 1 athletics, doctoral programs, 20+ endowed professorships, and the Le Moyne Area Neighborhood Development Strategy with the city. Honorary street sign at Salt Springs Road and Springfield Road passes unanimously. Items 40 (Parkside Commons) and 41 continue held.

title: BESS Moratorium Vote -- Energy Storage Location vs. Technology Debate
summary: Unnamed councilor votes yes on the 6-month Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) moratorium but cautions council not to miss energy efficiency opportunities. Argues the debate should focus on siting and zoning rather than opposing the technology itself, noting BESS facilities address blackouts and brownouts. Points out that at the prior public hearing, 8 of 10 attendees supported battery storage. Urges a comprehensive review of proper locations. Moratorium passes unanimously.

title: JACOMB LLC EV Charging at Progress Park -- Equity in Battery Storage Contracting
summary: Council votes on item 46 (JACOMB LLC agreement for EV charging stations and battery storage grants at Progress Park). Councilor Majok urges fairness for local small businesses entering the battery/EV space, warning against excluding local operators in favor of larger outside companies. Council President Paniagua echoes concern about waiving RFP processes, noting this has denied Black-owned, LGBTQ, and union businesses bidding opportunities. A third councilor notes these are the first publicly-accessible Level 3 EV chargers in Syracuse and frames the investment in climate goals and park usability. Item passes unanimously. Helen Hudson, newly appointed at-large councilor, recognized in chamber but declines to speak.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Item 39 Axon LPR Contract -- Monto Explains Revocability and Flock Transition
summary: Councilor Monto introduces item 39 (Axon LPR camera contract) and addresses public attendees concerned about surveillance. Explains council spent months reviewing the contract, which is 'highly and easily revocable' if Axon violates terms. Flock cameras are being held (not cancelled) to prevent service lapse during vendor transition. Item passes unanimously despite public disruption.

title: Items 40-43 UKG Pro Payroll Modernization -- Council Hesitation and Forward Vote
summary: Councilor Williams introduces items 40-43 (Mosaic Consulting/UKG Pro payroll modernization) and delivers public statement acknowledging months of council concern about allocating additional funds. States council is 'at a point that we need to move forward' and hopes the administration can 'prove us wrong.' Items pass unanimously after extended disruption forced adjournment and constituent removal.

title: Axon License Plate Reader Contract — Data Ownership, Privacy, and Federal Access Protections
summary: Joint Public Safety and Economic Development committee reviews proposed Axon contract for 26 license plate reader (LPR) cameras. Deputy Chief Trudeau explains LPR functionality: cameras read plates, data owned solely by SPD in a closed system (vendor has no access). LPRs used reactively for investigations, not live monitoring — vehicles involved in approximately 70% of crimes. Key discussion on federal immigration data access: SPD will not share LPR data with ICE or federal agencies absent a court order or subpoena. Chief confirms Axon system is opt-in by default (unlike previous Flock system that inadvertently left data-sharing enabled). Future goal: local law enforcement data-sharing MOAs with Onondaga County agencies, with explicit restrictions preventing further dissemination.

title: LPR Expansion Plans, Data Retention Policy, and Community Concerns on ICE-Connected Vendors
summary: Continued discussion on Axon LPR contract. Council questions data breach liability (deferred to executive session). SPD plans to expand from 13 to 26 cameras at original proposed locations across all city quadrants, focusing on traffic convergence points. Community concern addressed about contracting with companies that also serve ICE — Chief argues restricting vendors by federal client list would severely limit SPD capability. Data retention: 30-day automatic deletion for unused bulk data; evidence extracted for investigations follows crime-specific retention per Criminal Procedure Law. Council member asks about cross-referencing LPR locations with Vision Zero data (no current correlation). Real-life example cited: north side homicide solved using LPR cameras. Meeting moves to executive session for contract terms.

title: Parkside Commons Shelter Rent Request -- Developer Introduction and Deferred Maintenance Crisis
summary: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development requests 15-year shelter rent agreement from Common Council for Parkside Commons Phase 1 redevelopment. BFC Partners (NYC, 40 years, 20,000 units) and SAEVI (Buffalo, founded 1977, first NYS LIHTC deal, 4,500 units) present as joint venture developers; acquired Parkside Commons in October 2024. Property had severe deferred maintenance: 1,200 open work orders reduced to 185 (335 completed in last 7 days). Maintenance staff doubled from 6 to 12, with dedicated technician per 2 buildings plus 6 cleaners. New local maintenance manager starting. Work orders triaged by health/safety priority (heat, hot water addressed immediately). Council challenges response time claims -- specific building went without heat and residents did not receive space heaters as stated. Francisco Santiago (regional facilities manager) describes systematic approach: 5-step diagnostic process, vendor coordination for parts, quality verification by random callback. Hot water tank replacements took 4-5 business days from vendor arrival but total ticket-to-resolution longer. Commissioner of Assessment Matt OJ introduces the agenda item.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Security Infrastructure -- 351 Cameras, SPD Office, Door Access Control, and Squatter Evictions
summary: Developers detail security improvements: hired full-time security director Josiah Holman coordinating with SPD; 5-person security team operates 24/7 (1 camera monitor + 4 patrol, both campuses). Installed 351 security cameras (interior/exterior) monitored by Reliance Security; assisted in 11+ SPD investigations and 30+ tenant-initiated complaints. Replaced all entrance/exit doors with hardened hardware; doors currently unlocked pending electronic fob access control installation (~1 month out). Without fobs, locking doors causes immediate destruction by intruders. Previously required 70 in-person security shifts over summer due to site design. Cleared 20 units from squatters and drug traffickers, renovated and re-leased to legitimate tenants. Council member questions developer transparency -- doors unlocked for 2+ months not disclosed to tenants. Electric baseboard heat per bedroom, 2 hot water tanks per building. Previous owner completely separated from new ownership. Developers use OneSite software for work order management. Maintenance staff quality control: supervisor random callbacks, detailed work order documentation required, proactive vs reactive approach. Council notes skepticism about information completeness.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Site Plan -- West Campus Renovation, East Campus Demolition, and 393-Unit One-for-One Replacement
summary: Developers present full site plan: 393 total apartments split between west campus (200 units, outlined for renovation) and east campus (193 units, slated for demolition and replacement with 2 new buildings -- Building A: 5 stories at former Patrician/Snowflakes Bakery site, Building B: 4 stories at former deli site). East campus identified as unsalvageable due to: super-block topography preventing emergency vehicle access, multiple unsecured entrances/exits, basement tunnels connecting buildings enabling criminal escape, hilly terrain hard to control, mid-century 'poor planning' design. Camera vandalism, door destruction, and squatter flooding documented as ongoing challenges. West campus renovations: all-new kitchens (quartz counters, wood cabinets), bathrooms, appliances, plumbing/electrical systems, roofs, windows, facade, landscaping, playgrounds, and electric heat pump HVAC replacing baseboard. Renovation and new construction simultaneous; financing expected March-June 2026 from HCR. Commissioner Michael Collins (Neighborhood and Business Development) explains HUD requires maintaining unit count -- east campus residents must be relocated to new buildings before demolition. Council asks about east campus future; told future phase with NYS, community engagement during construction period (~2+ years).
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Community Engagement Demands, Tenant Association, Renovation Scope, and HCR Financing Timeline
summary: Council presses developers on community engagement -- initial meeting at church/Boys and Girls Club drew only a few attendees due to cold weather. Council insists on door-to-door engagement with both Parkside tenants and neighboring homeowners, not just scheduled meetings. Developers commit to monthly meetings with tenant association and broader community separately. Building details: Building A = 5 stories (former Patrician/Snowflakes Bakery site), Building B = 4 stories (former deli site). Current project funding separate from future east campus phase (future phase with NYS/HCR). West campus renovation scope: all-new kitchens (quartz countertops, wood cabinets), bathrooms, appliances, plumbing/electrical systems, roofs, windows, facade, landscaping, playgrounds, and electric heat pump HVAC replacing baseboard. HCR financing expected March-June 2026; renovation and new construction simultaneous. Commissioner Michael Collins explains HUD contract requires maintaining 393 affordable units -- east campus residents must be relocated to new buildings before any demolition. East campus problems detailed: super-block topography prevents emergency vehicle access, multiple unsecured entrances/exits, basement tunnels between buildings exploited for criminal escape, 70 security shifts required over summer due to site design.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: $220M Capital Stack, Shelter Rent PILOT Agreement, Green Space Concerns, and East Campus Future Timeline
summary: Commissioner Collins describes east campus as physically infeasible for safe operation -- camera-height ceilings allow vandalism, mid-century 'poor planning' design, security costs exceed what property can cash-flow. Councilor Patrona Jones-Rowser raises green space concerns about concentrating 393 residents into smaller footprint. Council clarifies geographic terminology -- 'hilltop' (east campus) vs 'lower east side' (near Kennedy Square) are distinct communities requiring separate outreach. Total project cost: $220M ($125M direct construction, remainder in architect/engineer fees, loan interest, permits). Capital stack: $75M from HCR (tax-exempt bonds + subsidy), ~$100M from federal and state LIHTC, ~$45M in non-cash sources (accrued interest, assumed HCR debt extensions). One-for-one 393-unit replacement; bedroom mix unchanged per HUD regulations. Current Mitchell Lama shelter rent yields $270-330K/year in property tax; new PILOT projected at ~$800K/year -- a net increase for the city. PILOT consistency required for senior mortgage and bond agency approval. East Adams comparison: 11-12 phases at $140M each. East campus future development at least 2 years out; developers and city will engage community on what site could become (open space, retail, more housing) during current construction period.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Tenant Relocation Plan, Section 8 Rent Protections, and Community Engagement Demands
summary: Developers detail relocation approach: 30-40 apartments held vacant as staging units; each household relocates for approximately one month while unit is renovated; all moving costs covered by developer. Currently 60 of 200 west campus units already vacant. Model apartment will be renovated first for tours to build enthusiasm. Minimum 30-day advance notice before relocation; developers will assess each household for pets, medical equipment, and mobility needs. Residents who decline renovation cannot be forced but developers will work to convince them. All residents on Section 8 -- pay 30% of income regardless of renovation status; actual rents range $900-$1,900/month with HUD paying balance. If resident loses income, HUD recertification can reduce tenant portion to zero with federal government covering full rent. Council demands community engagement at East Adams/Pioneer Homes standard -- calls Parkside 'second biggest housing transformation in our city.' Council member explains developers are dealing with two distinct communities on the east side despite geographic proximity; homeowners of 30-50 years adjacent to property must also be engaged. Council warns residents deeply skeptical from decades of broken promises by multiple ownership groups: 'they have been told... people have told them the same thing and look things are falling.'
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Trust Building, East Adams Comparison, Community Ambassadors, HFA Priority Designation, and Next Steps
summary: Council delivers closing message: city has 'failed that corridor for a long time' with 'decades and decades' of broken housing promises; transparency and follow-through non-negotiable. Developers show completed Section 8 project in downtown Buffalo as quality example; council acknowledges construction quality but emphasizes process over product -- 'we're moving people... we're moving bodies across the street... I am not in favor of warehousing people.' Developers completed community needs assessment (30% response rate); implementing homework help with Boys and Girls Club on-site based on survey feedback. Commissioner Collins notes community ambassador programs should be utilized; East Adams/Pioneer Homes experienced same dual-community dynamic where demolished-section residents did not identify with Pioneer Homes community. CNY Jazz held first-ever summer series at Parkside, facilitated by city. HFA (Housing Finance Agency) designates Parkside as '#1 priority in Syracuse' -- New York State committed to investment. Collins holds weekly meetings with development team and weekly HCR calls. Bilingual engagement planned (English and Spanish). Next community meeting: February 10 at Parkside community room (capacity ~50-60). Current maintenance staff will not perform renovations (separate contractors). Committee chair invites developers to return regularly for progress updates.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Regional Market Area Mobility Improvements — $3.5M Pedestrian/Bicycle Project Overview
summary: Public Transportation Committee chair Marino L. Nave convenes meeting on the RTC/Regional Market area mobility improvement project, a $3.5M federally and state-funded pedestrian and bicycle infrastructure project. City engineer Marza Malach presents: project is in preliminary design, covering Park Street from Hiawatha Boulevard to NBT Parkway and connecting streets. Improvements include 8-10 foot shared-use paths, new ADA-compliant crosswalks with push buttons, and mid-block crossings near the Regional Transportation Center. Construction estimated spring 2027, potentially delayed by NYS DOT review backlogs. Councilor Nave frames the project as a 20-year advocacy effort for north side residents without vehicles, particularly new Americans who walk to the regional market.

title: Shared-Use Path Design Details — Crosswalks, Safety Beacons, and Streetscape Integration
summary: Detailed design discussion of the RTC area mobility project. Key concerns: no traffic light funding at Carbon/Hiawatha crosswalk (TAP grant restricts to pedestrian/bike only, no vehicular improvements), but flashing beacons may be added for safety. Parks department will be engaged for street trees and canopy coverage. Council members raise snow removal for shared-use paths and green infrastructure design for stormwater management. Shared-use path dimensions vary: 10-foot where right-of-way allows, 5-foot sidewalk in constrained areas. Discussion of bike/pedestrian lane markings and prevention of vehicles parking on paths. Burn Dairy proposed new store at Park/NBT intersection could increase local traffic.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: New American Pedestrian Safety Advocacy and Green Infrastructure Design
summary: Councilor Nave shares personal account of witnessing a new American family struggling to walk in the road carrying groceries in winter due to lack of sidewalks, framing the project as essential equity infrastructure. Council member Ehrenreich advocates for green infrastructure integration: snow swales, rain management, and tree canopy to improve year-round usability and reduce maintenance burden. Engineer confirms preliminary design will incorporate these elements. Public comment period closing Friday; public meeting was well-attended. Discussion of regional market expansion plans and proposed Burn Dairy store affecting future traffic patterns.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Public Testimony on Park Street Pedestrian Deaths and Sidewalk Infrastructure Gaps
summary: Neighborhood activist John Meyer (Washington Square Neighborhood Watch/Task Force, 5154 Park St) delivers public testimony on pedestrian dangers. Reports 3 deaths in 3 years on Park Street and describes new Americans from nearby mosque walking in roads to reach the regional market. Recounts incident of woman losing groceries when struck by car at unfinished crosswalk. Meyer has published a 10-year goal of walkable access to the regional market. Engineer confirms project will install 2 crosswalks across Park Street, ADA corners with push buttons, and 10-foot shared-use paths connecting to DPW's Washington Square path. Additional questions about bike racks, Veo stations, and bus shelters — funding limited but locations can be designed into plans. Council also raises vehicle parking on shared-use paths as enforcement concern.

title: Lead Water Sampling Program -- Testing Compliance and Full Service Line Replacement Strategy
summary: Chair Dr. Chol Majok opens Public Works Committee meeting on lead in residential water. DPW Commissioner reports third consecutive testing cycle under EPA 15 ppb action level for 100-home sampling program. City transitioning testing vendor from 120 Water to Trupanti (RFP winner, serves St. Paul and Minneapolis, first NY State client, same lab). Cadmus Group engineering study underway with EPA to prepare for 2027 standard reduction from 15 to 10 ppb. Commissioner chose full service line replacements (public + private side, main to meter) rather than EPA-mandated public-side only -- completed 800 full replacements against 1,500 public-side mandate. City committed to full replacement going forward. Orthophosphate treatment coats interior of lead pipes; residents advised to flush cold water mornings. Water from Skaneateles Lake is lead-free at source -- lead enters through individual home service lines. Commissioner is a city resident with a lead service line himself.

title: Service Line Replacement Map, EPA 2027 Full-Replacement Mandate, and Homeowner Coordination
summary: Commissioner presents 2,900-home replacement map targeting homes where public-side was previously replaced (2005-2007) but private-side lead remains. EPA mandate effective November 2027 requires full service line replacement (main to meter); city already proactively doing full replacements. Commissioner explains public vs private side: city owns main-to-curb-stop, homeowner owns curb-stop-to-meter. Even after replacement, orthophosphate treatment continues because internal plumbing (solder, faucets) may contain lead. 800 full replacements completed in pump station project areas: Eastwood, Mosley, Forest Hill, Sunnycrest, University area -- commissioner kept going street-by-street beyond project boundaries. Homeowner coordination required for basement access; some residents refuse. Free service for renters and owners alike. Ehrenreich commends crew professionalism observed on west and east sides. Water from Skaneateles Lake is lead-free at source -- 100+ year infrastructure is steel, cast iron, ductile iron (even hollowed Adirondack logs) but does not introduce lead. Lead enters only at individual home service line connections.

title: Vendor Transition from 120 Water to Trupanti, Community Organization Opposition, and Filter Type Debate
summary: Discussion of 120 Water to Trupanti vendor transition: 120 Water was emergency hire recommended by Buffalo, community organizations strongly opposed them and city was overcharged. Trupanti won RFP after reference checks (serves St. Paul, Minneapolis, most of Texas); first NY State client, using same lab as 120 Water. Contract awaiting law department approval (council already voted). Community orgs not consulted on Trupanti selection. Lead Safe CNY Coalition engagement: commissioner attends monthly meetings, speaks on updates, works with 'Latoya' contact. Filter debate intensifies: 6,000+ Brita pitchers distributed in 2024 to all K-grade-1 students across city and charter schools; most filters reportedly discarded (too large, taste complaints). Council member challenges pitcher-only policy -- EPA requires certified lead-reduction filter, not specifically Brita pitcher; city should explore faucet-mount alternatives. Commissioner cites EPA standard requirements. Chair Majok pushes for consistent education through community centers, libraries, TNT organizations, and bold-letter mailings. Transportation barriers identified -- commissioner personally delivers filters to residents unable to travel. Trupanti contract includes provision for additional voluntary two-family home testing beyond EPA-mandated 100-home tier-one pool.

title: Child-Priority Replacement Strategy, Filter Distribution Equity, and Education Gaps
summary: Council pushes for prioritizing lead service line replacements at homes with children; Commissioner explains current phase covers 2,900 private-side completions via grant while citywide plan uses equity scoring for child population density. Filter distribution debate: WIC collaboration mentioned, Brita pitcher filters widely rejected (too large, taste complaints, many discarded); Council member argues city should revisit filter type since EPA requires lead-reduction certification, not a specific product. Commissioner attends Lead Safe CNY Coalition monthly meetings and personally delivers filters to residents lacking transportation. Council pushes for consistent education: bold-letter mailings, community centers, libraries, school partnerships. Commissioner reports distributing 6,000+ Brita filters in 2024 to all K-grade-1 students in city and charter schools. Ongoing debate about balancing street-by-street efficiency with needs-based targeting of vulnerable households.

title: Public Testimony on Child Lead Poisoning Rates, North Side Water Pressure Incident, and CIP Funding
summary: Oceanceana Fair (Families for Lead Freedom) testifies that approximately 600 children per year are poisoned by lead in Onondaga County -- equivalent to filling McKinley Brighton School. Primary source is lead paint, but water exposure adds risk at lower levels: NY State action level is 5.5 micrograms/deciliter vs CDC's 3.5, and newer studies show lower-level exposure is more damaging. Mandatory testing at ages 1 and 2 per NY State well-child check; children 5 and under at highest risk. Chair Majok commits to offline coordination on education strategy. North side water pressure incident raised by Councilor Nave -- very low pressure two weeks prior, possible Aqua connection; concern that low-flow water cannot be flushed adequately. Commissioner disagrees, says flowing water is fresh regardless of pressure. Peter Kings (community member) presents water transient theory: 20% non-revenue water (from 2000 report), shifting soils cause pipe movement, low/no-pressure events could pull soil contaminants into water via water hammer effect -- a potentially underexplored mechanism. Commissioner presents funding: $25M Capital Improvement Plan over 6 years for water infrastructure and lead service line replacements; $22,756,047 one-time Bipartisan Infrastructure Law lead service line grant (deemed safe from federal clawback).

title: Federal and Bonded Funding Breakdown, Lead Inventory Scale, and Bureaucratic Barriers to Replacement
summary: Commissioner details funding sources: $2M one-time federal grant for lead service line inventory, $5M bonded in 2025 to continue replacements beyond pump station projects (added Westmoreland from Genesee to Euclid and other areas), $48K/year annual testing contract with Trupanti. JJ Lane contractor actively replacing lines -- any home with exceedance in sampling gets immediate replacement. Last testing round: 4 exceedances out of ~100 participants (20+ did not return kits despite prior participation). EPA requires homeowner self-testing (chain of custody: home to mail to lab, no intermediaries). Scale of remaining work: 14,000 known lead service lines (some public-side only, some private-side only) plus 16,000 unknown lines (concentrated in Eastwood and Salt Springs areas that were not part of the city when homes were built -- no records exist). Approximately 1,000 total replacements completed 2024-2025. Previous map of 2,900 private-side completions still in EFC review (Environmental Facilities Corporation, NY State) -- delayed from expected 2025 start. $25M CIP is for 2026 over 6 years (~$4.2M/year). Commissioner candidly identifies 'bureaucracy' as primary barrier to faster replacement -- contracts must go through law, council, and EFC approval. Ryan Or (water systems construction engineer) introduced during EFC discussion.

title: Contractor Strategy, Construction Timeline, Community Communication, and Language Barriers
summary: Commissioner details proactive contractor strategy: broke contracts into smaller packages to attract more bidders -- 5 contractors at pre-bid conference, awarded to 2 (Phase 1: private-side-only 2,900 homes). Phase 2 planned to draw additional contractors. Strategy anticipates fierce nationwide competition for contractors when 2027 EPA mandate takes effect (Chicago alone has 400K lead lines). Competitive bidding produced below-expected costs. JJ Lane continues as active replacement contractor with construction manager and engineering firm field oversight. Target: 2,900 homes in approximately one year. Council requests street-level replacement schedule on city website instead of dot-map. Language barriers identified as factor in testing participation dropoff -- second-round returns fell to ~100 (from 120 first round) as residents who passed earlier feel no urgency. Council proposes using district councilors and community organizations to encourage participation. Chair Majok commits to collective meeting: commissioner, Kevin Henry (executive overseeing DPW), community organizations (Families for Lead Freedom, Lead Safe CNY Coalition), and council members to align efforts.

title: State Aid Resolution -- Increase Aid to Municipalities
summary: Councilor Hudson introduces item 10, a resolution co-sponsored with Council President Paniagua requesting increased state aid to municipalities. Council unanimously supportive with brief enthusiastic endorsement. Dr. Chol Majok transitions to items 11-23.

title: SU West Campus One-Way to Two-Way Street Conversion -- Safety and Traffic Study
summary: Extensive discussion of Syracuse University's request to convert Stadium Place and Standard Street from one-way to two-way traffic. Neil Burke (DPW) clarifies SU initiated the request. Jennifer Bbeby (SU Campus Planning) provides I-81/dome shuttle context -- streets were two-way until 1979, switched to one-way for dome shuttle that no longer uses the route. Traffic engineer Amy Dake (Pasro Associates) presents safety case: two-way streets reduce speeds, improve pedestrian visibility, and are safer for cyclists. Council scrutinizes pedestrian safety near dormitories, congestion concerns during I-81 construction, and alignment with Vision Zero. Area not in high injury network. SU offers to absorb signage/restriping costs. Streets have already operated as two-way during recent dome events.

title: DPW Capital Package -- Salt Barn $4M, Fleet Equipment, TIP Projects, Lodi Street Overrun
summary: Anne Fordock (DPW First Deputy Commissioner) presents items 12-13: $4M salt barn replacement, 100% CHIPS-reimbursable, Oct 15 completion target to avoid disrupting snow operations -- two buildings for treated and untreated salt. Jeff Regola (Fleet Director) covers items 14-15 (road reconstruction equipment, CHIPS-reimbursable) and 16-17 (standard fleet capital, not CHIPS). Mary Robinson (City Engineer) presents item 18 (Inner Harbor sidewalk TIP, 80% federal, new sidewalks on Van Rensselaer and West Kirkpatrick including bridge), item 19 (Westside Trail preliminary design $386K, 80% federal, Lebart Park to Magnolia via abandoned railroad bridge), and item 20 (Lodi Street connector $125K cost overrun, total $475K). Council requests comprehensive trails review meeting with Parks and DPW including Cold Brook plans.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: James Street TIP, Chaffi Bridge, Hazard Mitigation, Parks Capital, Inner Harbor Railings $3M, syr.gov Hosting
summary: Omnibus segment: (1) Item 21 -- CNS Engineers $29K increase for James Street improvement TIP (South Salina to Grant Blvd), construction ~2028. (2) Chaffi Ave pedestrian bridge reapplication (failed 2024, connected to Creek Walk Phase 3). (3) Item 23 -- Onondaga County Hazard Mitigation Plan adoption, required for FEMA 15% flood insurance discount; debate about neighborhoods added to flood zones that haven't flooded. (4) Item 24 -- Rose Society $2K plaque donation. (5) Items 25/56/57 -- Inner Harbor railings $3M comprehensive replacement, code-noncompliant, needed to permit festivals when Clinton Square loses access to I-81 in 2027; 27,000 attended Pride there last year. (6) Items 54/55 -- Progress Park playground $500K, first all-inclusive ADA playground with shipping container rental stand. (7) Item 26 -- 200 hemlock trees. (8) Item 27 -- Connect Kids $20K field trip grant. (9) syr.gov website hosting co-terming to fiscal year. Commissioner Saiisha Burke and Josh Wilcox present parks items.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Vision Zero CIP Authorization
summary: Neil Burke (DPW) presents items 29-30 authorizing the CIP program for Vision Zero work. Schedule A with project details was intended for the packet but may not have been included; Burke commits to sharing immediately. Council requests it be sent to the Law Department for review.

title: SFD Bryer Code Compliance Software -- No Cost Three-Year Agreement
summary: Deputy Chief Elton Davis presents item 31, a three-year agreement with Bryer for code compliance software used for sprinkler system and fire alarm report submissions. No cost to the city. Council enthusiastically approves.

title: SPD NAN Associates IT Amendment $120K to $200K -- 1153 West Fayette Move Update
summary: First Deputy Chief Rich Trudeau presents item 32, amending NAN Associates IT services contract from $120K to $200K to cover 1153 West Fayette move IT infrastructure (door locks, cameras, network). No additional funding -- moving within existing account. Interim bridge until city IT RFP (already issued by Dave Proak) is awarded. Move update: fire completely moved to 6th floor, moving down floors, on schedule. Council asks about community room -- no designated room but training rooms with partitions available for community events. Council interested in simulation room and planned police/fire museum in public entrance.

title: Reduced Guns Office DOJ Grant Reprogramming -- Unbelievable and OG's Out, Funds to Southwest Center and Good Life
summary: Latif Johnson-Kenzie (Director, Reduced Guns Office) presents fund reprogramming across items 33-36. Item 33: Rescind Unbelievable contract ($186,472) -- signed 2022, no work performed in two years. Chief Strategy Officer Stephanie Pasqual clarifies contract also expired Jan 31, 2026. Item 35: OG's contract expired; both organizations' funds redistributed to Southwest Center (Syracuse Model Neighborhood, $272,278) and Good Life (with Salvation Army for victim family services). Grant expires Sept 30, 2026 -- Biden-era DOJ, no similar grants under current administration. EXALT program (item 37, held for budget correction) co-located with On Point on Harrison Street, started ~3 weeks ago with cohort of 10 youth from probation/Good Life referrals. Council demands committee meeting with agency presentations, org chart, and full DOJ budget before voting.

title: STRIVE Grant Year 2 -- Intimate Partner Violence Reduction
summary: First Deputy Chief Rich Trudeau presents item 38, year 2 of the STRIVE (Statewide Targeted Intimate Partner Violence Reduction) grant from NYS DCJS. Funds two detectives (up from one in year 1), overtime, and a Domestic Violence High-Risk Team (DVHRT) -- multidisciplinary rapid-response team with SPD, Vera House, and DA's office providing immediate relief to highest-risk DV victims. Team coordinates relocations, orders of protection, and comprehensive case analysis. Council asks about out-of-town relocations and DA coordination.

title: SPD PAL Program -- $50K DCJS Anti-Violence Grant
summary: Deputy Chief Tom Series presents item 39, a $50,000 grant from NYS DCJS Anti-Violence Initiative for the Police Athletic League. Funding covers equipment, uniforms, field trips, a teens-and-cops podcast series, a barbershop skills program for non-traditional career paths, and CPR/first aid certification for youth.

title: Police and Fire Budget Audit Resolution
summary: Councilor Donna Moore introduces item 40, a resolution requesting the city auditor conduct an independent budget review of police and fire departments within 3 weeks using existing resources. Council clarifies this is not an RFP for external audit -- the auditor will use in-house staff. Brief debate about scope; at least one councilor declines to co-sponsor. Councilor Williams asks about RFP implications; confirmed auditor uses existing resources only.

title: NYS Archives Records Management Grant -- City Hall Basement Digitization
summary: City Clerk Janet presents item 42, application for NYS Archives grant to bring in records management consultant. Staff have spent two years organizing City Hall basement records, completing about a third. Records include engineering, finance, and assessment files in broken boxes with no retention schedule. Consultant will determine what to keep/destroy per retention schedules. Previous grant funded digitization of marriage records. Records being cataloged in a database for retrieval. Council member volunteers to help.

title: Water Department Employee Lease Renewal -- 20 West Genesee Street
summary: Michael Layman (Assessment Department) presents item 43, a two-year lease renewal for a water department employee at 20 West Genesee Street with a 5% rate increase.

title: Parkside Appraisal, Litigation Appraisal, Railroad Billboard Tax Correction
summary: Michael Layman presents: Item 44 -- hiring appraiser for 1939 Fayette St East property in contemplation of sale to Parkside Commons; Councilor Nave notes Parkside has new property management crew as of last month. Item 45 -- independent trial-ready appraisal for ongoing litigation. Item 46 -- tax roll correction for four railroad properties with billboards inadvertently classified as Section 7 (railroad use), returning to Section 1 (fully taxable). Item 47 (executive session on pending settlement) deferred to Monday.

title: Item 48 Confirmation
summary: Councilor Jones-Rowser confirms item 48 is ready to move forward. No presentation needed.

title: Parkside Commons Lot Sale $45K, SPD Item Confirmation, Assessment Items
summary: Councilor Monto presents items 49-53. Item 49 ready for Monday. Item 50 (SPD item) confirmed ready by Chief Trudeau. Item 51 -- Michael Layman presents city selling vacant lot at 2016-2024 Fayette St East/Ellis to Parkside Commons for $45,000 (appraised value) for Phase 1 south-side construction. Layman notes title should say 'not less than' $45K to cover recording/title costs. Brief discussion of lot location relative to Parkside development phases -- south side of East Fayette, corner lot at Ellis near former bakery site.

title: Entertainment Licensing Reform -- 350-Person Threshold, County Planning Board Referral Exemption
summary: Jake Dishaw (Deputy Commissioner Code Enforcement) presents item 52, entertainment licensing ordinance reform creating two license types based on 350-person crowd threshold. Large venues (e.g., Westcott Theater) need per-event licenses for emergency response review; small venues (e.g., 443 Social) get annual licenses. Modeled on certificate of use and tobacco licensing with revocation procedures. Responds to business owner feedback about over-regulation. Item 53: exempting certain low-impact zoning actions (variances, special permits, site plans) from mandatory referral to Onondaga County Planning Board, reducing delays caused by OCPB schedule misalignment. Larger projects still referred; aims to eliminate red tape for low-impact development.

title: Bond Ordinances Ready — Revised Schedule for City-Owned Buildings
summary: Council reviews items 11-28 including multiple bond ordinances. Jimmy Monto (presenting for Dr. Chol Majok) confirms items 11-26 are ready, noting a revised schedule for city-owned buildings on items 15/16. Hanah Ehrenreich confirms item 28 is also ready. Items include the DPW Dig Once Streetscape ($245K) and CHIPS Reimbursable Sidewalk projects.

title: EV Charging JCOM Waiver Debate — Equity Concerns vs. Grant Deadline
summary: Extended debate over waiving the RFP process for JCOM to write National Grid EV charging grant applications (items 30-32). Councilor Caldwell leads opposition, arguing the city habitually bypasses competitive bidding and shuts out minority-owned and union-affiliated businesses. Administration (Owen Kernney, City Planning and Sustainability) explains JCOM is the only National Grid-approved vendor willing to work at no upfront cost, with a Feb 27 grant deadline and potential $400K-$500K in infrastructure funding. Council reaches compromise: keep items on agenda, administration will attempt rapid RFP, but a waiver may still be needed. Council sends clear message that waivers must be the exception, not the norm. Item 29 (Good Cause Eviction opt-in) introduced and tabled by Councilor Ehrenreich.

title: SPD 1153 Move Bond — Revised Schedule A from Bond Counsel
summary: Donna Moore confirms items 33-38 are ready. Jimmy Monto notes that the Schedule A for items 33 and 34 (including the SPD 1153 West Fayette Street move plan, $1M bond) has been revised by bond counsel.

title: UKG Pro Contract — Expiration Corrected to August 2027
summary: Corey J. Williams confirms items 40-43 are ready, noting item 40 (Mosaic Consulting Group UKG Pro implementation contract) involves no new money — funding comes from a previous budget line. The contract expiration date is corrected from March 2027 to August 2027.

title: Parkside Commons and Item 52 Held — Financial Questions
summary: Rita Paniagua announces items 51 (Parkside Commons Phase 1 tax exemption) and 52 are both held pending financial questions from Councilors Williams and Paniagua. Meeting adjourns immediately after.

title: Items 12-22 Overview -- Midland Ave Lot Acquisition and DPW Infrastructure Batch
summary: Councilor Majok presents items 12-22. Item 12: $5,000 acquisition of vacant 0.1-acre lot at 636-638 Midland Ave near the creek for passive recreational green space, consolidating city ownership of a flood zone block. DPW staff confirms property has been vacant 20+ years. Brief overview of remaining items before detailed presentations.

title: NYS DOT Transportation Alternatives Grant -- Westside Pedestrian and Bicycle Safety
summary: DPW engineer Neil presents item 13: NYS DOT Transportation Alternatives grant application for pedestrian and bicycle safety improvements in the Westside/Skunk City area, leveraging existing SMTC studies. Council asks about RFP status and vendor selection; Neil confirms no RFPs yet and will return for bonding authorization. Council emphasizes early vendor selection planning.

title: TIP Signal Interconnect $260K Overrun, Bridge New York Culvert, and SU Comstock Ductbank Permissions
summary: City Engineer Mary Robinson presents items 14-22. Items 14-16: North-south/east-west corridor signal interconnect phase 2 on James/S. Salina streets came in $260K over budget on rebid, bringing total to ~$4.6M with local share at 32% since no TIP money available. Items 17-19: East Calvin Street culvert replacement over Meadbrook ($1.476M, 100% Bridge New York reimbursement) needs amended language for potential cost overruns and 24-month schedule. Items 20-22: Three revocable permissions for Syracuse University residential halls at 501 Comstock, 505 Comstock, and 315 Waverly for underground ductbanks. Council questions annual fee structure ($1/linear foot since ~2000, tied to CPI), permanent revocable permission nature, and street cut permit process.

title: SCSD Turf Field Replacement Bid Waiver -- Piggybacking Law Uncertainty
summary: Tom Ferrer, SCSD Director of Facilities, requests bid waivers for field turf replacement at Henninger (Sunnycrest) and Valley (Clary) fields, both 20+ years old. Contractor ready April 1 with July completion. Piggybacking procurement method questioned after Binghamton-area court ruling; NYS Comptroller guidance created uncertainty. Council pushback: Councilor Caldwell challenges RFP waiver practice and long-term capital planning; Councilor Ehrenreich raises turf safety and heat index concerns vs grass. Ferrer notes field turf consistency benefits for maintenance. JSCB phase 2 projects at both sites required 5-year aid reset. ITC stadium update: awaiting state approval then city engineer will bid. Councilor Majok highlights Valley field as heavily used 7 days/week by diverse community.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Pool Salt Conversion Update and SPD Helio Health Mental Health Training RFP
summary: Parks Commissioner Sasha Bur presents item 23: $2,000 payment to Sundance Leisure for pool saltwater system repair (FY2025), with March 11 training scheduled. Deputy Chief Tom (SPD) presents items 24-25: Helio Health selected via RFP for officer mental health awareness training (item 24) and crisis evaluation services (item 25). Previous evaluator Dr. Bernard severed due to conflicts of interest. Council President Paniagua expresses reservations about Helio Health's reputation, citing poor performance in wraparound housing services. SPD defends selection based on RFP results, local presence, and separation of evaluation staff from co-responder field staff.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: SPD Helio Health Discussion Continued and 1153 Move Funding Reallocation
summary: Continued Helio Health discussion: Councilor Jones-Rowser raises constituent complaints about Helio Health housing operations on Catherine Street including drug use. Council notes overlap potential with fire department mental health programs (Krauss Help People). Item 26: Deputy Chief Rich Trudeau requests $343,000 reallocation from unallocated cash capital for SPD move to 1153 West Fayette and project management costs. Previous bond authorization couldn't cover services due to bonded cash restrictions, requiring revised funding approach.

title: Senior Property Tax Exemption Expansion -- 65% Exemption, $58,399 Income Cap
summary: Assessment staff presents item 28: expanding the senior property tax exemption program to 65% of assessed value with income eligibility up to $58,399. Staff notes agreement with Councilor Jones-Rowser on the approach. Brief discussion confirms readiness for introduction and vote.

title: Third-Party Building Plan Review Renewal -- Budget Advocacy and ROI Discussion
summary: Jake Dishaw, Deputy Commissioner of Code Enforcement, presents item 29: renewal of six third-party plan review firms for building permit review. Reports dramatic improvement -- projects now assigned same day vs sitting in queue for weeks. Council requests data comparing permit volumes before/after. Budget cut from $600K to $300K last cycle; Dishaw would request $750K+ next year as firms confirmed more staff with more funding. Council advocates ROI analysis showing permit revenue per dollar invested. Discussion clarifies distinction between NYS Uniform Building Code review and planning commission/zoning processes.

title: HUD Environmental Review Officer Designation and Property Disposition Updates
summary: Kimone Jordan (NBD Director of Housing) presents item 30: designating Deputy Commissioner Dishaw as environmental review certifying officer per new HUD guidance that preparers cannot sign off on their own reviews. NBD staff will prepare NEPA/SEQR reviews; Dishaw will certify. Brief property updates: 635-637 North McBride Street in final 30-day surplus/foreclosure notice from Law Department; 532 Court Street going to land bank resolution before council.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Parkside Commons and Flock ALPR Cameras -- Both Items Held
summary: Councilor Monto reports items 31 and 32 continue to be held. Item 31 (Parkside Commons shelter rent): Assessment Commissioner OJ and Councilor Williams meeting Friday, may be ready for Monday vote. Staff emphasizes shelter rent request is consistent with and at ceiling of standard practice. Item 32 (Flock Group ALPR cameras revocation): held without discussion.

title: 1153 West Fayette AV Equipment Budget Amendment
summary: Michael Layman, Deputy Commissioner of Assessment, presents item 33: amending a prior ordinance to increase Huber Brewer's authorization for audiovisual equipment purchase and installation at 1153 West Fayette. No additional money requested -- reallocating within the $2M FFE bond authorized last summer. Council questions whether items 26 and 33 would be combined (separate accounts, different departments).

title: 1153 AV Modernization and Parkside Commons Shelter Rent Detailed Discussion
summary: Extended wrap-up covers two major topics. (1) 1153 AV equipment: Rich Trudeau details $497K from $2M FFE allocation for smartboards, dual EOCCs (fire 6th floor, police 5th floor), replacing archaic equipment. Only one partial RFP respondent; Huber Brewer fulfilled via waiver. Council praises police/fire collaboration on IT and mental health as model for shared services. (2) Parkside Commons shelter rent (item 31): Councilor Williams questions discrepancy between $215K agenda figure, $280K developer calculation, and projected $800K. Commissioner OJ explains: requesting $550/unit/year floor (393 units), 10% gross rent less utilities as alternative formula. $800K is developer projection if rents increase, not current ask. Williams presses on how future gains realized. Broader debate: council feels forced to accept tax exemption deals to get affordable housing while taxpayers bear costs. Assessment defends shelter rent model as predictable and low administrative burden.

title: iSol Talent Acquisition Agreement — Bridging Recruiting Gap to UKG
summary: HR presents item 10, an agreement with iSol Talent Acquisition (formerly Applicant Pro) to bridge the recruiting gap until UKG Pro goes live. Indeed recently cut the city's free job postings from unlimited to 3 per month, with 90 vacancies to fill. iSol provides access to Indeed plus 300+ job boards with 2-3 day setup. Councilor Caldwell offers to partner on distributing job openings to local community workforce organizations like Northeast Community Center.

title: Midland Avenue Paving Project — $620K Budget Overrun, TIP Funds Unavailable
summary: City Engineer Mary Robinson presents items 11-13 for the Midland Avenue paving project (Ostrander Ave to West Seneca Turnpike). Bids came in $620K over budget due to a one-year delay from NYS DOT staff shortages and additional curbing. Local funds cover the shortfall, bringing total to $5.286M. Funding remains 71% federal, 13% Marchiselli, 16% local. Low bidder is Nardozi. Completion expected this year.

title: SPD 1153 Parking Design — Pump Track Relocation and Westside Trail
summary: Mary Robinson presents items 14-16 for engineering design of two new parking lots at 115 South Geddes and 1171 West Fayette/Magnolia plus pedestrian access across an abandoned railroad bridge ($150K bond). Eric Annis (chief development officer) addresses community concerns about eliminating the existing pump track at Light Park — commits to building a new, improved pump track nearby alongside the Westside Trail connecting downtown to the near westside and Tip Hill. Councilor Caldwell suggests alternative parking near Ukrainian National Home that was previously dismissed as too far but is the same distance. Administration agrees to investigate before Monday.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Parks Omnibus — Breanna Stewart Kirk Park Donation, Landscape Contracts, and Community Programs
summary: Commissioner Aisha Bird presents multiple parks items. Item 17: $50K Breanna Stewart Foundation donation for Kirk Park basketball court upgrades (lighting, rims, backboards); Stewart may visit Syracuse. Items 18-23: six landscape architectural service agreements at $300K each (5 of 6 local, two-year contracts, project-based). Item 24: Cornell Cooperative Extension 4H summer camp. Item 25: $20K ash tree treatment (148 trees). Item 26: Barks and Rex contract extension ($21K increase). Commissioner highlights upcoming improvements: Homarus concession/splash pad, playground investments, Thornden Park stage. Caldwell requests tick/deer awareness signage in parks. Resolution honoring Carmelita Sap Walker for Common Council service; all councilors join.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Pi Kappa Epsilon Donates $13,280 for Firefighter Behavioral Health Programs
summary: Fire Chief Mike Maz presents item 28: acceptance of $13,280.52 donation from Pi Kappa Epsilon fraternity (Syracuse University chapter) for the Fire Department's Behavioral Health Assistant Program and Critical Incident Stress Management teams. Funds support training firefighters to identify and support colleagues experiencing psychological or mental health issues after difficult calls.

title: Resolution Honoring SPD Officer Jamie Pastorello
summary: Donna Moore presents item 29, a resolution honoring Police Officer Jamie Pastorello for exemplary service and community compassion during a December 2025 incident. Co-sponsored with Councilors Caldwell and Jones-Rowser. All councilors join the resolution.

title: Assessment Platform Migration — Image Mate to Schneider Geospatial Beacon
summary: CIO Vinnie Scipion presents item 30: migration of Image Mate Online to Beacon (Schneider Geospatial) for the Assessment Department. Platform was acquired and must align with Onondaga County's migration timeline. Implementation costs waived ($14K value), annual cost ~$8K. Commissioner Matt OJ (Assessment) notes the platform gets ~250,000 hits/year and is used by every municipality in Onondaga County. Councilor Caldwell asks about local vendor alternatives; Assessment confirms predecessor SDG was local and responsive. Council discusses broader philosophy of aligning with county platforms on a case-by-case basis.

title: Fiscal Adviser Contract Renewal — Debt Issuance and JSCB Financing
summary: Chief Financial Officer presents item 31, a one-year renewal of fiscal adviser and marketing services for government bond and short-term debt issuance. Originally awarded via RFP three years ago; firm is local (250 South Clinton St). Annual fees average ~$90K: ~$25K for June general offering, $8-10K per JSCB short-term debt issuance. First of two available one-year renewals; will re-RFP after both renewals used.

title: IT Vendor Extension — Pending RFP Award from 26-28 Responses
summary: Dave Proek (Director of IT) presents item 32, requesting extension of an existing local IT vendor agreement. An RFP was issued in August with 26-28 responses currently being scored. The extension bridges service until the new award is finalized.

title: Railroad Lease Extended to 10 Years, Assessment Corrections, Out-of-State Plates, and School Zone Camera Data
summary: Multi-item segment. Item 33: Michael Layman (Assessment deputy commissioner) amends the South Geddes Railroad lease to extend from 1 to 10 years with right of first refusal. Item 34: Assessment role corrections for senior exemptions and a shelter rent property at 1445 South Salina. Item 35: Vicky Bos (Finance deputy commissioner) presents $8K agreement for out-of-state DMV plate lookups for parking ticket enforcement, which generates more revenue than cost. Councilor Jones-Rowser reports stolen/abandoned vehicles with out-of-state plates in her district. Extended discussion on school zone speed camera program: only June-July statements reconciled due to vendor reporting issues and disputed bank fees. Councilor Caldwell requests committee meeting with data on ticket volume, payment compliance, and camera relocations. Cameras do not operate during school breaks. Vendor (Florida-based) handles collections but city added local cash payment option.

title: Items 36-39 — Item 36 Withdrawn, HUD and Code Items Introduced
summary: Patrona Jones-Rowser presents items 36-39. Item 36 is withdrawn. Items 37-38 are companion pieces regarding the HUD annual action plan. Michelle Spansky (deputy commissioner, Neighborhood and Business Development) begins presentation of HUD items.

title: HUD CDBG Action Plan, Senior Tax Exemption Expansion, and Federal Compliance Concerns
summary: Three major topics. (1) Michelle Spansky presents items 37-38: HUD Year 52 annual action plan for CDBG, ESG, and HOME entitlement grants; public meeting drew ~15 attendees with 10 speakers. (2) Assessment Commissioner presents senior citizen property tax exemption expansion under NYS RPTL Section 467: extends eligibility to those earning up to $46,999.99 with up to 65% partial exemption. Staff processed 2,800 applications last year. Councilor Caldwell has championed this for months. (3) Councilor Ehrenreich raises concerns about Trump administration requiring immigration documentation from CDBG-funded agencies. Deputy Commissioner Spansky and Commissioner Michael Collins (NBD) confirm current court injunctions prevent enforcement; agencies are NOT being asked to collect that information. Executive session with corporation counsel recommended for detailed legal discussion.

title: Parkside Commons Held Again, Dr. Linda LeMura Honorary Sign, BESS Moratorium Introduced
summary: Jimmy Monto presents items 40-45. Items 40 (Parkside Commons tax exemption) and 41 held for further discussion. Item 42: honorary street sign for Dr. Linda LeMura at Salt Springs/Springfield Road near LeMoyne College; name typo corrected late the prior night. All councilors join. Items 43-45 handed to Jake Dishaw (deputy commissioner, code enforcement and zoning).

title: Battery Energy Storage Systems — Six-Month Moratorium and Working Group
summary: Jake Dishaw (deputy commissioner, code enforcement and zoning) presents item 43: a citywide six-month moratorium on battery energy storage systems (BESS). City Planning Commission held a public workshop; an internal working group from multiple departments is developing local regulatory standards. Discussion of outreach to National Grid and external organizations, though potential conflict of interest noted since the city would be evaluating National Grid's equipment. Council members Dr. Chol Majok and Rasheada Caldwell volunteer to join the working group. Councilor Nave raises saline batteries as a safer alternative to lithium.

title: Special Use Permit, EV Charging Grant Missed, and Good Cause Eviction Hearing Announced
summary: Multi-topic segment. (1) Jake Dishaw presents item 44: special use permit for neighborhood market at 401 Pond Street, approved by planning commission with conditions for storefront improvements. Councilor Nave voices conditional support. (2) Item 45: Parkside Commons zone change R5 to MX2 approved by planning commission to eliminate split-zone parcel. (3) Extended EV charging follow-up: Commissioner Michael Collins reports the JCOM grant opportunity was missed because the RFP requirement could not be met in time. Owen Kernney contacted alternative vendors but none could provide the full package. One ongoing grant remains but requires ~$170K city match. Councilor Nave expresses disappointment at lost revenue. Caldwell asks if JCOM could still execute; Collins confirms yes but notes unresolved agreement issues. (4) Good Cause Eviction public hearing announced for tomorrow in town hall format with sign-up cards; Councilors Jones-Rowser and Paniagua to co-chair. (5) Code enforcement concerns raised about trash at West Gifford liquor store and parking at Brighton/Canon corner store.

title: Veo Micromobility Contract Continued Hold and Transportation Committee Scheduling
summary: Item 17 (Veo micromobility contract) continues to be held. Public hearing scheduled for February 4th at 10:00 AM via Public Transportation Committee meeting.
