title: Assessment Appraisal Advocacy and Item Holds Including Parkside Commons
summary: Council member Williams presents items 24-32; item 24 held, item 26 (property appraisal) debated. Commissioner Michael Collins (Neighborhood and Business Development) advocates moving forward with appraisal despite council hesitation, noting it does not commit to purchase and assessor backlog could delay the project. Council and deputy mayor agree to discuss offline. Remainder of items ready.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Donna Reese Honorary Street Sign Installation Planning
summary: Council member Jones-Rowser presents items 33-42; item 33 (building code update) continues to be held. Announces honorary street sign for Donna Reese with installation planned for February 20th or 27th; date to be announced at next meeting. Full history of Donna Reese to be presented at next study session. All council members agreed to co-sponsor.

title: Parkside Commons Community Meeting Requirements, Flock Camera Hold, and Vape Recycling Inquiry
summary: Multiple items covered: (1) Parkside Commons (item 43) held — council requests developer schedule at least three more community meetings; (2) Item 44 sent back to planning commission; (3) Flock camera ordinance (item 45) held pending Axon contract resolution; (4) Chief Russen confirms DWI item (46) ready; (5) CRB reappointment (48) ready. Additionally, Council member Caldwell inquires about vape disposal — OCRRA director of recycling explains vapes contain batteries and pharmaceuticals requiring separate management; state advisory committee developing a program. Public hearing on battery moratorium scheduled for that evening.

title: Fire Apparatus Replacement Follow-Up: Training Truck Status and Fleet Deficiencies
summary: Council member Dr. Chol Majok follows up on fire apparatus replacement data requested at Jan 21 study session. Training truck #1 has 86,000 miles, is severely overdue, and can no longer be used on roads — limited to recruit training for understanding truck components. Three spare trucks also past their operational life by one to five years. Current legislation (items 20-21) would replace one of the end-of-life trucks.

title: Fire Apparatus Replacement Program: Decades of Deferred Maintenance Nearly Resolved
summary: Fire Chief explains apparatus replacement history — decades of deferred maintenance created a 14-truck deficit across engines and ladders. Over the last 8 years, systematic purchasing has nearly closed the gap. Current request (items 20-21) for one ladder truck, plus planned two-engine purchase next year, will bring the program to full compliance with no further deficiencies. End-of-life trucks auctioned. Meeting adjourns after this discussion.

title: SU Comstock Construction Project Overview — Public Works Committee
summary: Committee chair Dr. Chol Majok convenes Public Works committee to review Syracuse University's three-building sophomore housing initiative on Comstock Avenue. Hayner Hoyt Corporation president Jeremy Thurston and SU director Joel Fury present project scope: Waverly (762 beds) and Comstock (537 beds) residence halls, with Ostrom hall already underway. Foundation work began ~2 months ago; LaBella Associates is completing third-party building permit review. City administration explains the permit/fee structure and flags the core issue: an unprecedented 2-year full road closure on Comstock was not anticipated when the council adopted the new fee schedule last year. The initial 90-day administrative closure (approved by DPW commissioner) will expire end of March; any extension requires council approval.

title: Road Closure Fee Schedule Debate — Precedent and Budget Deficit Tensions
summary: Council scrutinizes the fee structure for Syracuse University's unprecedented 2-year Comstock Avenue road closure. Fees paid so far: $75 liability waiver and $1,500/meter for parking meter removal. The road cut permit — which triggers the compounding 14-day renewal fee schedule — has not been issued. Under current rules, fees could reach ~$2.6M for the full closure. Council raises concerns about precedent-setting: legislation should apply uniformly to all developers. A councilor invokes the city's $20M+ projected budget deficit, questioning whether fee modifications for development feasibility can be balanced against the city's own fiscal needs. Administration requests 90 more days (through end of June) to develop an approach for full road closures going forward.

title: Fee Calculation Methodology, Traffic Safety Measures, and SU Tax-Exempt Status
summary: Engineering staff explain the fee calculation: ~30,000 sq ft from curb to curb at unit pricing per the fee schedule, with 14-day renewal periods yielding the $2.6M two-year estimate. The scope could expand if closure extends property-line to property-line. CNS Engineers produced a vehicular/pedestrian traffic plan reviewed by city engineers: detours via Walnut and Marshall streets, fencing, flag personnel at entry/exit points. SU commits to full replacement of sidewalks, curbing, and road repaving upon project completion. A councilor pointedly notes 'Everything but our lost property taxes,' highlighting tension over SU's tax-exempt status and the city's investment in supporting university development.

title: Project Economics, Energy Design, and Pedestrian Safety Discussion
summary: Discussion quantifies the full project economics: ~$600,000 in road closure fees per 90-day period, ~$1M building permit for two buildings, $253M hard construction cost for the Comstock dorms ($360M across all three SU residence halls), employing 600-700 workers. Buildings will use National Grid power with high-efficiency heat pumps but will not be energy-independent. Council clarifies that prior fatal dump truck incidents near SU campus were attributed to a separate University Ave housing project, underscoring pedestrian safety concerns. SPD recommended two new four-way stops on Ostrom Avenue, which have been well-received by the community. Chair thanks Hayner Hoyt and reaffirms the city's commitment to following its own permitting rules fairly.

title: Payroll Modernization Overview -- AS400 Manual Processes, Employee Self-Service Vision, and HCM System Architecture
summary: Finance Committee convenes long-awaited payroll modernization presentation. Josh Syrson (API project manager), CFO, and Mike Smith (Assistant Director HR) present objectives: (1) improved business processes -- current employee status changes require printing AS400 screenshot, handwriting changes, scanning, DocuSigning (10-15 min per change vs seconds in modern system); employee self-service for address updates, open enrollment, direct deposit; (2) sustainable payroll management -- weekly + bi-weekly + SURA payroll cycles, paper time sheets from 2,000-person organization, 2 retirees + 3 staff in payroll, recently processed 24 police officer retropay corrections dating to 2021 (40 staff hours for 24 corrections); (3) workforce analytics -- overtime cost analysis currently impossible, compliance tracking across departments, data-driven scheduling. System diagram: timekeeping (SWC/TeleStaff) and HCM (HR + payroll) shown as cyclical process; AS400 handles demographic data and payroll but lacks position management, applicant tracking, employee self-service. Current demographic data described as 'incomplete at best' and 'isolated' -- cannot generate reports or track work authorization status. Nine union contracts with complex deduction cascades make every personnel change multi-step.

title: SWC Rollout at 70% Live, Oracle Failure Analysis, UKG Pro Selection, and Mosaic Consulting RFP
summary: SWC digital timekeeping at 70% live across city (TeleStaff for public safety, SWC for civilian departments). Water fully live, Parks near-complete; additional portion 'ready' but phased to ensure support bandwidth (~250 employees max per month go-live). Ehrenreich asks why standard payroll (Paychex/ADP) wasn't used -- ADP evaluated but per-employee costs reached tens of thousands monthly; checks would mail from Cleveland (employees want same-day local pickup). Oracle Fusion analysis: previous contractor claimed Oracle would integrate with TeleStaff/SWC but 'that wasn't the case' -- CFO compares to 'Android phone with iPhone charger.' Syracuse City School District adding $900K in salaries to get Oracle operational; city would have followed same path. Re-evaluated: UKG Pro selected as SaaS ('leasing a car vs building a car') -- vendor handles maintenance/updates, competitive price, same ecosystem as existing SWC/TeleStaff. RFP issued for implementation services; Mosaic Consulting Group selected as 'most qualified by far.' If Oracle had gone live a year ago, it would have covered all HCM modules shown in green on diagram; without it, city stranded in timekeeping-only mode. Rolling out SWC without HCM foundation causes ongoing pain: new hires delayed weeks in system, PTO balances out of date, org chart not maintained.

title: SWC Challenges Without HCM, Bi-Weekly Payroll Fix, Oracle Decision, Staffing, and 'Why Is It Different Now?'
summary: Ongoing SWC challenges persist without HCM: aging software requires multiple license types (clock, computer, payroll each separate); no mobile punch capability; county server dependency adds failure points; new hires delayed weeks in SWC; PTO balances out of date; org chart not maintained. 2025 accomplishment: bi-weekly payroll period shifted from Wednesday-Tuesday to Sunday-Saturday, enabling accurate SWC time card approval (previously employees approved time before working final days). Business process mapping completed. UKG Pro selected over Oracle; Mosaic chosen via competitive RFP. ~10 employees assigned (5-6 full-time dedicated); may need 1-2 additional hires. Time savings from automation expected to offset implementation staffing. Finance Committee Chair directly asks 'why is it different now?' after two years of the same presentation. Mike Smith responds: better team structure reducing HR/API silos; more active leadership engagement; thorough business process understanding; correct software (UKG over Oracle); right partner (Mosaic via competitive RFP vs prior waiver-of-competitive-bid contracts). Smith declares passionately: 'We're sick of it. We are so tired of dealing with AS400 and processing paperwork like we're still running the Erie Canal. We're a modern city... and a major part of our office equipment is a pen. We're done. We're over it.'

title: SWC Culture Change, Module Prioritization If Underfunded, County Mosaic Alignment, and $7.8M Project Cost to Date
summary: SWC rollout driving culture change -- time sheets historically 'felt optional' with some employees not submitting for months; DocuSign delays led to paying employees after separation. SWC end of life March 2027; parallel rollout continues (timekeeping + HCM design simultaneously). TeleStaff confirmed as scheduling software repurposed for payroll; UKG Pro will properly connect scheduling with accrual management. Onondaga County also evaluating Mosaic for Kronos-to-cloud migration. Councilor Moore (experienced with Dayforce from prior NYS employment) asks about module prioritization if funding cut to 50% -- musts: demographic data, payroll, timekeeping, position management; employee self-service debated (staff argues essential for direct deposit phishing prevention, W-2 distribution, training tracking); performance management first to cut; AI chatbot module deliberately excluded from scope. Council asks total spend: $7.8M over ~5.5 years (2020-2025), roughly half SWC and half Oracle. Contractor cleanup: EY relationship ended late 2024; ERP1, Dear Alien Bait, Cherro Technologies eliminated. Active: UKG (Kronos license), Kathleen LLC (former AS400 programmer), Central City Data (former API employee doing AS400 programming).

title: Staffing Impact, Applicant Tracking Gap, Concierge Onboarding, AS400 Sunset Timeline, and Regional Systems Landscape
summary: Staff deployment question: new system won't reduce headcount but redirects effort from manual compilation to value-added analytics (e.g., analyzing water department overtime spikes vs just processing paychecks). Current applicant tracking entirely paper-based; city lost Indeed full suite access -- limited to 3 job postings/month, no automated applicant communications. UKG applicant tracking module provides centralized database, automated emails to applicants, concierge onboarding (pre-hire paperwork, integrated training, E-Verify). AS400 sunset targets: timekeeping decommissioned July 1, 2026 (successful SWC rollout); payroll/HR demographics July 1, 2027 (12-18 month UKG Pro implementation). AS400 also used for water billing and assessment -- those remain. County landscape: PeopleSoft for general ledger/AP/AR (city piggybacks); county invested heavily in PeopleSoft project costing. City grant cost allocation currently 100% manual journal entries -- modern system would automate grant reimbursement tracking (e.g., police overtime auto-allocated to grant). Rochester school district spent $30M+ on Oracle. No New York municipality currently live on UKG Pro; State of Indiana and Boulder, Colorado cited. Council asks about city peer models.

title: $7.8M Project Spend Breakdown, $15M Police/Fire Overtime Without Reports, Contractor History, and SWC Water Success
summary: Total project spend confirmed at $7.8M over ~5.5 years (2020-2025), split roughly half SWC and half Oracle. Costs ballooned in 2024 due to EY consulting. Contractor cleanup detailed: EY, ERP1, Dear Alien Bait, Cherro Technologies all terminated; active contracts: UKG (Kronos license), Kathleen LLC (former AS400 programmer), Central City Data (former API employee). CFO highlights systemic gap: $330M+ operating budget but unable to run on-demand overtime listing; police and fire departments spending nearly $15M in overtime without decipherable analytical reports. SWC Water Department success story: 5 months live, department reports fewer paycheck errors, employees now have overtime hour visibility before paychecks arrive. Culture change trajectory: initial resistance gives way to compliance once benefits demonstrated; Water first to admit system is working and saving time. County also evaluating Mosaic for cloud migration. Council member asks about department head resistance to digital transition -- confirmed as real challenge but improving with demonstrated results. Training on company time, not personal time.

title: Workday Evaluation, Training Plan, Council Expenditure Hesitancy, July 2027 Timeline, and Project Ownership Group
summary: Workday evaluated but rejected -- would require scrapping SWC timekeeping and retraining on entirely new platform. Training plan: employee-facing website with video courses and self-paced modules; managerial role-specific training; help desk same-day response for live departments; office hours sessions. SWC training added to formal onboarding alongside sexual harassment training. Finance Committee Chair explicitly states: 'I hope you also understand why the council is hesitant to approve another major expenditure for a project that seems like we've been asked to do so a number of times already.' Without Mosaic, UKG Pro rollout would take 3-5 years (no internal UKG Pro expertise) vs ~1 year with partner. Project asks: ~$1M implementation services + ~$750K recurring annual. Josh Syrson adds to 'what's different' answer: past had too many vendors producing disjointed pieces; now extensive internal process mapping done, central API leadership vs prior siloed approach where 'consultants preyed on' split between HR and payroll. Digitizing bad processes doesn't make them better -- examined processes first. Project ownership group: Deputy Mayor, API Director, HR Director, CFO, First Deputy Commissioner of Finance. Expected completion: June/July 2027. Fully cloud-based system. Mosaic contract is holdover agenda item from November/December 2025. Council requests presentation materials and projected timeline shared.

title: DPW Capital Improvement Bond Authorizations and CHIPS Reimbursement Process Reform
summary: DPW First Deputy Commissioner presents multiple capital bond items: streetscape repair tied to TIP projects (items 11-12), fiber optic trunk replacement (13-14), city-owned building improvements at $800,000 (15-16, council questions lack of itemized list — bond counsel requires useful life detail), sanitary/storm sewer reconstruction (17-18), road reconstruction program with holdover prioritization (19-20), CHIPS-reimbursable sidewalk projects to optimize state funding (21-22), engineering consulting agreements (23), and water department item (24). First Deputy Commissioner of Finance reports CHIPS reimbursement backlog fully resolved — new streamlined process with monthly DPW-Finance meetings, shared tracking documents, and vendor photo requirements eliminates past coordination failures.

title: American Leak Detection Contract Extension for Water System Monitoring
summary: Water Department Management Analyst presents extension of contract with American Leak for leak detection across the water distribution system using specialized sensors on conduits, services, and water mains. Council requests cost-benefit analysis quantifying water savings from early leak detection; staff offers to provide annual reports with volume and cost data.

title: Property Owner Name Change for Right-of-Way Crossing Permission
summary: Water Department presents administrative item changing the property owner name on an existing right-of-way crossing permission previously approved by council. Brief routine item with no questions.

title: Chainsaw Training at Heath Park and Good Cause Eviction Local Law Introduction
summary: Two items: (1) Parks Commissioner presents agreement with Dan Groves DBA Olympic Tree and Landscape Management for basic chainsaw operations training at Heath Park on behalf of Onondaga Earth Corps — two-day training, no cost to city, insured; (2) Council member Ehrenreich introduces local law opting into Good Cause Eviction (NY Real Property Law Chapter 50, Article 6a) with amended language adjusting small landlord definition to one dwelling unit. Automatically tabled for future discussion.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: EV Charging Infrastructure Pilot at Progress Park: Grant Applications and Competitive Bid Waiver Debate
summary: Major discussion on three-part EV charging proposal: paired NYS DEC and National Grid grant applications (combined $400K+) for two Level 3 public chargers at Progress Park, plus competitive bid waiver for turnkey vendor JCOM (Canadaigua-based, 40+ municipal clients). JCOM would write grants, design, procure, install, and maintain chargers for 5 years at no cost to city with revenue-positive operation. Extensive council debate on RFP waiver — members criticize lack of advance notice and demand waivers be 'the exception not the rule.' Council raises equity concerns about Progress Park location vs city center, MWBE vendor inclusion, and maintenance costs after year 5. Budget Director acknowledges need for improved procurement transparency. Also: Veo contract renewal (item 30) held for Monday vote after morning committee meeting.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs
Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Police Department 1153 West Fayette Street Move Plan: $1M Bond Authorization
summary: First Deputy Chief Rich Trudeau presents $1M bond authorization for police and fire department move from PSB to 1153 West Fayette Street. Detailed line items: movers for both departments, functional fitness gym equipment (officer wellness), radio antenna for 911 base station connectivity, interior/exterior signage, CHA project management completion, tactical training shoot house with simmunition for reality-based use-of-force decision training, audiovisual/comstat equipment, combined police-fire memorial museum ($20K), and $24K contingency. City will only bond for actual expenditures, not full authorization.

title: Fire Department Hazmat Apparatus Replacement and Ground Ladder Procurement
summary: Fire Chief presents two capital items: (1) Items 35-36 — bond authorization for new hazardous materials apparatus replacing 2010 unit (16 years old, will be 20 when replacement arrives after 4-year build time). Syracuse has Type 1 hazmat team, highest level in NYS. Purchases via SourceWell/HGAC cooperative contract. Council asks about train derailment preparedness — chief explains mutual aid and state task force plans; (2) Items 37-38 — ground ladder replacement moved from operating to capital budget due to 18-month supply chain lag. Fleet of 197 ground ladders; roof ladders most frequently damaged during ventilation operations. Council asks about high-rise response — chief explains aerial vs ground ladder capabilities, 110-foot limit. Chief invites new council members for facility walkthrough.

title: Mosaic Consulting UKG Pro Contract Approval and Payroll Modernization Funding Clarification
summary: Council member Williams presents Mosaic Consulting Group agreement for UKG Pro software implementation (payroll modernization) following previous day's committee meeting. Budget staff clarifies: $6.25M previously approved, $3.7M spent, approximately $2.5M remaining — no new funding requested. Contract is 18-month term from execution. Council identifies need for new request letter with corrected timeframe (originally introduced Sept/Oct 2025). Item 41 deferred to Wednesday executive session. Also: IT Director presents item 42 opening discussion.

title: IT Vendor License RFP Waiver and Railroad Property Assessment Reclassification
summary: Two items: (1) IT Director completes request for RFP waiver on long-standing vendor (6-12 year relationship) — police department adoption expanded licenses past public bid threshold, but vendor is the only area provider of this product; (2) Commissioner of Assessment presents role corrections for railroad properties — state reclassified railroad from non-sealing to sealing, requiring assessment role changes. Sealing classification caps assessed value with exemption on amounts exceeding the state-dictated ceiling.

title: Property Transfers, Foreclosure Completions, and Building Code Update Hold
summary: Council member Jones-Rowser presents items 44-50. Item 44 (building code update) still held pending NYS Division of Building Standards guidance. Item 45 amends ordinance for 102 Elmhurst to reflect demolition (now vacant land). Items 46 and 49 create building lot sites in current and future housing strategy areas. Items 47, 48, 50 complete foreclosure process on three long-vacant residential properties in severe disrepair — Law Department discovered missing council authorization needed to close on foreclosures that had been in process through multiple cycles.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Parkside Commons Financial Review Hold and Axon Contract Committee Meeting
summary: Council member Monto holds item 51 (Parkside Commons tax exemption) pending financial questions from himself and Council member Williams. Item 52 (Axon LPR contract) held for committee meeting on Friday at noon. Meeting moves to executive session for legal settlement discussion.

title: Study Session Ready Confirmations — SPD Parking Follow-Up and iSol HR Scope
summary: Quick confirmations on items 10-26. Eric Annis (chief development officer) follows up on the alternative parking location (1320 West Fayette) for SPD 1153 move: the site is 1300 ft from the building vs 850 ft for the pump track, has a narrow parcel limiting parking capacity, and lacks Westside Trail connectivity. SPD previously evaluated and rejected it. Brief question on whether iSol/Applicant Pro (item 10) covers fire and police; clarification deferred to HR. Councilor Ehrenreich notes she will abstain from item 24 (Cornell Cooperative camp) as her children are enrolled.

title: Senior Exemption Held for Data Analysis, Donna Reese Sign Dedication Announced
summary: Patrona Jones-Rowser presents items 36-39. Item 36 withdrawn, 37 ready, 38 held for public hearing. Item 39 (senior tax exemption expansion) held after discussion with Assessment Commissioner Matt OJ who explains the income threshold decrease would expand the eligible pool by approximately 10-20 taxpayers, with larger benefits accruing to those making less. Council requests quantification data before voting. Donna Reese honorary street sign dedication announced for Friday Feb 27 at 1pm at West Brighton and Webster.

title: Items 40-45 — Parkside Held, BESS and Permits Ready
summary: Jimmy Monto confirms items 40-45. Items 40-41 (Parkside Commons) held again. Item 42 (Dr. LeMura honorary sign) ready. Items 43 (BESS moratorium), 44 (Pond Street special use permit), and 45 ready. Brief coordination on waiver for item 46 (EV charging JCOM agreement) between Councilors Majok and Nave.

title: EV Charging JCOM Agreement Approved — MWBE Language and Revenue Model Clarified
summary: Council advances item 46: JCOM agreement for EV charging grant applications at Progress Park. Commissioner Michael Collins explains change from 3 to 2 chargers for faster Level 3 service; site chosen because National Grid recently upgraded the substation. Council secures commitment for MBE/WBE local subcontractor language in future contract. Joe Barry (first assistant corporation counsel) confirms ordinance is contingent on grant receipt with no binding contract with JCOM until grants are awarded. Karina Freeland (sustainability planner) details: no city staff time or match required (median household income qualifies), all costs covered by DEC and National Grid grants, 7.5% networking fee, city controls pricing and receives revenue. First 5 years maintenance with JCOM, city free to contract anyone after. Also: Richard Osber (HR) confirms iSol/Applicant Pro covers all departments citywide (fire and police use civil service list for sworn positions but civilian positions eligible). Council requests executive session on Good Cause Eviction legal specifics.

title: ACA Filing Fee Waiver -- BPASS/One Group Federal Compliance Submission
summary: HR representative Richard presents waiver for late-filed ACA compliance submission. One Group (BPASS parent company) handles ACA data tracking and compilation; filing fee covers per-head submission to federal government. Council President confirms she was pre-briefed and supports pushing the waiver through.

title: Transition and Item Confirmations -- Committee Handoffs
summary: Brief procedural segment confirming item 11 is ready and transitioning between committee chairs. No substantive policy discussion. Councilor Ehrenreich and Councilor Jones-Rowser referenced picking up their items.

title: Items 27-30 Ready, Parkside and Flock Held, 1153 AV Bond Clarification
summary: Councilor Jones-Rowser confirms items 27-30 are ready for Monday vote. Items 31 (Parkside Commons) and 32 (Flock ALPR) continue to be held. Item 33: Michael Layman (Assessment) clarifies the 1153 West Fayette AV/FFE items are tangible bondable goods, distinct from the $343K cash capital police moving expenses. Session adjourns with no executive session.

title: OCRRA Battery Recycling Kiosk Locations Debated: Youth Center Safety and Staffing Concerns
summary: Continued debate from Jan 7 study session on OCRRA battery recycling kiosk locations at community centers. Council members raise concerns about placing kiosks at youth-focused centers with limited staff (2 per site). Alternatives suggested include public libraries, City Hall, and Bob Cecil Center (adult-focused). DOE grant funding tied to city placement. Items 14-15 withdrawn. Item 13 held for committee meeting.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Crouse Health EAP Follow-Up: 244 Firefighters Served Over Five Years, $12,000 Budget Confirmed
summary: Follow-up on Crouse Health EAP agreement from Jan 7. Fire Chief provides data: 244 firefighters utilized services over past 5 years (~49/year). Union had been paying for Crouse services after city stopped funding. Council questions whether $12,000 annual budget is sufficient; Chief confirms based on Crouse negotiations. Contract includes renewal periods for future years. Item 22 withdrawn.

title: Public Safety HQ Parking Lease at 220 South Gettis and Multi-Layered Parking Strategy
summary: Item 23 (Mosaic/UKG) held again. Item 25: Lease of 100 parking spaces at 220 South Gettis for public safety headquarters at $37.50/month/space. Commissioner of Assessment presents multi-layered parking strategy: short-term Gettis lease (asphalt ready), mid-term train track lot (needs clearing, grading, paving — not feasible until spring). Council questions lot capacity and notes train track site appears limited. Per-space pricing ensures city pays only for usable spaces.

title: OCRRA Battery Recycling Safety Deep Dive: DOE Grant Deadline, Fire Prevention Partnership, and Staffing Debate
summary: Extended discussion on OCRRA battery kiosk safety. Kiosks use fire-protective materials (cell block/pour-over) with 20-year zero-incident track record at Wegmans. Fire Chief Monz confirms fire prevention team collaborated for approximately one year. Key risk: batteries in trash compactors, not in kiosks. Council concerns center on adding unpaid responsibilities to underpaid Parks staff at small youth centers. DOE grant has Q1 2026 go/no-go deadline — losing kiosk placement means losing all education funding and fire department training kits. Councilor Ehrenreich cites child hospitalized from discarded button battery. Eventually held for committee meeting.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: FY2025 Independent Audit Results -- Clean Opinion, $710M Retiree Health Liability, and $20M Budgetary Surplus
summary: Bonadio Group presents FY2025 independent audit results to Finance Committee. Clean/unmodified opinion on 114-page financial statements (900 audit hours). One material weakness (capital projects tracking -- repeat finding) and three significant deficiencies identified. GASB 101 (compensated absences) implemented. Audit delayed ~1 month from planned January 31 issuance due to SEFA (Schedule of Federal Awards) going back and forth through February. Bureau of Accounts (BOA) currently down one staff position, raising concerns for future audit timelines. Key financial highlights: $710M unrestricted deficit driven entirely by retiree health liability (state provides no mechanism to pre-fund); general fund budgeted to use $19M of fund balance but actually increased by $1.7M, producing ~$20M budgetary surplus; unfilled positions identified as key driver of expenditure savings; school district similarly had $27M surplus; water fund profit $3.7M, sewer $2M. Unassigned fund balance at $114M -- approximately 50% of annual expenditures, historically unprecedented. Audit difficulty rated 'moderate' relative to other municipalities.

title: Fund Balance Strategy, ARPA Spending Cliff, and Post-Federal Revenue Outlook
summary: Revenue surplus driven by ~$8M in ARPA funds (difficult to budget); expenditure savings from unfilled positions across multiple departments plus DPW equipment maintenance (~$1M) and assessment department (~$1M, due to reval not occurring). ARPA funding ($80M+ total) now essentially exhausted -- programs previously supported must find replacement funding. HUD program future uncertain. State pass-through funding expected to decline as federal flows decrease. Sales tax starting to decline ('recession indicator'). Interest income expected to tail off with falling rates. Fund balance discussion: $114M unassigned at ~50% of annual expenditures; S&P and Moody's credit rating partially tied to high balance. Auditor recommends fund balance plan: use surplus for one-time projects and capital expenditures, fund ongoing operations with stable revenue (property taxes); spread drawdown over 5-year plan; warns against supporting ongoing programs with fund balance. 'Evan' (administration finance staff) noted in audience. Council asks Bonadio for ARPA spending breakdown by program -- auditors can provide generalizations (mix of revenue replacement/salaries and project-based spending). State OSC could criticize high fund balance levels, though all municipalities similarly positioned post-ARPA.

title: Audit Findings Detail -- Capital Projects, SURA Documentation, HOME Inspections, and IT System Transition
summary: Auditors detail five findings. (1) Capital projects material weakness (repeat): manual tracking of revenue recognition and receivables on grants caused misstatements. (2) Grant management delays (repeat): $36M in DOT/CHIPS expenditures with only $30M claimed -- delay in reimbursement submission affects cash management. Recommendation: centralize grants management function in BOA/Finance. Council asks if city lost funds -- no significant losses identified yet, but delay creates risk of state denial. (3) Procurement waiver documentation (repeat): insufficient evidence of sole-source justification process. (4) SURA (Syracuse Urban Renewal Agency) payroll documentation: of 23 new hires sampled, 2 missing properly completed forms, 8 not coded to SURA (listed as city employees), 5 missing final pay rates. (5) HOME program rental unit inspection (repeat from prior year): monitoring requirement not in place by June 30 FY2025 year-end. GASB 101 increased compensated absences liability (now recognizes amounts employees may use, not just payouts). GASB 103 (financial reporting model) coming next year. IT system upgrade contracted but not yet operational; auditors cannot yet evaluate; printed pay stubs still in use ('behind the times').

title: Budget-to-Actual Revenue Walkthrough, Competitive Bid Waiver Findings, and Grant Reimbursement Delays
summary: Council requests line-by-line budget-to-actual walkthrough. Revenues: property tax $64M budget/$59M actual (short), sales tax $123M/$121M (short, declining -- recession indicator), licenses/permits $3.1M/$4.4M (pickup of $1.3M), fines $5.2M/$4.2M (short), interest income $11M/$11.9M (above but expected to decline), departmental revenues $13.3M/$15M (pickup). Assessment surplus (~$1M) due to citywide revaluation not happening; $20M budgeted for reval. Council notes they trimmed budget by ~$20M. Competitive bid waiver finding discussed in detail: auditors require documentation of sole-source justification, evidence of governance evaluation; council member notes longstanding concern that city issues contracts without competitive comparison. Parking meters acknowledged as proprietary/sole-source. Grant management delay specifically on CHIPS/DOT funding: city staff identified incomplete documentation before state submission, causing delay between payment to vendors and reimbursement claims. Budget-to-actual activity categories don't align with department-level budgeting, limiting utility of presentation format.

title: Self-Insurance Cost Projections, Debt Capacity Analysis, and Bond Rating Discussion
summary: Continued budget-to-actual walkthrough: federal/state aid $90M budget vs ~$100M actual (pickup from ARPA); workers comp settlement $4.7M unbudgeted one-time revenue. Expenditures by category: general government $59.2M/$56.8M, public safety (police+fire) $187M/$182M, transportation $28.3M/$23.5M, home/community services $17.2M/$18.5M (over budget), culture/recreation (includes all parks) $16.2M/$14.5M. Automated traffic enforcement cameras (bus stop + red light) expected to increase fines revenue next fiscal year. SURA documentation recommendation: ensure position, pay rate, and title documentation. Self-insurance discussion: healthcare costs rising approximately 10% annually, retiree health liability driven by actuarial projections; federal government described as 'most unpredictable in history' creating funding uncertainty. Debt analysis: total debt decreased from $484M to $451M (6.7% reduction); debt ceiling at $623M providing runway. Moody's A1 rating driven by multiple factors including fund balance, tax capacity, and cash position. No peer cities identified prioritizing accelerated debt payoff. Potential for future debt refundings as interest rates decline.

title: Mayor Owens Announces Return of Veo Micromobility Operations for 2026 Season
summary: Mayor Sharon F. Owens announced the return of Veo's shared micromobility service to Syracuse effective March 9, 2026, following its seasonal winter suspension. The 2025 season generated 648,000 rides exceeding 1 million miles, with 24% ridership growth. Survey data shows 66% of riders lack car access and 52% use Veo to connect with transit. The updated 2026 agreement includes cargo baskets, expanded community outreach, improved accessibility measures, and underage riding prevention.

title: Public Works Committee Opening & SU Sophomore Housing Project Overview
summary: Chair Dr. Chol Majok opens the Public Works and Infrastructure Committee meeting with council members present. Hayner Hoyt Corporation president Jeremy Thurston and Syracuse University Director of Campus Planning Joel Fury present an overview of the SU sophomore housing initiative: three residence halls totaling $360M — Waverly (762 beds), Comstock (537 beds) on Comstock Avenue, and Ostrom residence hall. Foundation work is underway on the Comstock/Waverly projects, with Ostrom already well advanced.

title: Comstock Avenue Road Closure Fee Schedule Debate — Unprecedented 2-Year Full Closure
summary: City administration representative Eric presents the core policy issue: the SU Comstock construction project requires an unprecedented 2-year full road closure, but the city's fee schedule (adopted in 2025) compounds every 14 days, resulting in approximately $2.6M in fees — a scenario not contemplated when the fee structure was designed. The initial 90-day administrative closure (approved by DPW Commissioner) expires end of March; council authorization is needed for extension through June. Council members scrutinize whether modifying fees for this project sets a problematic precedent, especially given the city's projected $20M+ budget deficit heading into budget negotiations.

title: Comstock Construction: Fee Methodology, Safety Measures & Traffic Management
summary: Detailed discussion of the fee calculation methodology — Engineering department staff explains the $2.6M estimate is based on curb-to-curb square footage (~30,000 sq ft at $500/200 per sq ft in 14-day periods). Council requests breakdown of all permits and fees per project. The university commits to full replacement of sidewalks, curbing, and road repaving upon completion. Discussion of construction safety measures: fencing, flag people, traffic detours to Walnut and Marshall Streets, and two new four-way stops on Ostrom Avenue added per Syracuse Police recommendation. Building permits for Waverly/Comstock estimated at ~$1M. City staff inspection costs and time investment also raised as a concern.

title: SU Comstock Project Economics, Energy Design & Committee Close
summary: Council members ask about energy independence and total project cost. The Comstock buildings will use heat pump technology powered by National Grid (not energy-independent). Combined Comstock project hard cost is approximately $253M; all three SU residence hall projects total $360M in construction value, employing 600-700 workers. Eric clarifies that the fatal dump truck incident involving two SU students was related to a separate housing development project on University Avenue, not the Comstock project. Chair Majok acknowledges Hayner Hoyt's contributions, reaffirms the city's commitment to fair permitting processes, and adjourns the committee meeting.

title: Parking
summary: Downtown Syracuse Foundation published a comprehensive parking directory listing 47+ parking facilities (garages, lots, and valet services) with contact information and addresses. This resource serves visitors and businesses navigating downtown parking options and is available as a downloadable guide, supporting downtown economic development and user experience.

title: Plan a Visit
summary: Downtown Syracuse Foundation promotional guide encouraging visitors to use multiple transportation modes including walking, biking (via Empire State Trail), driving (with affordable parking), and public transit (Centro Bus Service). Content positions downtown as accessible and easy to navigate across all mobility options.

title: Plan a Visit
summary: Downtown Syracuse Foundation promotional content highlighting multimodal transportation accessibility in downtown, including walkability, bicycling infrastructure (Empire State Trail), affordable parking options, and Centro Bus Service. This is visitor-focused marketing material rather than policy or operational guidance.

title: Syracuse Mayor Walsh's proposed budget raises taxes and water rates by 2%
summary: Mayor Walsh proposed a $348.4M city budget requiring a 2% increase in property taxes and water rates to offset inflation pressures and declining major revenue sources (sales tax and state aid). The budget relies on $30M from reserves and projects $6.6M in new property tax revenue, while generating additional revenue through automated school zone ticketing ($4M) and building permits ($1M+). The increase faces constituent and council opposition.

title: Mayor's budget plan includes tax hike for Syracuse residents, investment in city resources
summary: Mayor Walsh's FY2026 budget proposal includes a 2% property tax increase ($57 per $100k assessed home) to raise $6.6M, plus a $27M draw from the $120M rainy day fund, to address rising mandatory costs and declining sales tax revenue in the $348M spending plan. The budget prioritizes infrastructure (27 miles road reconstruction, 6 miles sidewalk work), public safety (Fire Station 12 reopening, new police/fire academies, SPD drone program), workforce development (800 summer jobs), and strategic planning (Comprehensive Plan 2050), with notable IT/digital services reorganization and GIS expansion initiatives.
depts: Department of Public Works and Parks

title: Safer Streets Programs looking to progress their initiative--asking for better training
summary: The Safer Streets Program, which targets high-risk youth to reduce gun violence, is requesting contract extension and enhanced training from Syracuse Common Council. The program experienced a 4-month service gap after Project Heal abruptly ended their contract in September 2024, but resumed operations in February 2025 with On Point for College. Leadership emphasizes need for better-trained credible messengers and therapists to effectively engage youth in violence intervention and mentorship.
depts: Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence
Syracuse Common Council Public Safety Committee

title: 'Baffled': Company running former Skyline responds to City of Syracuse lawsuit
summary: The City of Syracuse filed a lawsuit on April 2, 2025 against Clear Investment, the company managing the former Skyline Apartments, seeking to install a receiver due to alleged failure to complete renovations. CEO Amy Rubenstein claims the company remains committed to the project and attributes delays to vandalism and flooding incidents, while city officials and code enforcement documentation show minimal progress since the company took over in 2023.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse Code Enforcement in the spotlight
summary: Syracuse Code Enforcement Department is requesting $1M in additional budget funding, including $500K for third-party plan review firms to accelerate permit approval processes and $500K for hiring two additional code inspectors. The initiative responds to high-profile code violation incidents and constituent complaints about slow permitting that the Commissioner argues hampers economic growth.
depts: Department of Code Enforcement and Zoning Administration
Office of the Commissioner of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse Common Council questions Walsh administration over unfinished IT project
summary: Syracuse Common Council scrutinized a $8.1M Payroll Modernization IT project that remains incomplete since 2020. The Walsh administration scaled down the original four-phase initiative to focus on timekeeping functionality. Council members expressed concerns about value delivery, questioned procurement practices involving waivers and external consultants, and demanded greater accountability for future project phases. The NY State Attorney General is investigating the project.
depts: Office of Analytics, Performance and Innovation

title: Syracuse Councilor worries Mayor's budget relies too heavily on rainy day fund
summary: Syracuse Common Council Finance Chair Corey Williams expressed concern that Mayor Ben Walsh's $348 million budget proposal relies too heavily on a $27 million draw from the city's general fund reserve, compounded by a proposed 2% property tax increase. Williams warns that the cumulative structural deficit ($27M + $7M) signals unsustainable long-term fiscal trends, while Walsh defends the plan noting reserves remain $35 million above recommended levels despite the withdrawal.

title: 'We need to replace Bill Simmons': SHA resident calls for accountability
summary: Syracuse Housing Authority (SHA) resident Mable Wilson publicly called for the replacement of Executive Director Bill Simmons, citing poor treatment of residents and lack of accountability. Mayor Walsh has expressed lack of confidence in SHA leadership and stated the need for change, though termination authority rests with the SHA Board of Commissioners. This escalates ongoing scrutiny of SHA management following reports on the paused Children Rising Center project.

title: I-Team obtains email thread between SHA, city hall and developer for East Adams project
summary: The Syracuse Housing Authority failed to vote on a $2M city funding agreement for environmental work on the East Adams housing project after commissioners claimed they hadn't reviewed the resolution beforehand. However, obtained emails reveal SHA leadership and counsel were involved in all negotiations. City lawyer Susan Katzoff expressed serious concern that SHA's board action jeopardizes a $30M RCN funding deadline dependent on NEPA completion.

title: SPD explains use of "tactical strikes" seen in viral arrest video
summary: Syracuse Police Chief Joseph Cecile defended the department's use of 'tactical strikes' (controlled punches to face/head) documented in a viral arrest video from April 9, 2025, stating the practice complies with DCJS-approved use of force policy and serves a tactical purpose to prevent suspects from accessing weapons. One officer faced discipline for minor body camera violations and will be retrained; the Chief characterized the arrest as within departmental standards.

title: One police officer to face discipline following viral forceful arrest in Syracuse
summary: Syracuse Police Chief Joseph Cecile announced on April 18, 2025, that one officer will face discipline following a viral April 9 arrest on South Avenue. The incident involved two men stopped for tinted windows; video showed forceful takedowns and head strikes by officers. Chief Cecile characterized the strikes as "controlled strikes" consistent with use-of-force policy, though the specific nature of the discipline remains pending investigation completion.

title: VIDEO: SHA Executive Director insists he won't resign in confrontation with I-Team
summary: Syracuse Housing Authority Executive Director Bill Simmons refused to resign amid agency problems during a confrontational I-Team interview. The SHA held a secret board meeting that violated New York State Open Meetings Law by conducting over an hour of discussion and taking votes before allowing press and public access.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse Mayor taking Nob Hill to Supreme Court
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced plans to take Nob Hill Apartments management to State Supreme Court over $340,000 in accrued statutory fines related to code violations including broken elevators, inoperable boilers, inadequate heat, and lack of hot water. The action follows months of tenant complaints against Mayfair Management, with Building 3 residents most affected. Tenants are questioning rent obligations while living in substandard conditions.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Services

title: Syracuse city auditor releases analysis of Walsh's city budget proposal
summary: City Auditor Alex Marion released a pre-enactment analysis of Mayor Walsh's $348M FY2025-26 budget proposal, marking the first time a city auditor has reviewed a budget before passage. Marion acknowledges budget challenges while recommending revenue optimization through modernized fee structures (permits, parking, code enforcement) and clarification of fund balance accounting. The auditor also advocates for increased state support to address Syracuse's tax-exempt property crisis, where less than half of properties pay property taxes.

title: Mayor Walsh launches 'Sustainable Syracuse' to fight climate change and cut carbon emissions
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh launched the 'Sustainable Syracuse' initiative to reduce the city's carbon footprint and build climate resilience. The program received $100,000 from New York State Department of Environmental Conservation and will develop a greenhouse gas inventory and climate action plan, led by Syracuse's Division of Planning and Sustainability in partnership with C&S Companies.
entities: New York State Department of Environmental Conservation

title: DPW budget hearing held to discuss where things stand with water in Syracuse
summary: Syracuse Department of Water held a budget hearing before the Common Council to address a drinking water safety crisis marked by high lead levels (among worst in the nation per NRDC), low water pressure from aging pipes, and a staffing shortage of 16 employees. Commissioner Robert Brandt reported progress on water pressure repairs (+1.8M gallons/day) and plans to replace 2,900 lead service lines this fiscal year, while Council Member Chol Majok characterized the lead issue as a leadership problem requiring broader systemic solutions beyond departmental actions.

title: Syracuse Fire and Police Chiefs go before common council for budget hearings
summary: Syracuse Fire and Police Chiefs presented budget requests to Common Council on April 22, 2025, with combined departmental spending around $100 million (one-quarter of city budget). Fire Chief Monds requested an additional $3 million for firefighter positions and training; Police Chief Cecile highlighted cost stabilization through 10-hour shifts and proposed expanding social worker response to mental health calls as a long-term savings strategy, citing Eugene, Oregon's 4% reduction in police calls.

title: I-Team: Silence from Syracuse Housing Authority Board of Commissioners
summary: The Syracuse Housing Authority Board of Commissioners (7 members appointed by Mayor Walsh) is under investigation by NBC3's I-Team for failed leadership, dysfunction, and Open Meetings Law violations. The board oversees the East Adams Neighborhood housing project (tens of millions in funding) but has not responded to journalist inquiries about legal violations or executive director accountability. All seven commissioners declined to comment on whether they were aware of the open meetings violation.
entities: Syracuse Housing Authority Board of Commissioners

title: City of Syracuse cracks down further on apartment slumlords with Nob Hill its latest target
summary: Syracuse Mayor Ben Walsh announced court action against Nob Hill Apartments (managed by Mayfair Management Group) for $340,000 in code violations plus $100/day penalties. The 760-unit complex has deteriorated under current ownership, with residents reporting months without hot water, safety issues, and increased crime. This represents the city's second enforcement action this month against slumlord properties, signaling escalated code enforcement strategy.

title: Syracuse lawmakers say plan to reassess property values won't happen this year
summary: Syracuse Common Council postponed a planned $1.8 million citywide property revaluation project—the first in 30 years—citing economic uncertainty and constituent anxiety about tax increases. The project, which would address 30-40% inaccuracy in property assessments, is now unlikely to advance in 2025. Council members face public concern that reassessment will raise taxes, despite the typical outcome being mixed (one-third up, one-third flat, one-third down).

title: Syracuse residents urged to voice opinions on proposed $348M budget
summary: Syracuse Mayor Walsh proposed a historic $348M budget with a 2% tax and water rate increase, requiring $27M in reserve fund withdrawals. A public meeting on April 29 invited resident input before the Common Council considers amendments by May 7. City Auditor Alexander Marion issued a first-ever budget review, warning that the shrinking tax base cannot sustain the highest property tax in city history and cautioning about long-term fiscal sustainability if reserve depletion continues.

title: Housing Authority public hearing lasts 20 minutes, with no leadership present
summary: The Syracuse Housing Authority held a 20-minute public hearing on April 26, 2025 regarding its draft annual plan with minimal attendance and no leadership present—neither Board Commissioners nor Executive Director Bill Simmons attended. The sparsely attended hearing included only journalists, press, and two public housing residents, raising transparency and accountability concerns for the city's housing governance.

title: Syracuse Housing Authority to hold public hearing on annual plan Friday night
summary: The Syracuse Housing Authority will hold a public hearing Friday, April 25 at 6 p.m. on its five-year annual plan covering 2,157 public housing units and $62 million in expected funding. The plan also addresses the stalled $32 million Children Rising Center project and a new central office relocation, amid concerns about missed deadlines and accessibility of public meeting notices.

title: Independent firm, used by Walsh Administration, finds 26 'red flags' with mayor's budget
summary: An independent audit by the Bonadio Group identified 26 red flags in Mayor Ben Walsh's $348M FY2026 budget, recommending $26M in cost reductions. The Syracuse Common Council voted unanimously to reject the budget and implement only $16M in cuts (7% across departments), creating conflict with the Administration over fiscal sustainability and departmental impact.
depts: Office of the Chief Administration Officer

title: Democratic mayoral candidates at odds over budget cuts
summary: Three Democratic mayoral candidates are divided over a $3.5M budget amendment passed by the Common Council on May 9, 2025. Deputy Mayor Sharon Owens opposes the cuts and criticized the Council for lack of transparency, while Councilors Pat Hogan and Chol Majok—her opponents in the June primary—championed the reductions as fiscal responsibility. The Mayor has 10 days to reject the amendments.

title: Syracuse Common Councilors slash budget; Mayor Walsh calls cuts "draconian and dangerous"
summary: The Syracuse Common Council unanimously approved a FY2025 budget that freezes property taxes but cuts $16 million across departments through 80+ amendments, rejecting Mayor Walsh's proposed 2% increase and $27M reserve draw. Walsh strongly opposed the cuts as 'draconian and dangerous,' citing risks to police, fire, parks, and code enforcement operations, while council leadership framed cuts as efficiency measures allowing departmental discretion. The mayor has veto authority but council appears positioned to override.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Syracuse lawmakers slash city budget by $16 million; Walsh calls cuts 'draconian and dangerous'
summary: Syracuse Common Council voted unanimously to cut Mayor Ben Walsh's $348M budget by $16M, eliminating a proposed 2% property tax increase and implementing 7% cuts across all departments. Walsh and City Auditor Marion criticized the process as secretive and dangerous, with department heads warning of service reductions in fire, police, and parks operations. The mayor has 10 days to veto the amendments.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: 'We need to do better': SHA Board Chair addresses open meetings law violation
summary: Syracuse Housing Authority Board Chair Calvin Corriders Sr. acknowledged a violation of New York State Open Meetings Law during an April 17 board meeting where the public and press were excluded from portions of the meeting. Corriders committed to accountability and self-reporting to the state, emphasizing the need for greater transparency and public engagement at SHA meetings and hearings.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse Common Council, Mayor at odds over proposed city budget
summary: Syracuse Common Council unanimously rejected Mayor Ben Walsh's proposed $348M FY2026 budget due to a projected $27M deficit. The Council passed a revised budget with 7% cuts across all departments ($16M total) and no tax increase, but Mayor Walsh and department heads warned the cuts will eliminate seasonal jobs, reduce public safety initiatives (including ShotSpotter), curtail parks events, and impact fire/police operations. Walsh has 10 days to accept or reject the Council's amendments.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Inside look at Syracuse Mayor Ben Walsh's budget strategy over his tenure in office
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh discussed his 8-year budget strategy emphasizing conservative planning and fund balance growth from $50M to $120M. The city has achieved surpluses in 4 of 8 budgets despite anticipated deficits, with investments generating additional revenue. Current $348M budget proposes $27M fund balance draw amid uncertainty over sales tax leveling and federal funding cuts.

title: Syracuse budget cuts threaten police safety initiatives, chief says
summary: The Syracuse Common Council unanimously rejected the city's proposed budget, prompting the Syracuse Police Department to warn that cuts could eliminate key public safety initiatives including noise party details, aggressive panhandling programs, and officer hiring plans. The department faces staffing shortages and risks losing overtime funding critical for these community-focused operations, though core functions like 911 response and major crimes investigations will continue.

title: Mayor Ben Walsh, department leaders raise concerns over council budget transparency
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh and department heads publicly criticized the Common Council's lack of transparency during budget deliberations, citing an eleventh-hour approval of 80+ amendments cutting $16M from the proposed budget with no prior notice or negotiation. The Council engaged an outside accounting firm ($20,750) whose recommendations were withheld from the administration and public, prompting concerns about accountability and proper governance processes.

title: State housing investments helping homeowners in Syracuse
summary: New York State's Affordable Homeownership Opportunity Program (AHOP) is delivering results in Syracuse, with the first home sold to Theresa Durham on Loomis Ave. Gov. Hochul's state budget allocates over $500 million for housing initiatives statewide, with a goal to create or preserve 100,000 affordable homes in five years. Home HeadQuarters expects to complete 40 new homes this season under the program.

title: City departments highlight impact of steep budget cuts approved by common councilors
summary: Syracuse Common Council approved steep across-the-board budget cuts affecting multiple city departments with varying severity. DPW faces 9% cuts impacting trash, plowing, and road repair; Parks 7%; Police 5%; Code Enforcement 9%; and Fire Department $2.4M. Department heads report the cuts were unexpected, severe, and will directly compromise service delivery and public safety.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Decisions on Syracuse budget cuts happened behind closed doors
summary: The Syracuse Common Council conducted a closed-door meeting on May 2nd with the Bonadio Group to review budget analysis recommending $26M in cuts, ultimately implementing $16M in reductions via 80 amendments. The non-public meeting, while technically legal as a caucus, has triggered transparency concerns from the mayor's administration and open government advocates who question whether budget decisions should occur outside public view.

title: Syracuse common council stands by budget cuts as mayor decides his next move
summary: The Syracuse Common Council unanimously approved $16 million in budget cuts across city departments, eliminating a proposed property tax increase and reducing reserve draws. Acting Council President Pat Hogan defended the reductions despite criticism from the Walsh administration, stating the council remains firm on its proposal while remaining open to discussion. The mayor has until Monday to accept, reject, or negotiate the council's amendments, with a final budget deadline of early June.

title: Syracuse city budget under Mayor Walsh increased by $102 million over eight-year term
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh's proposed FY 2025-2026 budget of $348 million represents a $102 million increase over his eight-year tenure. The Common Council has filed 80 amendments and proposed cuts, with the Mayor committing to review each before announcing vetoes. City Auditor Alex Marion attributes budget growth primarily to labor contracts and salary increases, with notable funding increases in police ($48M to $65M), fire ($33M to $48M), and DPW ($32M to $40M) departments.

title: EY's private meeting with Syracuse city leaders raises questions on payroll modernization
summary: EY's October 2022 private 'wavespace' brainstorming session with senior Walsh administration officials at Firebarn Community Center raises transparency concerns about the $8.1M Payroll Modernization project. Whistleblower Advanced IT owner Susan Fahrenkrug alleged EY used her company as a 'pass-through' to secure the contract, with EY presenting timelines before her contract was signed. Mayor Walsh terminated EY's contract in January 2025, but questions persist about procurement practices and contract structure.

title: Mayor issues 45 vetoes to Common Council's amendments on Syracuse city budget
summary: Syracuse Mayor Ben Walsh issued 45 vetoes on May 19, 2025 against Common Council amendments to the city budget, rejecting cuts to police, fire, code enforcement, and Parks departments totaling $16M. The Council had commissioned external analysis recommending $26M in cost reductions across the $348M budget, but Walsh argued the cuts harm public safety and city services; 37 amendments worth $3.4M were accepted.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Walsh vetoes more than half of Syracuse lawmakers' budget amendments
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh vetoed 45 of 82 budget amendments (55%) that Syracuse Common Council passed, reducing a proposed $348M budget by $16M. Walsh prioritized protecting public safety funding and revenue-generation initiatives while blocking elimination of a 2% property tax increase and water rate increases. Council has two weeks to override vetoes, needing only six votes, with Walsh signaling willingness to negotiate.

title: Walsh vetoes more than half of Syracuse lawmakers' budget amendments
summary: Mayor Walsh vetoed 45 of 82 budget amendments (55%) that the Common Council passed, cutting $16M from his $348M spending plan. Key vetoes targeted public safety (police/fire), revenue-generating departments (Code Enforcement, Administrative Adjudication), and eliminated a proposed property tax increase while preserving a water rate increase. The Council has two weeks to override vetoes with a six-vote majority.

title: I-Team: A closer look at what's behind the Syracuse Mayor's budget amendment vetoes
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh submitted 45 vetoes to the Syracuse Common Council's 82 budget amendments, protecting the ~$350M budget from property tax increases while proposing a $5/year water rate increase. Key vetoes target public safety cuts that would eliminate 20 police positions (10 crossing guards, 10 special patrol officers), risk ShotSpotter surveillance system, and impact fire department dispatch and equipment repair capabilities. The Common Council must adopt a final budget by June 2, 2025.

title: Mayor Walsh rejects most of common council's budget cuts, calls for compromise
summary: Mayor Walsh vetoed more than half of the Common Council's 81 budget amendments that cut $16M from his proposal, seeking to restore approximately $13M without a property tax increase. The dispute centers on protecting public safety and quality-of-life services, with a critical June 1 deadline for resolution before the July 1 fiscal year. Walsh cited lack of pre-amendment consultation and the council's failure to identify new revenue sources as limiting negotiation options.

title: Syracuse councilors poised to override mayor's attempt to restore budget cuts
summary: Syracuse Common Council is poised to override Mayor Walsh's veto of budget cuts, rejecting his attempt to restore $13 million in spending reductions and a 2% property tax increase. The council seeks to limit the city's $27 million reserve draw to approximately $13.5 million to preserve fiscal independence amid federal funding uncertainty. A council override vote is expected at Tuesday's meeting.

title: Syracuse lawmakers unanimously vote to override Walsh's budget vetoes
summary: Syracuse Common Council unanimously overrode all 45 of Mayor Ben Walsh's budget vetoes on May 27, 2025, finalizing a $348 million budget that includes spending cuts and eliminates a planned 2% property tax increase. The override follows the council's unprecedented 82 amendments to Walsh's original proposal and represents a significant legislative-executive conflict. City Auditor Marion raised concerns about process transparency, while Walsh stated he will develop contingency spending plans and require department heads to detail program impacts before the July 1 fiscal year start.

title: Common council overrides mayor's vetoes, approves broad budget cuts
summary: Syracuse Common Council unanimously overrode Mayor Ben Walsh's vetoes on May 27, 2025, approving $16 million in broad budget cuts across city departments. The council processed 45 amendments to restore cuts without public hearings on the amendments themselves, keeping taxes flat and eliminating a proposed water rate increase. The process drew criticism for lack of transparency, with Mayor Walsh stating there was zero public dialogue about the council's amendments and characterizing it as the council's unilateral budget decision.

title: Syracuse political infighting likely to continue as contentious city budget is finalized
summary: Syracuse Common Council unanimously overrode all 45 of Mayor Ben Walsh's budget vetoes on May 27, 2025, rejecting his proposed property tax increase and $27 million reserve fund draw. The contentious process—featuring closed-door executive sessions and a third-party cost-cutting report kept from public review—has triggered transparency concerns from open government advocates and sparked escalating personal conflict between Walsh and council leaders ahead of the 2025 mayoral election.

title: Syracuse police and fire departments face off in charity basketball game
summary: The Syracuse Police and Fire Departments held a charity basketball game called 'Bravest versus Finest' to benefit the United Way, with the Fire Department winning 92-88. Both department chiefs expressed enthusiasm for reviving the community engagement event after a 4-5 year hiatus, signaling improved interdepartmental collaboration and public service mission alignment.

title: I-81 Project intensifying across the City of Syracuse and suburbs
summary: The I-81 viaduct replacement project is intensifying across Syracuse and suburbs, moving from outer edges toward downtown with daily traffic pattern changes. The project includes a new flyover ramp in Cicero, South Bay Road Bridge construction, and plans to replace the viaduct with a Community Grid featuring tree-lined medians, pedestrian crosswalks, and bike lanes. State DOT projects completion by 2028 and urges drivers to use the I-81 Connect App and exercise caution in construction zones.
depts: Department of Transportation - New York State
entities: New York State Department of Transportation

title: Banners honoring Syracuse veterans expand to all corners of the city
summary: The City of Syracuse is expanding its Hometown Heroes banner program, which honors local military veterans and service members. Originally launched along James Street and Kirk Park for a three-year run, the initiative now extends to five additional locations citywide (Burnet Park, NBT Bank Stadium, CNY Regional Market, Upper Onondaga Park, and Syracuse University's National Veteran's Resource Center) due to high community demand. Mayor Walsh framed this as a significant civic achievement in public celebration of veterans' service and legacy.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Officials celebrate success of I-81 project's local hiring initiative
summary: The I-81 reconstruction project has exceeded its 15% local hiring goal on two contracts and come close on two others, with 233 local residents hired so far. Success is attributed to word-of-mouth, training programs like Syracuse Build, and systematic removal of employment barriers through the Local Hire Initiative—a significant improvement over the historical 7% local hire rate on major public works projects.

title: I-Team: Meeting between Mayor, Council President Pro Temp, Finance Chair on city budget
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh met with Council President Pro Temp Pat Hogan and Finance Committee Chair Corey Williams for 45 minutes to discuss the city budget amid a dispute over proposed amendments. The Common Council voted unanimously to reject Walsh's $348M budget proposal and cut $16M through 7% departmental reductions, based on independent analysis by Bonadio Group. Walsh indicated openness to compromise and has 10 days to issue vetoes on the 80 proposed amendments.

title: A rainbow over City Hall: Syracuse honors Pride Month with flag raising
summary: Syracuse held its annual Pride Month flag raising ceremony at City Hall on May 31, 2025, with Mayor Ben Walsh and CNY Pride leadership affirming the city's commitment to LGBTQ+ inclusion and equality. The Pride flag will remain flying throughout June as a visible symbol of the city's values around diversity, unity, and resilience.

title: In Syracuse budget battle, council leader pledges to override all mayoral vetoes
summary: Syracuse Common Council Acting President Pat Hogan pledged to override all mayoral vetoes on a budget that cuts $16 million from Mayor Ben Walsh's proposal to address a projected $27 million deficit. Walsh indicated he will issue vetoes on Monday, but has no legal recourse once the council's nine members vote unanimously to override; department heads warn the cuts will eliminate jobs and threaten core services like fire response and parks programming.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Syracuse Public Safety building could become apartments next to jail and courthouse
summary: Syracuse's Public Safety Building on State Street will be repurposed following the relocation of police and fire departments to a leased facility at 1153 W. Fayette Street by January 2026. Onondaga County is leading an RFP process to redevelop the two-acre downtown property, with housing (apartments) as the preferred use case despite proximity to the county jail and courthouse. The city will pay $1.8M annually in rent for the new headquarters, comparable to current costs.
depts: City of Syracuse Department of Assessment

title: Syracuse Rescue Mission: Fence separating homeless from downtown is not a solution
summary: Syracuse installed fencing along Dickerson Street near the Rescue Mission to address drug activity and protect vulnerable homeless populations, but leadership including the Rescue Mission Executive Director and unhoused residents dispute its effectiveness. The city frames this as a temporary Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design (CPTED) measure while exploring parallel solutions, but critics argue it displaces rather than solves the underlying problem.

title: Long awaited outside audit complete of Mayor Walsh's payroll modernization attempt
summary: Syracuse Common Council scheduled a Finance Committee meeting (June 13, 2025) to review and publicly release an external audit of Mayor Walsh's five-year payroll modernization initiative, which has cost taxpayers over $8 million and remains incomplete. Foxpointe Solutions completed the commissioned audit, with the meeting marking a potential turning point as the administration issues new RFPs to complete remaining project components.

title: Westcott neighborhood remains resilient amid recent challenges
summary: The Westcott neighborhood, a mixed-use commercial district known for unique retail and dining, faces challenges from recent break-ins and theater-related incidents that threaten small business viability. City leadership (Mayor Walsh) and neighborhood association (Sam Gruber) emphasize the area's resilience and economic importance while announcing increased police patrols and event enforcement to prevent further deterioration.

title: Fence outside Syracuse Rescue Mission sparks major concerns from homeless community
summary: A new chain-link fence erected along Dickerson Street behind Syracuse's largest homeless shelter (Rescue Mission) is generating significant community backlash. While city officials frame it as a public safety measure to reduce drug activity and violence, unhoused residents and advocates argue it isolates vulnerable populations, impedes emergency response times, restricts family access, and merely displaces—rather than solves—underlying issues. The CIO should monitor this as a case study in how physical infrastructure interventions intersect with homelessness, mental health, and equity concerns.

title: Moody’s affirms Syracuse’s strong bond rating for seventh straight year
summary: Moody's Investors Service affirmed Syracuse's A-1 bond rating and stable outlook for the seventh consecutive year, covering Mayor Ben Walsh's entire tenure. Walsh cited this rating as vindication of his administration's financial management against council members who have argued for deep budget cuts based on claims of fiscal mismanagement.

title: Audit reveals failures in Walsh administration's payroll modernization project
summary: A 36-page audit of the Walsh administration's payroll modernization project—which cost the city over $8 million in five years—reveals significant oversight failures, unusual procurement waivers, and incomplete system deployment. Key issues include reliance on external consultants (EY), non-cooperating former staff (Caliva, May), unpaid vendor claims, and continued employee reliance on paper checks despite modernization efforts.

title: Tower 701, geared toward young professionals, is asking for $1,850 a month for rent
summary: Tower701, a converted Crowne Plaza hotel on East Genesee Street, is now leasing studio apartments (315 sq ft) to young professionals at $1,850/month—significantly above Syracuse's $1,230 average for one-bedrooms. Units lack full kitchens but include furnished spaces, utilities, and Wi-Fi; 12% will be affordable housing. Deputy Mayor Owens frames it as a partial solution to housing crisis while acknowledging need for broader strategic housing development.

title: Report finds Syracuse lead paint ordinance may not be preventing poisoning cases in kids
summary: A report by Families for Lead Freedom Now and Earthjustice finds that Syracuse's 2022 lead paint ordinance, despite generating 7,000+ citations, is not reducing childhood lead poisoning cases. The analysis reveals enforcement gaps including repeated citations on same properties without follow-up and over half of rental properties unregistered. Code Enforcement and Health Department officials maintain the ordinance is still in early stages and expect longer-term data to demonstrate impact.

title: Syracuse councilors kill city-wide property reassessment in politically charged year
summary: Syracuse Common Council voted to withdraw a $1.8 million city-wide property reassessment initiative that the Walsh administration championed to address tax inequities across 41,000+ properties. The decision became politically charged during the Democratic mayoral primary, with councilors citing constituent affordability concerns despite analysis showing only one-third of homeowners would face tax increases. The city will continue reassessments at a slower individual pace.

title: State Supreme Court files injunction against illegal Syracuse weed shop
summary: New York State Supreme Court issued an injunction against The Herbal Center, an illegal cannabis retailer at 916 Salina St., prohibiting cannabis sales and operations. Mayor Ben Walsh announced the enforcement action, which resulted in a $10,000 penalty to the city and rejected the business's constitutional claims and privacy arguments.

title: 'These pools raised me': Public stays hopeful about Onondaga Park despite recent violence
summary: Following a fatal shooting of 15-year-old Adhon Thomas at Onondaga Park pool on June 24, 2025, the pool reopened June 26 with increased police patrols promised by Mayor Ben Walsh. Despite safety concerns and three additional child shootings on the northside, Parks Commissioner Syeisha Byrd and community members expressed commitment to the park's continued operation, noting its critical role in youth development while providing staff with trauma support services.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation & Youth Services

title: Mayor Walsh: Syracuse Democrats send message with Owens' resounding primary win
summary: Deputy Mayor Sharon Owens won the Democratic primary for Syracuse mayor on June 26, 2025, with an overwhelming majority, defeating Pat Hogan and Chol Majok. Mayor Ben Walsh characterized the result as voter endorsement of his administration's two-term record and commitment to continuity in city government. Owens will face Republican Tom Babilon and possible independent candidates in November; if elected, she would become Syracuse's first Black mayor.

title: Shootings are up in Syracuse from last year, Community speaks on child-involved violence
summary: Syracuse experienced a 46% increase in shooting incidents compared to June 2024, with four children shot in less than 24 hours including one fatality at Upper Onondaga Park Pool. The Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence, led by Director Lateef Johnson-Kinsey since 2022, reports progress through intervention and credible messenger programs, though year-over-year homicides are down (9 vs 11 deaths). Community anxiety is rising as residents, particularly youth, express fear about summer activities and public safety.
depts: Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence
entities: Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence
