title: Syracuse hosted Healthy Housing 101 event for safe home practices
summary: Syracuse City Code Enforcement Department held the annual 'Healthy Housing 101' event to educate residents on safe home practices and encourage reporting of housing violations. Code officials emphasized that renters should not hesitate to contact the department about issues like lead, heat, water, and smoke detectors, with enforcement officers pledging support to address reported problems.

title: Neighbors question safety at Syracuse city pool after fatal shooting
summary: A fatal shooting of a 15-year-old outside Onondaga Park Pool on June 25 has sparked community safety concerns and criticism of the city's response. Mayor Walsh promised increased police presence at pools, but residents report seeing no visible officer patrols at three visited facilities (Kirk Park, Lincoln Park, McKinley Park) on June 26, raising questions about implementation and transparency.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Syracuse residents frustrated by delays in convent demolition
summary: Demolition of the former Maria Regina College convent complex remains stalled months after a March fire, frustrating northside residents and local officials. The project requires finalized agreements between current owner Mark Congel and Home Leasing (developer of a planned 181-unit housing project) before deconstruction can begin. Commissioner Michael Collins indicates paperwork is nearly complete, though Councilor Marty Nave has criticized Congel's property neglect and suggested the city may need to intervene.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse Police Chief discusses city pool safety after homicide
summary: Syracuse Police Department announced new public safety measures at city pools following a fatal shooting of a 15-year-old at Onondaga Park Pool. Chief Joseph Cecile detailed a roving officer detail and deployment of school resource officers to increase pool safety and community oversight during summer months when schools are closed.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Leaders: Police union left context out of Facebook post calling Armory Square 'chaotic'
summary: The Syracuse Police Benevolent Association's Facebook post describing Armory Square as 'chaotic' with 'gangbangers' and claiming 40 arrests was criticized by city leadership for lacking context. Chief Cecile clarified that most arrests were for low-level violations like open containers and loitering, not violent crimes, and expressed concern that inflammatory language damages business and community perception. The incident highlights tension between police union communications and municipal messaging priorities around public safety and economic development.

title: Syracuse officials and residents question police union's portrayal of Armory Square safety
summary: Syracuse Police Benevolent Association's social media post characterizing Armory Square as 'chaotic' and claiming gang infiltration has drawn pushback from city officials and business owners. Police Chief Joe Cecile distanced himself from the union's messaging, clarifying that weekend arrests involved open container and loitering violations rather than serious crimes, and warned that such posts could harm downtown business confidence and tax revenue. The incident reflects tensions between police union communications, official departmental messaging, and downtown stakeholder interests.

title: 'Nothing short of chaos': Police Union leader defends vague Armory Square Facebook post
summary: Syracuse Police Benevolent Association President Joe Moran defended a vague Facebook post about 40 arrests and 5 guns recovered in Armory Square, citing transparency and public safety concerns. The post characterized downtown as 'chaotic' without detailing arrest charges (primarily open containers/loitering violations) or gun recovery circumstances. Deputy Mayor Owens criticized the post's use of 'gangbanger' language, while Moran doubled down on claims of gang infiltration and officer safety threats during late-night enforcement operations.

title: Despite recent violence, overall homicide rate in Syracuse down from last year
summary: Syracuse Police Department reports a 30% decrease in homicides compared to the same period last year, attributed to enhanced community outreach efforts and strategic policing. Despite the positive trend, the department acknowledges ongoing challenges and recent violent incidents. The decline is part of broader crime reduction efforts involving the Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence and community partners.
entities: Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence

title: IKEA could boost struggling Destiny USA, connect nearby attractions
summary: IKEA will open its first Upstate New York store in the former At Home space at Destiny USA mall, positioning the Swedish retailer as a major anchor tenant to stabilize the struggling shopping center. City leadership views the move as catalytic for regional economic development, strengthened by proximity to the Micron project, Inner Harbor, and CNY Regional Market. The store is expected to open later in 2025 and drive foot traffic to neighboring tenants and adjacent high-traffic areas.
depts: Office of the Chief Innovation Officer

title: Syracuse expands Surge Link broadband to more underserved neighborhoods
summary: Syracuse is expanding Surge Link, its municipal broadband service, to 9,200+ additional households in underserved neighborhoods (Valley, Skunk City, Washington Square, Northside, Prospect Hill, Hawley-Green) through a $15M fixed-wireless infrastructure investment supported by a $10.8M ConnectALL grant from Governor Hochul. The service offers 100 Mbps at $14.99/month (low-income) or $36.99/month with no contracts, caps, or rental fees, positioning digital equity as essential infrastructure for education, healthcare, and economic opportunity.

title: Syracuse's broadband service, Surge Link, expands to more low-income households
summary: Syracuse Mayor Ben Walsh announced the expansion of Surge Link, the city's municipal broadband service, to six additional low-income neighborhoods (Valley, Skunk City, Washington Square, Northside, Prospect Hill, Hawley-Green), bringing total coverage to 9,200+ households. The $10.8M expansion was funded through New York State's ConnectALL program and targets areas with lower broadband utilization, higher poverty/unemployment rates, and educational barriers. Service pricing remains $14.99/month for low-income subscribers and $36.99 for others, with no equipment rental fees.

title: City of Syracuse considers adding electric vehicle chargers
summary: Syracuse Public Works Committee is exploring municipal electric vehicle charging infrastructure. City staff presented three state funding programs (NYSERDA Charge Ready, National Grid Make Ready, NY DEC ZEV program) to support installation of 16 chargers across three city garages. Council members expressed interest in pursuing funding sources, with further research and meetings planned before implementation decisions.
entities: New York State Department of Environmental Conservation

title: Major Syracuse Housing Authority project faces completion date delay
summary: The Syracuse Housing Authority's East Adams neighborhood transformation project in the 15th Ward has received an 18-month completion extension from the Syracuse Planning Commission, pushing the deadline from Spring 2026 to September 2027. The delay was attributed to lender concerns about meeting the original timeline. Housing Authority leadership faces ongoing scrutiny regarding project management and tenant services.

title: Syracuse officials explore options as Trump kills funding for neighborhood rebuild
summary: President Trump's spending bill rescinded $30 million in federal Neighborhood Access and Equity Grant funding awarded to Syracuse in 2024 for Pioneer Homes neighborhood infrastructure rebuild tied to I-81 viaduct removal. City officials are exploring legal arguments that funds may be obligated and alternative funding sources to replace the lost federal dollars, while internal disagreement exists over whether the administration delayed securing the money.

title: Latest construction update for STEAM School in Syracuse reveals plan to create bus loop
summary: The STEAM High School construction project has advanced with city leaders requesting permanent closure of South Warren Street in front of the school to create a dedicated bus loop. The Syracuse Common Council will review the proposal, which requires State Department of Transportation input. The school is on track to open in September 2025 with 250 ninth-grade students.
entities: New York State Department of Transportation

title: Skan native Rachel Dewitt to swim length of Skaneateles Lake Tuesday, raising awareness
summary: Rachel DeWitt, a Skaneateles native, swam the 16-mile length of Skaneateles Lake on July 22, 2025, to raise awareness and funds for the Skaneateles Lake Association. The effort raised approximately $7,000 to support clean water initiatives for the lake, which serves as a major drinking water source for Syracuse and surrounding communities.

title: Syracuse's Mayor says he's never used the term sanctuary city
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh clarifies that Syracuse is not officially designated a sanctuary city, distancing the city from the term as the Trump administration escalates ICE enforcement nationwide. Walsh emphasizes Syracuse's identity as a welcoming refugee resettlement community while confirming Syracuse Police will not conduct immigration raids, only arrest lawbreakers. County Executive McMahon reports no ICE raids have occurred in Syracuse or Onondaga County to date.

title: Syracuse and Onondaga county leaders report progress on lead paint remediation
summary: Syracuse and Onondaga County leadership report progress on a joint lead paint remediation initiative targeting the city's aging housing stock. Only 1% of 541 tested preschoolers showed elevated blood lead levels; 72% of 8,000+ code violations have been addressed, with the program also placing foster families in 12 remediated homes. County Executive McMahon noted mixed results, with further testing pending.

title: Syracuse Police Chief gives his take on "Public Saftey trends" in Armory Square"
summary: Syracuse Police Chief Joe Cecile issued a statement responding to public safety concerns in Armory Square, crediting SPD officers and commanders for implementing a coordinated strategy to address late-night loitering and block parties. The statement emphasizes proactive policing and professional conduct, contrasting with the Police Benevolent Association's more inflammatory social media messaging about the same weekend enforcement operations.

title: Eastwood Heights renovation hits another snag, third deal kicked back to city council
summary: A reader comment expresses skepticism about delays in the Eastwood Heights renovation project, which has been returned to city council for the third time. The commenter suggests the repeated postponements may be intentional to allow the property to change hands for more profitable development once residents relocate.

title: NAACP, leaders react to Syracuse Police Union President doubling down on social media post
summary: Syracuse Police Benevolent Association President Joe Moran's social media post characterizing Armory Square as infiltrated by 'gangbangers' has prompted criticism from NAACP Regional Director Linda Brown-Robinson, who characterizes the messaging as racially coded and an 'injustice.' The post cited 40 arrests and 5 recovered guns from proactive policing operations, but lacks context. NAACP leadership has signaled willingness to engage with PBA leadership on the matter.

title: Brooklyn Pickle Owner says new Marshall St. location could open in August
summary: Brooklyn Pickle, a local Syracuse sandwich shop celebrating its 50th anniversary, announced plans to open a new location on Marshall Street at Syracuse University, potentially in August 2025. This expansion represents continued business growth for the established local brand, which has recently opened locations in Utica and North Carolina.

title: 'Look at what we're living with': Eastwood Heights residents show conditions to the I-Team
summary: Eastwood Heights senior residents are experiencing deteriorating living conditions including water damage, roof leaks, and poor air quality in their Syracuse Housing Authority building. A third iteration of a renovation project has been repeatedly delayed despite promises, with the latest deal pending Common Council vote. Residents express frustration over prolonged suffering and broken commitments from SHA and the city.

title: Syracuse surpasses greenhouse gas reduction goals set in 2010; mayor says
summary: Syracuse has exceeded its 2010 greenhouse gas reduction targets, achieving 67% emissions reductions for city operations (goal: 40%) and 29% for the community (goal: 7%) as documented in the 2024 Greenhouse Gas Emission Inventory report. Mayor Walsh attributes success to LED conversion, fuel-efficient vehicles, and optimized waste collection. The city plans to develop a data-informed Climate Action Plan with public meetings scheduled for Fall 2025 to guide pursuit of net-zero emissions.

title: Syracuse dog shelter location still up in the air more than a year after site search began
summary: The Syracuse Parks and Recreation Department has withdrawn a municipal dog shelter proposal from Common Council consideration after more than a year of planning. Parks Commissioner Syeisha Byrd cited the need for additional time to develop a comprehensive campus plan and explore alternative locations, though the department maintains commitment to the project. The $500,000 planning and design appropriation has been tabled indefinitely with no timeline for reintroduction.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Full STEAM ahead for new regional school's first cohort of students this fall
summary: A new regional STEAM school is opening in downtown Syracuse this fall in the renovated 125-year-old Central Technical High School building. The inaugural cohort of 250 students (80% from Syracuse city) will occupy the first two floors while construction continues, with plans to eventually grow to 1,000 students over four years. City and county leaders view this as a potential model for cross-district educational collaboration and regional student recruitment.

title: Syracuse Police Department's animal control unit warns of increase in virus impacting dogs
summary: The Syracuse Police Department's Animal Control Unit issued a public health alert on July 22, 2025, warning of increased Parvovirus cases among dogs in Syracuse. The advisory provides symptom identification, prevention guidance (vaccination updates, avoiding unvaccinated animals), and directs residents to contact veterinarians for suspected exposures. This represents routine community health communication and potential operational demand on animal control resources.

title: Groups say EPA should use emergency powers to address lead water problem in Syracuse
summary: Advocacy coalition including EarthJustice and NRDC is petitioning the EPA to invoke emergency powers under the Safe Drinking Water Act to force Syracuse to address lead contamination in drinking water. The groups cite questionable sampling procedures from 2023-2024 and question the validity of subsequent independent testing, while the city maintains water meets EPA standards and sampling errors have been corrected.

title: Common Council holds off on decision of adding red light and speed zone cameras
summary: The Syracuse Common Council delayed approval of the Automated School Zone Enforcement Program, which would deploy 34 speed cameras and 36 red light cameras at school zone locations citywide starting Sept. 3, 2025. The third-party vendor Jenoptik will fund all infrastructure costs and recoup investment through fine revenue sharing at $50 per violation. Council cited insufficient information as reason for the postponement.

title: Syracuse Common Council completes swap with SHA, intends to build Children Rising Center
summary: The Syracuse Common Council approved a land swap between the City and Syracuse Housing Authority on August 12, 2025, transferring Eastwood Heights to SHA and acquiring Latimer Terrace for the City. The City intends to develop the Children Rising Center at Latimer Terrace by 2033 as part of a HUD Choice Neighborhoods Implementation grant, addressing delays caused by SHA's failure to secure market tax credits.

title: City of Syracuse seeks to close nuisance convenience store in Outer Comstock neighborhood
summary: The City of Syracuse is pursuing revocation of a special use permit for Market convenience store in Outer Comstock neighborhood, citing illegal cannabis and vape sales, tobacco license violations, and quality-of-life complaints from residents. A public hearing is scheduled for August 27, 2025. The case demonstrates code enforcement action and community concern about nuisance retail operations near schools and parks.

title: James Pass Arboretum to Close Aug. 11–12 for tree work
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Parks & Recreation's Forestry Division will temporarily close James Pass Arboretum on August 11-12, 2025 for tree removal and maintenance work as part of the Pass Arboretum Improvement Plan. Work includes removing invasive, dead, and structurally unsound trees while pruning others to improve health and storm resilience, with the park expected to reopen by 4 p.m. on August 12.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: New York facing $34 billion budget gap in wake of federal spending cuts
summary: New York State faces a $34.3 billion cumulative three-year budget gap, exacerbated by $1.4 trillion in federal spending cuts enacted by President Trump's Big Beautiful Bill. The state comptroller projects $3–3.4 billion in future federal aid losses, with Medicaid and school aid hit hardest. State debt is projected to reach $95.1 billion within five years, approaching the constitutional debt cap and constraining future infrastructure investment.

title: Organizations call for more information about lead levels in Syracuse water
summary: Community organizations including Families for Lead Freedom Now have filed an EPA petition demanding investigation into elevated lead levels detected in Syracuse drinking water during summer 2024. The Chief Policy Officer disputes the findings, attributing prior exceedances to sampling errors and asserting current water meets EPA standards. Community advocates are calling for emergency declaration, at-tap water filters, and public education campaigns.
depts: Office of the Chief Policy Officer

title: City of Syracuse shows renters and homebuyers how to choose wisely
summary: Syracuse's Healthy Housing 101 program, launched in 2018, provides guided educational tours for renters and homebuyers to identify code violations and safety hazards in properties. The annual initiative, led by the Department of Neighborhood and Business Development, demonstrates inspection techniques for detecting lead deterioration, water damage, electrical hazards, and other health/safety risks to support informed housing decisions.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse Police discuss National Night Out and Dome Patrols
summary: The Syracuse Police Department is organizing National Night Out at Kirk Park on Tuesday to build community-police relationships through informal engagement. Concurrently, the department has increased patrol presence inside and outside the Dome for the upcoming football season, with Chief Joe Cecile advising attendees to plan ahead for traffic and arrive early.

title: Box truck strikes Parkway Bridge again
summary: A box truck struck the underside of the Parkway Bridge in Salina on August 1, 2025, closing all traffic lanes between Oswego Street and Park Street. The incident occurred two days after Syracuse Mayor Ben Walsh proposed installing "clanker" devices—audible warning systems positioned before bridges to alert drivers of insufficient clearance—following repeated strikes at the Park Street railroad bridge. No injuries were reported.

title: Syracuse auditor calls for measures to prevent vacant home fires
summary: Syracuse Auditor Alexander Marion has called for preventive measures to address vacant home fires in the city. The article appears to focus on fire safety risks associated with vacant properties, though the provided content is limited to a sarcastic reader comment rather than the full article text.

title: Burned out vacant houses contribute to Syracuse's housing shortage
summary: City Auditor Alexander Marion's report identifies that over one-third of Syracuse's 87 house fires last year occurred in vacant structures, exacerbating the housing shortage. The report recommends proactive interventions including zoning code adjustments (minimum lot sizes), an early warning system for tax delinquency, enhanced property boarding, standardized FEMA-style vacancy marking, and enforcement of the vacant property registry to address the estimated 1,600 vacant houses in the city.

title: Syracuse city auditor releases report aiming to cut down on vacant structure fires
summary: Syracuse City Auditor Alex Marion released a report titled 'Following the Fire' documenting 25 vacant property fires in 2024, averaging 2 fires monthly with $1M+ in damages and $160K in demolition costs. The report recommends preventive solutions including improved boarding methods, better hazard identification, and inter-departmental coordination to reduce future incidents.

title: Syracuse police chief clarifies policy adherence after PBA posts
summary: Syracuse Police Chief Joseph Cecile addressed tensions with the Police Benevolent Association (PBA) President Joe Moran, who posted that officers should disregard de-escalation policies in Armory Square due to crime concerns. Chief Cecile clarified that the PBA President lacks authority to direct policy and emphasized that officers will continue following established procedures. The Chief praised departmental professionalism and noted that Moran has partially walked back his controversial statements.

title: Plan to redevelop former Lafayette Country Club into housing clears first hurdle
summary: The Syracuse Planning Commission approved the master site plan for a 263-unit housing development at the former Lafayette Country Club on August 18, 2025. The project, featuring homes ranging from $350,000 to $1+ million, requires additional approvals including major site review, environmental impact studies, and code enforcement before construction can begin. Developers initially targeted late 2025 for groundbreaking but timeline remains uncertain.

title: Proactive policing and interventions result in lower crime rates in Syracuse
summary: Syracuse Police Department reports significant crime reduction through August 2025: 27% overall crime decrease, 18% violent crime reduction, and 29% property crime decline compared to 2024. Success attributed to proactive policing strategies including community beat walking and the Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence's custom notifications program targeting youth involvement with firearms.
depts: Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence

title: Statistics show drop in crime in Syracuse
summary: Syracuse crime statistics show a 27% overall decrease through the first seven months of 2025, with violent crime down 18% and property crime down 29%. Mayor Walsh and Police Chief Cecile attribute improvements to proactive policing programs including COPRA (community officer foot patrols) and the Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence, with homicides down 33% compared to the national average of 17%.
depts: Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence

title: Overall crime in Syracuse is down so far this year, officials say
summary: Syracuse Police Department reports significant crime reductions through July 2025, with overall crime down 27.3% year-to-date and violent offenses down 18%. Key metrics include homicides down 25%, burglaries down 30%, stolen vehicles down 51%, and gun crimes reduced across multiple categories. Mayor Ben Walsh and Police Chief Joe Cecile attribute these improvements to COMPSTAT data-driven policing strategies.

title: This is how far some 'school zone red light cameras' are from actual schools in Syracuse
summary: CNY Central investigates the placement of 36 school zone red light cameras across Syracuse, revealing that some cameras are positioned up to three-quarters of a mile away from actual schools. City officials justify the distances as preparing drivers entering school zones, with plans for potential expansion. Camera installations will be completed by September 3, with vendor Jenoptik maintaining infrastructure on city poles and right-of-way.

title: Syracuse Common Council honors President Helen Hudson
summary: Syracuse Common Council President Helen Hudson returned from medical leave following cancer treatment and was honored by the council with a unanimous vote to name a street after her. Hudson, the first Black woman to serve as council president, received public recognition from colleagues for her leadership and community contributions.

title: Housing initiative to improve curb appeal for Tipperary Hill and Salt Spring Neighborhoods
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh launched SYRneighborhoods, a housing initiative administered by the Syracuse Housing Strategies Corporation (SHSC) targeting Tipperary Hill and Salt Springs neighborhoods. The Neighborhood Block Challenge Program offers 50% matching grants up to $2,500 per participant for teams of three or more neighbors completing exterior home improvements. Phase two (2026) will provide grants and low-interest financing for major repairs; the CIO should note the initiative's reliance on community organizing and potential for digital tools in grant administration and project tracking.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse mural project aims to enhance safety and community spirit
summary: The City of Syracuse is implementing a mural project on the Southside at Taylor and State Street to enhance community aesthetics and pedestrian safety in advance of the I-81 viaduct removal. The initiative, led by the I-81 Project Director, includes monthly maintenance and serves as a demonstration of how tactical interventions can improve multimodal connectivity and neighborhood livability.
depts: Office of the Chief Innovation Officer

title: City Officials comment on the progress of I-81 Viaduct Project
summary: The I-81 Viaduct Project is advancing on schedule with visible construction progress on bridge replacements. City officials report mixed public feedback: residents appreciate rapid construction updates, but express frustration with traffic impacts and communication gaps. The viaduct removal remains on track for 2027, with project leadership emphasizing improved walkability and bikeability as core objectives.

title: Onondaga County budget proposal has lower taxes, money for law enforcement, child welfare
summary: Onondaga County Executive Ryan McMahon proposed a 2026 budget with an 11% property tax cut for homeowners, representing a 42% total levy reduction since 2018. The $1.6B budget prioritizes law enforcement ($6M helicopter, $700K drones), lead abatement ($1.2M), early childhood education, and a convention/innovation district around the Oncenter, with revenue increasingly dependent on sales taxes (projected 1.5% growth) rather than property taxes.

title: Residents voice concerns over Syracuse Police drone program
summary: Syracuse residents expressed privacy and civil rights concerns at a Common Council public hearing regarding a proposed five-year police drone program with vendor Axon Enterprises. The Syracuse Police Department stated drones would be used for investigations only, not patrol, but community groups argued for Fourth Amendment protections and safeguards prohibiting surveillance of public demonstrations. The program requires Common Council approval and represents an expansion of the city's existing technology partnership with Axon.

title: New Syracuse housing project aims to transform one of the city's poorest neighborhoods
summary: The Southside Renaissance Project, a $41 million mixed-use housing development in Syracuse's Elmwood neighborhood, is advancing with state, local, and federal funding support. Led by Charlene Tarver's coalition and co-developed by Brooklyn-based St. Nicks Alliance, the two-building project will deliver 75+ senior apartments plus ground-floor amenities including a food hall, daycare, workforce lab, rooftop garden, and retail incubator. Groundbreaking expected 2027; completion 2029.

title: Syracuse Common Council approves sale of old fire station for $900K renovation project
summary: Syracuse Common Council approved the sale of a vacant East Fayette Street fire station to JF Real Estate for a $900,000 renovation project. The former station, inactive for over a decade, will be converted into four second-floor apartments and a ground-floor restaurant. This represents mixed-use adaptive reuse of city-owned property aligned with economic development and housing goals.

title: Hundreds of tenants wait, yet again, for a hearing to hold notorious Nob Hill Apartment owners accountable
summary: Over 200 tenants at Nob Hill Apartments in Syracuse are experiencing unsafe living conditions including non-functional elevators, lack of hot water, and unrepaired damage. A city housing court hearing against the apartment owners has been postponed five times, leaving residents in a legal and contractual bind. Mayor Ben Walsh filed a State Supreme Court complaint in April against Nob Hill Apartment Group and its partners; tenants are demanding repairs by October 1 and a rent increase freeze.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Nob Hill tenants demand action amid ongoing legal battle
summary: Nob Hill apartment tenants are escalating demands for action regarding persistent code violations and poor living conditions (lack of heat, maintenance issues, unhooked utilities). A city legal action initiated in April has been postponed five times; tenants are considering a rent strike and organizing collective pressure for the next hearing to proceed without further delays.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Candidates speak on 'slave owner' comment made by fellow candidate, fired city employee
summary: Tim Rudd, a fired City Hall budget director and mayoral candidate, faced public scrutiny over January comments calling Deputy Mayor Sharon Owens a 'slave owner' used to control the city. At a campaign press conference, Rudd declined to address the remarks directly, instead focusing on his nine-point plan. Other candidates and the Democratic Party responded with condemnation; an Independent candidate defended Rudd's underlying critique of Mayor Walsh's governance.

title: New pilot program is helping lower accidents involving deer
summary: Syracuse launched a deer management pilot program in 2025 using traps and wildlife officers to reduce vehicle-deer collisions, property damage, and Lyme disease transmission. The program removed 137 white-tailed deer and generated 12,000+ meals for the food bank through venison donation, demonstrating co-benefits of public safety and food security initiatives.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Syracuse Police reveal 'suspicious package' that evacuated city hall
summary: A suspicious package that prompted evacuation of Syracuse City Hall on September 29, 2025 was identified as a New York State Department of Transportation noise monitoring device (Pelican box with microphone) placed by a contractor on September 25. Mayor Walsh indicated the device placement bypassed normal City-DOT coordination protocols and remains under investigation regarding its tree-mounted placement rather than standard utility pole installation.
depts: New York State Department of Transportation

title: Clear Investment Group pledges to transform troubled Skyline Apartments in Syracuse
summary: Clear Investment Group has committed to remediate the Skyline Apartments (352 units) on James Street by April 30, 2026, following a court-approved settlement with the City of Syracuse. The property, rebranded as 'The Metropolitan,' suffered from serious habitability issues including a 2021 murder, drug activity, flooding, fire, and vermin. Clear inherited the troubled asset from the previous owner Green Skyline Apartments, LLC in 2023 and is now working collaboratively with city officials to restore the building to safe, habitable conditions.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Services
entities: Legal Services of Central New York

title: Syracuse council scrutinizes $900K request for consultant in payroll project
summary: Syracuse Common Council scrutinized a $900,000 request to hire Mosaic Consulting Group (Nashville-based) to implement UKG payroll software, with annual operating costs of ~$750,000. Finance Commissioner acknowledged prior external consultant spending was excessive. Project approval likely, but implementation will occur under next administration as Mayor Walsh's term ends December 2025.

title: Cracks, floor collapse force Syracuse Fire's maintenance to find new temporary home
summary: Syracuse Fire Department's Maintenance Division facility on State Fair Boulevard (built 1969) has structural integrity issues—foundation cracks identified September 2024 and floor collapse in July 2025—forcing relocation of equipment repair operations. Fire Chief Michael Monds reports the department is in crisis-level time pressure to secure a temporary indoor facility within 5 years, currently using Syracuse University's garage bay as emergency overflow. Failure to relocate risks firefighter safety and operational readiness as fleet maintenance cannot proceed reliably.

title: New Syracuse school zone cameras catch nearly 60,000 speeders in just two weeks
summary: Syracuse deployed school zone speed enforcement cameras that issued nearly 60,000 warning letters in two weeks, with actual fines ($50) beginning November 3rd. The initiative detected one driver at 78 mph near Lincoln Middle School and 430 red-light violations city-wide, targeting behavior change in high-violation zones like S. Geddes St. near Fowler High School. Mayor Walsh and police leadership framed the program as child safety-focused rather than punitive.

title: Nearly 60,000 drivers get warnings for speeding in Syracuse school zones
summary: Syracuse launched a red light and speed camera program in school zones that issued nearly 60,000 warnings in the first two weeks of school (Sept 3-17, 2025). Mayor Walsh characterized the high volume as evidence of a 'speeding epidemic' and stated potential fines could exceed $3 million. A grace period ends November 2, after which violations will incur $50-$75 fines.

title: How many traffic violations have been found in Syracuse's School Zone Enforcement program?
summary: Syracuse's School Zone Enforcement Program recorded 60,380 traffic violations (59,950 speeding, 430 red light) during its first two weeks in warning period mode. Mayor Ben Walsh framed the initiative as a public safety priority to address a "speeding epidemic" around schools, with $50-$75 fines to begin after November 2, 2025. The program uses red light cameras and speed enforcement technology to encourage safe driving behavior rather than generate revenue.

title: Syracuse Common Councilors question locations of new school zone cameras
summary: Syracuse Common Council is scrutinizing the placement and enforcement of new camera-monitored school speed zones that launched over a month ago, with 60,000+ warning notices issued. Council members question whether cameras target actual student crossing patterns and are calling for extended warning periods and comprehensive review before fines begin November 3rd at $50 per violation.

title: Rescue Missions hosts groundbreaking ceremony for future facility
summary: Rescue Missions held a groundbreaking ceremony for the Mission Exchange Building, a $11.6M mixed-use facility at West Onondaga and Seymour Street in downtown Syracuse. The 45,000 sq. ft. project, funded through the state's Downtown Revitalization Initiative plus federal and private resources, will include office space, a 35,000 sq. ft. donation processing facility, and retail storefronts, representing a significant downtown revitalization investment.

title: As Mayor Walsh's term ends, the fate of the Columbus statue remains uncertain
summary: As Mayor Ben Walsh's term concludes in January 2026, the Christopher Columbus statue in downtown Syracuse remains in limbo despite a 2020 relocation plan to Heritage Park. Ongoing litigation by the Columbus Monument Corporation has prevented removal despite New York Court of Appeals approval. The incoming mayor will inherit this contentious issue, with candidates holding divergent positions on whether to proceed with relocation, abandon it, or maintain the status quo.

title: The old Syracuse Developmental Center will soon be a residential space, if approved
summary: The City of Syracuse's seized Syracuse Developmental Center property is undergoing demolition and redevelopment by the Albanese Organization into a mixed-income residential community. Phase one includes two multi-family buildings and 27 townhomes with affordable to market-rate pricing, pending Common Council approval of financing and state funding. Construction is targeted to begin in early 2026.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse schools bid farewell to superintendent Anthony Davis amid challenges
summary: Syracuse City Schools Superintendent Anthony Davis is departing at month-end after leading the district since 2022, with Deputy Superintendent Pamela Odom succeeding him. The transition occurs amid district challenges including payroll issues and below-average proficiency scores in ELA and math, with the board considering a $125,000 consultant contract for Davis post-retirement.

title: Supporters not ruling out new legal fight if push to remove Columbus statue continues
summary: The Columbus Monument Corporation is signaling willingness to pursue additional legal action if Syracuse's incoming administration continues efforts to remove and relocate the Christopher Columbus statue to a proposed Heritage Park. Current Mayor Walsh stated the statue will remain through his term; Deputy Mayor Sharon Owens (mayoral candidate) indicated Heritage Park would be a 2026 priority, while other candidates oppose removal. Monument Corp. member Nick Pirro indicated courts ruled their previous lawsuit premature but legal options remain available.

title: Syracuse car thefts drop by 50% in 2025, but university area remains a hot spot
summary: Syracuse achieved a 50% reduction in car thefts in 2025 (500 incidents vs. 1,000+ in 2024), attributed partly to a steering wheel lock initiative with Syracuse University and increased license plate camera surveillance. However, the university area remains a theft hotspot, with students leaving keys in vehicles cited as a primary vulnerability. The Syracuse Police Department recommends standard prevention measures including vehicle locking, secure parking, and key retention.

title: Take a look: Skyline Apartment owners reveal new progress on troubled building
summary: Clear Investment Group is making progress on renovating the troubled Skyline Apartments (to be rebranded as The Metropolitan) on James Street, with modernized elevators, upgraded fire and security systems, and completion of first three floors. The company aims for partial occupancy by December 31, 2025, and full readiness by April 30, 2026. This follows a September 2025 legal settlement with Syracuse after the city threatened action over alleged repair delays on the 352-unit building plagued by flooding, crime, and safety issues.

title: New energy-efficient homes in Syracuse offer opportunity for income-eligible buyers
summary: Syracuse is building four new energy-efficient, affordable homes on Tipperary Hill through a partnership between Home Headquarters, the city's Resurgent Neighborhood Initiative, and New York State's Affordable Homeownership Opportunity Program (AHOP). The homes will be Energy Star-rated and available to income-eligible buyers, with one representing Home Headquarters' 26th build. Mayor Ben Walsh emphasized the collaborative approach involving neighborhood advocacy, private housing partners, and municipal/state support.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development
entities: New York State Affordable Homeownership Opportunity Program

title: Mayor Walsh doles out more than $60K in bonuses in 'challenging fiscal environment'
summary: Mayor Walsh distributed over $60K in merit-based bonuses to 37 city employees in three tranches during 2025, including $2K awards to top administrators like Police Chief Cecile, Finance Commissioner Cannizzaro, and Water Commissioner Brandt. The bonuses, combined with 3% COLA raises and 20-40% pay grade increases, were distributed without Common Council approval. New York State Comptroller opinions cited in the article suggest such bonus payments may violate the state constitution.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Syracuse Common Council approves plans for former Syracuse Developmental Center
summary: The Syracuse Common Council unanimously approved comprehensive plans for redevelopment of the vacant former Syracuse Developmental Center site, including funding, demolition, and phased construction. The Albanese Organization is leading the project and will issue construction bids for infrastructure, utilities, and roadways in November, with demolition targeted for completion by October 31, 2025.

title: Syracuse to launch parking amnesty period in November, late fees to be waived
summary: Syracuse is launching a parking amnesty program from November 1-31, 2025, allowing holders of 140,000 unpaid parking tickets to pay reduced amounts (original ticket plus $15-$30 surcharge instead of accrued late fees). The initiative targets approximately $5.3M in uncollected revenue, with a successful 2020 precedent collecting $960K from 34,000 cleared tickets. A payment assistance program is also available for residents with substantial accumulated debt.

title: Syracuse launches amnesty program for unpaid parking tickets
summary: Syracuse Common Council approved a parking ticket amnesty program for November 2025 that waives late fees on delinquent parking tickets, allowing residents to pay original ticket amounts plus state surcharges. The city expects to recover approximately $1 million in revenue while providing financial relief to residents facing economic hardship. The program expands on a previous 18-day proposal to cover the entire month, with payment available in-person or online.

title: Syracuse school zone traffic warnings drop 41% as enforcement program progresses
summary: Syracuse's School Zone Traffic Enforcement program has achieved a 41% reduction in traffic warnings during its grace period (Sept 16-30), with automated speed and red-light cameras detecting 35,300 violations. Full enforcement with $50 fines begins Nov. 3 after a 60-day grace period. The data indicates drivers are responding to warnings, though continued violations suggest need for sustained awareness campaigns.

title: Syracuse businesses warned of scam with fake zoning invoices requesting wire transfers
summary: Syracuse Police and the Department of Neighborhood & Business Development issued a public alert warning businesses of an active scam involving fraudulent zoning invoices requesting wire transfer payments for approval, submission, environmental impact, and review fees. The city clarified it never requests wire transfers for zoning fees and directed businesses to verify invoices by contacting Code Enforcement at 315-442-8695.

title: ESF to Honor Mayor Ben Walsh and Phil Bousquet with 2025 Sol Feinstone Environmental Award
summary: SUNY College of Environmental Science and Forestry will award the 2025 Sol Feinstone Environmental Award to Syracuse Mayor Ben Walsh and attorney Phil Bousquet for environmental stewardship contributions. Walsh's administration has championed the Sustainable Syracuse Initiative and Climate Action Plan; Bousquet has leveraged New York's Brownfield Cleanup Program to support $9 billion in construction value statewide. The award dinner proceeds support ESF student scholarships.
entities: SUNY College of Environmental Science and Forestry

title: Syracuse parking garage closes due to structural concerns
summary: The parking garage at 300 East Fayette Street in downtown Syracuse has closed due to structural deterioration identified during an engineering inspection. The 41-year-old facility served 100-150 daily parkers including city staff and private monthly users. The city is directing users to the Washington Street parking garage as an alternative while safety concerns are addressed.

title: Syracuse Common Council discusses pilot program for alternatives to youth incarceration
summary: Syracuse Common Council is piloting an alternative-to-incarceration program for youth in partnership with Exalt, a New York-based organization. The program offers resume building, professional development, internships, and employment opportunities as alternatives to incarceration, modeled after an existing NYC initiative. Participants undergo application and acceptance processes before enrollment.

title: The Syracuse City Fire Department’s fleet has a new home for maintenance
summary: Syracuse Common Council approved a $5.7 million capital project to convert a 25,000 sq. ft. portion of the Dunk & Bright furniture showroom into a new fire department maintenance facility. The move addresses critical infrastructure failure at the State Fair Blvd. facility, where structural damage to the floor was declared unsafe for vehicle lifts and heavy equipment in July 2025. The new facility is expected to be operational by late spring 2026.

title: Syracuse partners with exalt to support at-risk youth: What we know now
summary: Syracuse Common Council approved $1.5M in state funding plus cannabis reinvestment dollars to launch exalt, a criminal justice diversion program for court-involved youth ages 15-19. The NYC-based non-profit will provide education support, paid internships ($18-20/hour), and workforce development to help at-risk youth avoid incarceration. Initial rollout targets 10 students with scaling planned as local staffing expands.
entities: New York State Division of Criminal Justice Services

title: Syracuse hosting two I-81 open houses this week
summary: The City of Syracuse is hosting two public open houses (November 12-13, 2025) as part of the Reconnecting Communities Planning Project to gather community feedback on draft street designs for five southside corridors and a proposed Linear Park following the I-81 Viaduct removal. This represents active community engagement on a major infrastructure and neighborhood reconnection initiative.

title: Second phase of East Adams neighborhood transformation project begins
summary: The Syracuse Housing Authority and McCormack Baron Salazar have begun Phase 2 of the East Adams neighborhood transformation project, focusing on brownfield remediation at Burt and State Streets. The site contains petroleum-impacted soils and elevated lead/PAH levels requiring soil excavation, vapor barrier installation, and environmental monitoring through winter, with vertical construction for 125 affordable senior housing units expected to begin in early 2026 under the Choice Neighborhoods Initiative.

title: Syracuse approves funding for youth alternative to incarceration program
summary: The Syracuse Common Council approved funding for the Exalt Youth program, a criminal justice alternative that provides internships and professional opportunities for youth involved in the justice system. The pilot program, modeled on a NYC initiative, is supported by a New York State Division of Criminal Justice Services grant and has generated positive sentiment from city leadership including Common Councilor Chol Majok.
entities: New York State Division of Criminal Justice Services

title: Vacant City Hall Commons in Downtown Syracuse could be converted to housing
summary: Holmes King Kallquist & Associates, a Syracuse architecture firm, proposes converting the vacant seven-story City Hall Commons building (built 1869) in Hanover Square into 39 mixed-use units featuring a restaurant anchor tenant and residential space. The Syracuse Landmark Preservation Board responded positively to the proposal on November 20, 2025, with further discussions planned. This project addresses downtown vacant property reactivation and historic preservation priorities.
depts: Department of Landmarks and Historic Preservation

title: Syracuse snow plow operators unlicensed, companies begin filing paperwork
summary: Syracuse's private snow plow operators currently lack required licensing despite city regulations mandating them. Five companies have begun the licensing application process but none have completed submissions. City Auditor Alexander Marion emphasized the importance of licenses for equipment verification and driver training, while City Hall clarified that contracted sidewalk snow removal vendors operate under different authority and are exempt from annual licensing requirements.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Community Services

title: Syracuse police oversight board regains momentum under new leadership
summary: Syracuse's Citizen Review Board has regained operational momentum under new administrator Nolita Tanyhill (hired May 2025) and chair Don Johnson, working through backlogged complaints and addressing cases that exceeded the 18-month statute of limitations. The board is expanding community engagement on police interaction protocols while Council public safety chair Chol Majok signals openness to legislative updates, though historically council and police have resisted expanding CRB investigative authority.

title: Mayor Walsh and Mayor-elect Owens reflect on significance of East Adams after years of planning
summary: Mayor Walsh and Mayor-elect Owens announced the groundbreaking for Phase One of the East Adams housing redevelopment project, a 133-unit affordable housing complex expected to open in 2027. The project represents a multi-year commitment to equitable neighborhood transformation, addressing public housing community needs while coordinating with adjacent developments including school district infrastructure, I-81 viaduct removal, and the new regional STEAM school.

title: Inspired by Delaware, Syracuse to install 'clanker' system in 2026 to stop bridge strikes
summary: Syracuse will install a 'clanker' safety system on the CSX railroad bridge along Park Street in 2026, modeled after Delaware's successful implementation in Newark. The system uses a giant arm with hanging buoys to physically warn oversized trucks before they strike the bridge, addressing 42 documented strikes since 2020. This infrastructure safety initiative reflects the City's proactive approach to accident prevention and public safety.
depts: Office of the Chief Innovation Officer

title: Freedom of Espresso celebrating 30 years of coffee in Syracuse
summary: Freedom of Espresso, a local coffee business with 30 years of operations in Syracuse, is being honored with a proclamation from Mayor Ben Walsh on December 5, 2025. The business has expanded to multiple locations across Central New York and represents a successful local enterprise in the community.

title: Headed out in Syracuse? Don't forget the city's Snow Operations Map
summary: The City of Syracuse operates a real-time Snow Operations Map that tracks plow locations and illegal parking during winter weather events. This digital service tool helps residents and visitors determine road conditions and accessibility during significant snowfall, supporting safe travel planning across the city.
entities: City of Syracuse Snow Operations Map

title: Syracuse, Ukraine Sister Cities mark 2 years with youth art exchange
summary: Syracuse and sister city Irpin, Ukraine marked their two-year partnership anniversary with 'Bridges of Hope,' a youth photography exchange involving 7-11 year olds from both cities. The exhibit opened simultaneously in Syracuse City Hall Commons and Irpin, featuring images captured by children documenting everyday life in their communities. Mayor Walsh framed the initiative as honoring resilience and unity, with Syracuse's Parks & Recreation and Youth Programs collaborating with the Onondaga Historical Association.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: 'Paused in progress': Mayor Walsh passes the torch to Sharon Owens at his final briefing
summary: Outgoing Mayor Ben Walsh held his final community briefing, highlighting achievements including exceeding housing development goals (3,151 units vs. 2,500 target), expanding online city services for permits and parking amnesty, and passing leadership to incoming Mayor Sharon Owens. The briefing emphasized continuity in vision while acknowledging the I-81 project and revitalization efforts as priorities for the incoming administration.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse crime is down overall, but sexual violence in one area drives citywide increase
summary: Syracuse violent crime decreased 19% citywide in 2025, with homicides down 41%, continuing a downward trend since 2020. However, sexual violence in the Southeast District (Valley, University Hill, Meadowbrook) increased 21.1% year-over-year and has risen 38.6% over five years, driving a 1% net increase in citywide rape cases. Outgoing Mayor Walsh credited state and federal resources plus police reform investments; incoming Mayor Owens is expected to maintain this public safety strategy.

title: Walsh credits recent initiatives for a drop in crime in Syracuse
summary: Mayor Walsh reports a 23.7% overall crime reduction in Syracuse year-over-year, with violent crime down 19% and property crime down 24.6%. He attributes success to the Syracuse Police Department and eight years of city initiatives including the Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence, Safer Streets program, and Police/Athletic Activities League. The announcement comes as Walsh prepares to leave office, with Mayor-Elect Owens expected to continue these efforts.
entities: Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence

title: Syracuse's plow operations more prepared to tackle snow events
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh reports Syracuse's snowplow operations are better equipped than in years past to handle winter weather events. The city has doubled its fleet from 12 to 20 plows since 2018 and recently hired additional drivers undergoing intensive on-the-job training. The article highlights ongoing recruitment for drivers and mechanics as the city addresses capacity challenges and workforce experience gaps.

title: Shootings with injuries down nearly 15% so far this year in Syracuse, police data shows
summary: Syracuse Police data shows shootings with injuries down 13% year-to-date (47 to 41 incidents), with shooting victims down 13% (64 to 56), reflecting success of Governor Hochul's state GIVE initiative across 28 participating communities. The statewide program, funded at $36.38 million for non-NYC agencies, continues through June 2026 using evidence-based gun violence reduction strategies. Albany, Buffalo, and Syracuse are among communities reporting double-digit declines in gun-related violence.

title: Syracuse issues 21,000 tickets for School Zone Traffic Enforcement violations in November
summary: Syracuse launched its School Zone Traffic Enforcement Program in November 2025, issuing 21,000 citations through automated cameras deployed around schools. The program captured speeding violations (90% of tickets), red light violations (10%), and school bus stop arm violations (645 tickets), with the city reporting a 60% decrease in speeding violations from October despite fewer cameras and school days in operation.

title: Streets, sidewalks, garbage, plus hiring and housing programs define Mayor Walsh's tenure
summary: Mayor Walsh reflects on eight years in office, highlighting infrastructure improvements (100+ miles of roads paved, 47 miles of sidewalks reconstructed), workforce development and hiring programs targeting marginalized populations, and housing initiatives. Deputy Mayor Sharon Owens will assume the mayoralty in January, with Walsh emphasizing continuity as the city prepares for I-81 removal and Micron development.

title: SPD recaps end-of-year crime stats
summary: Syracuse Police Department reported significant year-end crime reductions: violent crime down 18%, property crime down 24%, and shootings with injuries/fatalities decreased from 165 in 2020 to 56 in 2025. Chief Joe Cecile attributed success to proactive policing and outreach efforts while cautioning that challenges remain. The department uses COMPSTAT reporting for performance tracking and public transparency.

title: Syracuse officials: Clear your fire hydrants, move vehicles off roads amid heavy snow
summary: Syracuse city officials issued public guidance during a major December 2025 snowstorm, directing residents to clear fire hydrants (3-5 foot radius), remove vehicles from roads during overnight parking restrictions, and fully clear snow from vehicles. Fire Chief Monds and incoming Police Chief Rusin emphasized compliance to support emergency response, while Mayor Walsh highlighted the city's deployment of 30 vehicles and equipment with 12-hour shifts, characterizing this as the largest storm in years.

title: Syracuse residents share their thoughts on the snow; one called it a 'nightmare'
summary: Syracuse experienced significant lake-effect snowfall on December 30, 2025, with Onondaga County towns receiving a foot or more of snow. The article captures resident reactions ranging from negative (new international student calling it a 'nightmare') to pragmatic (local families managing shoveling and winter routines). Mayor Ben Walsh advised residents to stay home if unnecessary, and city officials reminded residents to clear snow around fire hydrants and vents.

title: Walsh ready to hand Syracuse city government to Sharon Owens
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh concludes his eight-year tenure as Syracuse's 54th mayor on December 31, 2025, handing leadership to Deputy Mayor Sharon Owens, who becomes the city's first Black mayor. Walsh highlights accomplishments in infrastructure (100+ miles of roads paved), housing, the I-81 community grid project, and basic city services, while transitioning to lead the New York State Canal Corporation.

title: State senator, city auditor: NY aid to cities isn’t keeping up (Your Letters)
summary: State Senator Christopher J. Ryan and City Auditor Alexander Marion advocate for a state commission to redesign New York's municipal aid formula (AIM), which has been frozen since 2012. Syracuse relies heavily on state aid as its second-largest revenue source, but cost inflation in salaries, benefits, and infrastructure has outpaced frozen funding, creating fiscal sustainability concerns across upstate cities.

title: Who’s on the ballots for Syracuse Common Council, school board races in 2025?
summary: Syracuse will hold Democratic primaries on June 24, 2025 for Common Council (councilor-at-large and council president races) and school board (education commissioner seats), with general elections on November 4. Key leadership transitions include term-limited Council President Helen Hudson and District 2 incumbent Pat Hogan running for mayor. Candidates can also pursue independent ballot lines beginning April 15.

title: State senator, city auditor: NY aid to cities isn’t keeping up (Your Letters)
summary: State Senator Christopher J. Ryan and City Auditor Alexander Marion advocate for redesigning New York's AIM (Aid and Incentives for Municipalities) formula, which has been frozen since 2012. They argue that Syracuse's reliance on state aid as its second-largest revenue source necessitates updated funding mechanisms to address rising operational costs, climate adaptation, cybersecurity, and tax-exempt property growth. A proposed state Senate commission would lead the redesign effort.

title: Who’s on the ballots for Syracuse Common Council, school board races in 2025?
summary: Syracuse is holding 2025 elections for Common Council (5 district seats, 2 at-large seats, and council president) and school board (3 education commissioner seats). Democratic primaries are scheduled for June 24 with general election on November 4. Key races include a contested at-large primary and an open District 2 seat due to incumbent Pat Hogan's mayoral run. Council president pays $39,140; voting councilors earn $36,050; education commissioners earn $16,000 annually.

title: Syracuse Mayor Walsh seeks 3rd straight tax hike: ‘It’s the responsible thing to do’
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh proposes a 2% property tax increase for the 2025-26 budget cycle, marking the third consecutive year of such increases. The increase is driven by rising retirement and health insurance costs, declining sales tax revenue, and reduced investment income, necessitating a $27.2 million draw from the city's $120 million fund balance. The $348.4 million budget proposal goes to the Common Council on April 9, 2025, for approval by May 8.

title: Syracuse Police Chief Joe Cecile will retire when Walsh’s term ends this year
summary: Syracuse Police Chief Joe Cecile will retire on December 31, 2025, when Mayor Ben Walsh's term ends. Cecile, who has served 40+ years in the SPD and became chief in 2022, publicly recommends that his successor be selected from within the department's ranks, signaling a leadership transition decision point for the incoming mayor.

title: Syracuse lawmaker Majok pulls proposal for panel to review police watchdog law
summary: Syracuse Common Councilor Chol Majok withdrew his proposal to establish a Citizen Review Board Legislative Update Committee, delaying efforts to modernize the police watchdog law (unchanged since 2011). The withdrawal followed pushback from council colleagues and the former CRB chair about timing and board independence. Majok plans to reintroduce the proposal after current board vacancies are filled and a full-time administrator is hired, hoped for this summer.

title: Syracuse computing its greenhouse gas output and forming climate action plan
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced the Sustainable Syracuse program, a state-funded initiative ($100,000 grant from NY Department of Environmental Conservation) to develop a municipal and community Greenhouse Gas Emission Inventory and Climate Action Plan. The city has contracted C&S Cos. to lead the planning effort, with community engagement beginning fall 2025, and a newly established sustainability task force will coordinate with the city's concurrent comprehensive plan revision.
entities: SUNY College of Environmental Science and Forestry

title: Syracuse brings back police officers vs. firefighters basketball game played for charity
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced the return of the 'Bravest vs. Finest' charity basketball game between Syracuse Fire Department and Syracuse Police Department, scheduled for May 23, 2025 at Corcoran High School. The event will raise funds for United Way of Central New York and marks the first public iteration of the traditionally closed game since 2019, with ticket sales at $10 per person and ancillary merchandise sales.
entities: United Way of Central New York

title: Years later, jury to decide whether Syracuse officer was right or wrong to fatally shoot man at Father’s Day party
summary: A federal civil trial began regarding a 2016 fatal shooting by Syracuse Police Officer Kelsey Francemone at a Father's Day barbecue. The case centers on disputed facts: whether victim Gary Porter was armed and firing, whether the officer properly identified herself, and the significance of gunshot residue evidence. A proposed $825,000 settlement failed due to insufficient City Council votes, forcing the case to jury trial after a grand jury had previously cleared the officer of criminal charges.

title: Mayor Walsh vetoes most of Syracuse council’s budget cuts, including police, fire
summary: Mayor Walsh issued formal vetoes on May 19, 2025, restoring $13 million of $16 million in budget cuts approved by Syracuse Common Council, including full restoration of $3.5M police and $2.4M fire cuts. Walsh accepted council's elimination of a 2% property tax rate increase but vetoed most other departmental cuts and a water rate reduction, setting up a likely veto override vote on May 27. This fiscal conflict reveals tensions over sustainable spending levels and reserve fund usage ($27M proposed vs. $14.4M after council cuts).
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Syracuse lawmakers end tumultuous budget process, overriding all of mayor’s vetoes
summary: Syracuse Common Council unanimously overrode Mayor Ben Walsh's 45 budget vetoes on May 27, 2025, adopting a $348.4 million budget with $16 million in cuts and a flat property tax rate. The council rejected Walsh's proposed 2% tax increase and $27.1 million reserve draw, instead using $14.4 million from reserves. This represents the most divisive budget process of Walsh's tenure, with both sides accusing each other of fiscal irresponsibility and misrepresenting the city's financial position regarding COVID relief fund composition and future sustainability.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Fence doesn’t solve problems of homeless population; it relocates them (Your Letters)
summary: Opinion letter critiques the city's fence installation at Dickerson Street near the Rescue Mission as a temporary containment measure that displaces rather than solves homelessness, addiction, and mental illness. Author, a West Onondaga Street business owner and community activist, advocates for decentralized, smaller-scale social services distributed across all neighborhoods rather than concentrated facilities that burden specific areas.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Community Services

title: Pat Hogan: ‘I’m not going anywhere’ despite overwhelming Syracuse mayoral primary loss
summary: Pat Hogan, a seven-term Syracuse Common Councilor, lost the Democratic mayoral primary on June 27, 2025, receiving 23% of the vote compared to winner Sharon Owens's 62%. Despite the decisive loss—worse than his 2013 primary defeat—Hogan stated he is remaining active in city politics and will continue advocating for community needs.

title: Syracuse, other Upstate NY cities on Trump administration list of ‘sanctuary jurisdictions’
summary: Syracuse has been designated by the Trump administration's Department of Homeland Security as a 'sanctuary jurisdiction' for allegedly obstructing federal immigration law enforcement. The city joins over 500 jurisdictions nationally and 12 in New York State on this list, with potential consequences including loss of federal funding. Current Mayor Ben Walsh maintains Syracuse's welcoming stance toward immigrants while the Police Department does not enforce federal immigration law.

title: Syracuse officials challenge police union’s attack on Armory Square’s safety
summary: Syracuse city officials and Police Chief Joseph Cecile publicly disputed a Syracuse Police Benevolent Association Facebook post characterizing Armory Square as 'chaotic' and 'infiltrated' by gang members. The officials clarified that increased police presence was a planned deployment to address illegal after-hours street drinking and unsanctioned block parties, not an emergency response to violent crime. Of 50 arrests over the weekend, 36 were violation tickets; only 6 were felonies (mostly gun possession) with no violent crime arrests.

title: See Syracuse’s 25 most dangerous intersections (and how the city plans to make them safer)
summary: Mayor Walsh seeks Common Council approval of Syracuse's Vision Zero Action Plan, targeting 25 intersections and 25 miles of roadway where 50% of serious injury/fatal crashes occurred (2019-2023). The plan proposes infrastructure improvements (crosswalk visibility, lane reductions, lighting, speed cameras) to achieve zero fatalities by 2055 with 35% reduction by 2035. Public Works Committee meets July 28 for vote.

title: Neighborhood pushback leads Syracuse, county to pause drop-in center for homeless
summary: Syracuse and Onondaga County have paused plans for a daytime drop-in warming/cooling center for homeless individuals on West Onondaga Street following neighborhood opposition. Business owners and residents launched a petition citing concerns about concentrated social services in an already-stressed corridor, prompting city officials to request the county halt the project. The $30–50 person facility, funded by opioid settlement money, was originally scheduled to open in August but now lacks a timeline; city leadership emphasizes the need for community engagement in future site selection.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse reports ‘significant’ greenhouse gas reductions over past 15 years
summary: Syracuse released a 2024 Greenhouse Gas Emission Inventory showing municipal operations achieved 67% emissions reductions over 14 years. The state-funded study by C&S Cos. will inform development of a Climate Action Plan targeting net-zero emissions, indicating the city is advancing sustainability goals with measurable progress data.

title: Syracuse lawmakers consider making it illegal to block a bicycle lane
summary: Syracuse Common Councilor Corey Williams has introduced legislation to establish "Blocking a Bicycle Lane" as a standalone traffic code violation, partnering with the city auditor to enhance bicycle safety enforcement. This policy change represents a Vision Zero initiative aimed at protecting vulnerable road users through targeted code enforcement.

title: Syracuse police chief hopes union leader ‘cools off’ after ‘dangerous’ online comments
summary: Syracuse Police Chief Joseph Cecile has publicly criticized the head of the police union for a Facebook post he characterizes as 'dangerous and reckless,' emphasizing that words matter in leadership. The incident reflects internal tensions within the Syracuse Police Department regarding union leadership communication.

title: Syracuse must do more for families drinking from lead pipes (Guest Opinion by Oceanna Fair & Nayyirah Shariff)
summary: Community leaders Oceanna Fair and Nayyirah Shariff call for urgent action on Syracuse's lead water contamination crisis affecting 14,000+ families. Testing showed lead levels 7x the EPA action level; the city disputes results but experts confirm danger. The authors demand pipe replacement, transparency, community involvement in EPA investigation, and accountability from officials.

title: Bricks are falling from a smokestack at historic former Syracuse train station. City orders it razed
summary: The City of Syracuse has ordered demolition of a 100-foot smokestack at the historic former New York Central Railroad building after bricks began falling onto public areas since April. The decision follows a dispute between the city's Code Enforcement division and property owner Tom Hornstein over responsibility for repairs; Hornstein attributes deterioration to nearby DOT construction vibrations and estimates demolition costs at $60,000, while the DOT and city attribute the damage to inadequate maintenance.
depts: New York State Department of Transportation

title: Residents in 2 Syracuse neighborhoods can now get $2,500 to improve ‘curb appeal’ of their homes
summary: Syracuse launched the Neighborhood Block Challenge, a matching grant program offering up to $2,500 per household for curb-appeal home improvements in Salt Springs and Tipp Hill-Far West Side neighborhoods. The program requires teams of at least 3 neighbors to apply collectively, with ~$70,000 budgeted. It operationalizes the city's 2024 housing strategy to stimulate middle-income neighborhood investment and private reinvestment.

title: Watertown seeks new city attorney after Syracuse law firm steps away
summary: Watertown (NY) is replacing its city attorney after Syracuse-based law firm Bond, Schoeneck & King withdrew from the role following criticism of attorney Kristen Smith's handling of ethics complaints and a state police investigation into a $3.34M golf course purchase. The city plans to issue an RFP within 30 days and appoint a new firm by end of 2026, preferring a local law firm for the general counsel position while BSK continues litigation and specialty work.

title: Blueprint 15 names Syracuse city official its interim executive director
summary: Blueprint 15, a key city partner organization, has appointed Sarah Walton LaFave, the city's director of East Adams Neighborhood Redevelopment, as interim executive director. LaFave replaces Raquan Pride-Green who stepped down to focus on his small business. This leadership transition may impact coordination between the city and Blueprint 15 on neighborhood development initiatives.

title: Syracuse’s STEAM school opens with welcomes and field trips on historic first day: ‘You all are pioneers’
summary: Syracuse opened STEAM High School on September 3, 2025, a regional magnet school in a renovated 122-year-old downtown building that represents a seven-year effort by local and state leadership. The inaugural class of 200+ ninth graders can specialize in nine STEAM-focused areas including data analytics, robotics, semiconductor manufacturing, and performing arts, with the school expected to grow to ~1,000 students. The $100M project received $71M in state investment and support from Micron Technology, positioning Syracuse to align education with regional workforce demands.

title: Syracuse killed 137 deer in neighborhoods last year, second most since 2019
summary: Syracuse culled 137 deer in neighborhoods during January-March 2024, the second-highest total since the program began in 2019. The city's tick and deer management initiative, which donates all venison to the Food Bank of Central New York (16,500 pounds/60,000 meals to date), is expanding due to new state law allowing sharpshooters to operate closer to residences (250 feet vs. 500 feet setback). A Common Council update on results and 2025-26 plans is scheduled for Monday with public comment opportunity.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs
entities: Food Bank of Central New York

title: From milking cows to making millions: How Syracuse’s property brothers built a real estate empire
summary: Syracuse-based IronHorn Enterprises, founded by brothers Colin and Greg Cleghorn, has built a $275M real estate portfolio focused on industrial properties and e-commerce warehousing across 17 states. The company's growth demonstrates regional entrepreneurial success and has generated local employment; recent acquisitions include the former Rite Aid distribution center (Clay) and warehouse leases to regional businesses like Beak & Skiff Apple Orchards.

title: Syracuse Inner Harbor is no place for a concrete plant (Editorial Board Opinion)
summary: Editorial board opposes Heidelberg Materials' plan to restart a concrete batch plant at 411-421 Spencer Street, two blocks from the Syracuse Inner Harbor and the $100M aquarium under construction. The board argues the 24/7 operation is incompatible with waterfront redevelopment goals and recommends the Common Council deny the special permit application. County Executive Ryan McMahon has offered to help relocate the facility.

title: Syracuse hires budget director who is expected to stay in role for Mayor-elect Owens
summary: Mayor Walsh appointed Evan Loving as Director of the Office of Management and Budget in coordination with Mayor-elect Sharon Owens, who will assume office in January 2026. Loving, a returning city employee with prior experience as special assistant to Walsh and subsequent roles in Virginia local government and consulting, will lead budget development, capital planning, purchasing, and equity compliance. The position has been vacant since February 2025 when former director Tim Rudd was terminated amid controversy.

title: Syracuse auditor: City’s ‘lifeless’ wage ordinance needs a serious overhaul
summary: City Auditor Alexander Marion released a scathing audit of Syracuse's 20-year-old living wage ordinance, finding it has become functionally ineffective due to numerous loopholes, undefined exemptions, and complete lack of enforcement mechanisms. The audit recommends a comprehensive overhaul including elimination of exemptions, assignment of enforcement responsibility to the office of management and budget, and adoption of a more robust living wage formula. This finding is critical for CIO consideration given implications for procurement policy, contractor compliance tracking systems, and data governance around wage ordinance enforcement.

title: Syracuse sues company that was supposed to save Skyline Apartments, says they ‘failed to invest’
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh's administration filed a lawsuit against Chicago-based Clear Investment Group for failing to rehabilitate the Skyline Apartments and three other distressed properties acquired in 2023. The company had been welcomed to Syracuse with promises to revive the notorious Skyline Apartments but allegedly neglected the properties instead.

title: Owners of former Skyline Apartments in Syracuse hope to talk their way out of city’s lawsuit
summary: The city of Syracuse has filed a lawsuit seeking a court-appointed receiver and $2 million in repairs for the former Skyline Apartments (now The Metropolitan), a 352-unit complex owned by Chicago-based Clear Investment Group. Despite Clear's claims of progress and willingness to negotiate, the city's code enforcement director states no substantial work has been completed, and disputes over electricity restoration and repair verification have stalled the project amid a housing crisis.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: 15th Ward should be in the conversation about Public Safety Building redo (Your Letters)
summary: A letter to the editor argues that the Syracuse Public Safety Building redevelopment should include affordable housing and community reestablishment discussions similar to I-81 corridor planning, noting the site's historical significance as former 15th Ward land destroyed during Urban Renewal. The writer questions why historic preservation focus on the building's mid-century modern architecture overlooks the neighborhood's erased history.

title: 60,000 drivers sped through Syracuse’s school zones last month
summary: Syracuse deployed automated speed and red-light cameras in school zones starting Sept. 3, 2025, issuing 60,000 speeding warnings during a grace period (through Nov. 2). After the grace period expires, violations will result in $50 fines for speeding and $250 fines for passing stopped school buses.
