The program has identified five high-violation school zone locations and represents a significant public safety technology implementation requiring IT infrastructure coordination and potential revenue generation.

title: 75 apartments proposed for historic Syracuse building near I-81. See renderings
summary: Developer Grazi Zazzara Jr. proposes Icon Lofts, a 75-unit residential project in a historic 1893 Syracuse Lighting Co. building at 311 Genant Drive near I-81. The $22.5M project includes market-rate and affordable units, parking, and commercial space, with tax incentives from SIDA and potential O-CHIP housing grant. The project aligns with the state's I-81 reconstruction initiative and represents adaptive reuse of a landmark structure.

title: A fence? Or a cage? Syracuse pens off street to contain trouble near homeless shelter
summary: Syracuse installed chain-link fencing on Dickerson Street to restrict access between downtown and the Rescue Mission homeless shelter, citing ongoing issues including drug sales, fights, overdoses, and predatory behavior. The move was implemented without advance notice to the shelter or its clients, raising questions about containment versus public safety approaches to managing homelessness and street-level disorder.

title: A lame-duck Syracuse councilor’s proposal angers historic preservation advocates
summary: Common Councilor Pat Hogan has proposed a zoning law amendment that would allow property owners of locally protected historic buildings to bypass Landmark Preservation Board approval for exterior repairs, with exemptions granted directly by city council. The proposal has drawn strong opposition from historic preservation advocates and the LPB chair, who argue it would effectively eliminate the board's authority. The Planning Commission will review the proposal and provide a recommendation within 60 days, potentially after Hogan leaves office at year-end.

title: Ben Walsh sweated Syracuse’s small stuff. He leaves behind a trail of street-level improvements
summary: Outgoing Mayor Ben Walsh's eight-year tenure focused on street-level quality-of-life improvements including 47 miles of sidewalk replacement, new trash/recycling systems, permitting process optimization, and aggressive code enforcement (42 convenience store closures). While delivering tangible infrastructure and operational wins, Walsh faced criticism for hesitancy on major public housing issues, three property tax increases that drew council resistance, and a significant IT payroll modernization failure ($90,000 wasted). His successor Sharon Owens inherits unfinished payroll system work and pending litigation from store closures.

title: Biggest Syracuse housing development in decades needs a new partner after land owner pulls out
summary: A major housing development (LaFayette Hills Estates) planned for a 168-acre former golf course site in Syracuse to support Micron Technology's incoming workforce faces delays after the land owner (B3R Investments) withdrew from the development agreement to pursue a land flip. Developer Kassie Smith is seeking a new investment partner to keep the project on track for spring 2026 construction. This represents a significant economic development and housing supply challenge as Micron begins site work on its semiconductor plant.

title: Calvin Corriders steps down as president of Syracuse Housing Authority board
summary: Calvin Corriders resigned as president of the Syracuse Housing Authority board after two years, stepping down one year before his term ended to focus on other volunteer roles and family. His departure comes amid ongoing leadership scrutiny and delays to the $1 billion East Adams neighborhood redevelopment project, which faced criticism over a controversial SUNY Upstate optometry school proposal and the withdrawal of a $31.5 million Blueprint 15 community project.

title: City audit finds major flaws in Syracuse’s sidewalk dining permits, recommends new rules and fees
summary: City Auditor Alex Marion's audit of Syracuse's sidewalk dining permit program reveals systemic failures: 25+ businesses operating without permits, lax enforcement across departments, and only $4,000 collected annually against enforcement costs. Recommendations include centralized permitting, year-round permits, modernized fee structures, and mandatory inspections to generate revenue and ensure public safety/accessibility compliance.

title: City council clears the way for major housing development near Syracuse zoo
summary: Syracuse Common Council unanimously approved a $134M mixed-income housing development on 23 acres near the Rosamond Gifford Zoo. The Albanese Organization will develop 260 affordable apartments (50-80% AMI) with Home HeadQuarters building 27 townhomes, with future phases including 100 market-rate and 100 senior affordable units. The city is providing $5.2M in land sale proceeds, $11M in low-interest loans, and $18.8M in bonding for infrastructure, with state tax credits and financing expected to close in December 2025.

title: City Hall: Syracuse community comes together to reduce crime -- and it’s working (Guest Opinion)
summary: Syracuse leadership reports significant crime reductions in 2025, with overall crime down 27.3% YTD and violent crime down 18% compared to prior year; shootings down 50% vs. five-year average. Success attributed to multi-agency coordination including SPD operational changes (KOPER Curve, revised patrol schedules), CPTED environmental design investments, diversionary response programs, and community partnerships with county/state law enforcement and the Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence.
depts: Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence
entities: New York State Division of Criminal Justice Services

title: Company news: Deka Eysaman to lead South Side Community Growth Foundation
summary: Deka Eysaman has been appointed inaugural executive director of the South Side Community Growth Foundation, a nonprofit focused on affordable housing and economic mobility in Syracuse's South Side. Eysaman brings extensive experience in community organizing, equity initiatives, and workforce development, including leadership of the successful I-81 Local Hire campaign that secured 15% local hiring commitments.
entities: Urban Jobs Task Force of Syracuse

title: Concrete plant proposal near $100 million Syracuse aquarium draws McMahon’s ire
summary: Heidelberg Materials has applied to reopen a dormant concrete batch plant at 411-421 Spencer Street, two blocks from Syracuse's $100M Inner Harbor aquarium project. County Executive Ryan McMahon opposes the proposal, arguing it contradicts lakefront redevelopment goals, though the city—not the county—holds permitting authority. Developer Len Montreal and Heidelberg have issued competing statements on community impact and economic benefits.

title: Crime is dropping fast in Syracuse. Police chief says it’s a snowball effect
summary: Syracuse crime has declined 27% year-to-date (through Aug. 11, 2025), with violent crime down 18% and property crime down 29%—reversing a three-year property crime increase trend. Police Chief Joe Cecile attributes gains to the Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence (launched 2022), which deploys ~25 'credible messengers' for community violence intervention, combined with proactive policing strategies. Gun violence data shows 44 people injured/killed through Aug. 11 versus a five-year average of 88, indicating sustained momentum in a key public-safety metric.
depts: Mayor's Office to Reduce Gun Violence

title: Days after budget fight, Moody’s says Syracuse’s creditworthiness remains strong, stable
summary: Moody's Ratings affirmed Syracuse's A1 bond rating with stable outlook, validating the city's strong financial position despite recent contentious budget cuts. The rating agency cited 33% fund balance reserves and economic growth prospects, supporting both Mayor Walsh's fiscal management claims and Common Council's rationale for reducing spending and limiting reserve drawdowns to protect long-term solvency.

title: Developer shrinks Syracuse apartment project near I-81 for second time
summary: Developer Christopher Geiger has reduced the Gateway Apartments project near I-81 for the second time, cutting units from 291 to 259 (11% reduction) after learning the eastern portion of the site would not qualify for state brownfield cleanup tax credits. The seven-story project at East Fayette and Almond streets remains a priority for the Almond Street corridor post-I-81 demolition, but financing and investor commitments are still pending with no construction timeline set.

title: Did Syracuse get all the IT work it paid millions for? Outside report calls for tighter oversight
summary: An $88,000 audit by Bonadio Group found that Syracuse's $3M IT modernization project with EY lacked adequate oversight, with city officials uncertain whether all billed work was completed. The report found no fraud in the controversial no-bid contract but recommended establishing a Chief Technology Officer position and centralizing software procurement to prevent future cost overruns and project failures.

title: Did Syracuse get all the IT work it paid millions for? Outside report calls for tighter oversight
summary: An $88,000 audit by Bonadio Group found no fraud in Syracuse's $3M EY payroll modernization contract but raised concerns that EY may not have completed all billed work. The report identified systemic IT governance weaknesses and recommended establishing a Chief Technology Officer position to improve oversight of decentralized software acquisition and project management.

title: Fines for speeding in Syracuse school zones start next week, but not at every site
summary: Syracuse school zone speed and red-light camera enforcement begins November 3, 2025, after a two-month grace period with warning tickets. Of 28 camera locations installed, 15 are active for enforcement while 10 will be relocated and 3 require school zone boundary adjustments. The program flagged over 95,300 violations in September alone, though the violation rate declined as warning letters were distributed, suggesting behavioral impact.

title: Fire damages vacant apartment building on Syracuse’s South Side
summary: A vacant apartment building at 105 Latimer Terrace on Syracuse's South Side sustained fire damage on October 16, 2025. The fire, which started in a second-floor bathroom, was extinguished in 40 minutes with no injuries reported. This incident exemplifies a recurring city problem: nearly 30% of Syracuse's 87 structure fires in 2024 occurred in vacant properties, with 1,490 vacant homes and 163 vacant commercial buildings currently in the city creating public safety and blight challenges.

title: Former Sears store on Syracuse’s South Side to be turned into semiconductor workforce training center
summary: New York State is investing $8.5 million to transform the vacant 1929 art deco Sears building at 1300 S. Salina Street into the ON-RAMP Workforce Innovation Hub, a semiconductor and advanced manufacturing training center anchored by Micron Technology's $100 billion regional commitment. The hub, part of a $200 million statewide initiative, will address workforce development barriers and support Syracuse's South Side economic revitalization alongside nearby manufacturing and community health investments.
depts: Office of the Chief Innovation Officer

title: Former Syracuse vocational school could be demolished for 138-unit apartment building
summary: City officials are negotiating with Philadelphia-based mixed-income developer Pennrose and local developer Luke Esposito to demolish the city-owned Johnson Vocational Center and construct a 138-unit apartment building with ground-floor commercial space. The project is part of a larger downtown revitalization corridor near the I-81 viaduct removal, expected to add 600+ apartments to the area and advance the city's affordable housing and economic development objectives.

title: Hogan: Council, not appointed board, should have final say on historic properties (Your Letters)
summary: Common Councilor Patrick Hogan defends proposed legislation to shift final decision-making authority on already-designated historic properties from the appointed Landmark Preservation Board to the elected Common Council. Hogan argues this ensures democratic accountability and balances historic preservation with present-day neighborhood and economic development needs, while maintaining the Council's existing role in the designation process itself.

title: House GOP bill punishes Syracuse, other ‘sanctuary cities’ by closing federal small biz offices
summary: House Republicans passed the 'Save SBA from Sanctuary Cities Act of 2025' which could force closure of the U.S. Small Business Administration's Syracuse district office serving 34 NY counties due to the city and state's sanctuary jurisdiction status. The Syracuse office approved $91.4M in loans through May 2025; closure would eliminate significant economic support for regional small businesses. The bill passed 211-199 and now faces Senate consideration.

title: House GOP’s ‘big beautiful bill’ cancels millions for Syracuse to revitalize poor neighborhood near I-81
summary: House Republicans' 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' cancels $30 million in unspent Federal Highway Administration grants awarded to Syracuse for I-81 neighborhood revitalization. Syracuse had received only $300,000 of its $30 million grant and was awaiting environmental review completion before accessing remaining funds. The bill permanently rescinds all unspent Neighborhood Access and Equity Program funds ($2+ billion nationally) to reduce federal deficit. Senate Democrats, including Sen. Schumer, plan to restore funding.
depts: Office of the Chief Innovation Officer

title: How owner Mark Congel lost control of historic Syracuse convent before fire ravaged it
summary: Historic St. Anthony's Convent on Syracuse's North Side was destroyed by a suspected arson fire on March 21, 2025, after years of inadequate security and maintenance by owner Mark Congel. The 129-year-old motherhouse building, designed by architect Archimedes Russell and acquired by Congel in 2015 for $500,000, suffered catastrophic damage requiring emergency demolition. Mayor Ben Walsh publicly criticized Congel for failing to protect the property, highlighting governance and property stewardship failures in a neighborhood lacking investment.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: It’s a ‘race to the finish,’ but Syracuse STEAM school will open in September (photos)
summary: Syracuse STEAM High School, a $100 million regional high school focused on science, technology, engineering, arts, and math, is scheduled to open in September 2025 with 250 inaugural freshman students despite ongoing construction. The state-funded project (98% reimbursement) represents significant infrastructure investment in workforce development for the region's growing tech economy, with facilities designed to support nine academic concentrations including robotics, animation, and data analytics.
depts: Department of Facilities and Capital Planning

title: Le Moyne College extends president’s contract
summary: Le Moyne College announced a two-year contract extension for President Linda LeMura, extending her tenure through June 30, 2028. LeMura will become the longest-serving president in the college's 160+ year history in December 2025, having served since 2014. This is a leadership continuity announcement at a major higher education institution in the Syracuse region.

title: Man tased by Syracuse police in convenience store arrest; they say he sold drugs outside
summary: Syracuse police arrested Tashawn Albert on April 9, 2025 at a Park Street convenience store for alleged drug possession and resisting arrest, deploying a taser during the encounter. Albert has filed a complaint with the Civilian Review Board alleging excessive force, claiming he suffered spinal fracture, broken tooth, and taser injuries, while police assert Albert resisted and reached toward his waistband. The incident highlights tensions between law enforcement procedures and civilian oversight mechanisms.

title: Mayor, Congressman, others praise retiring Syracuse superintendent in final days
summary: Syracuse Public Schools Superintendent Anthony Davis retires October 31 after 39-year career in education, including 3 years as superintendent. City leadership including Mayor Walsh, U.S. Rep. Mannion, and State Sen. May praised Davis for opening STEAM high school, addressing food insecurity, and mentoring community members. Deputy Superintendent Pamela Odom will assume leadership.

title: McMahon’s 2026 Onondaga County budget includes tax rate cut, new police drones, helicopter
summary: Onondaga County Executive Ryan McMahon's proposed 2026 budget totals $1.6 billion with a 3% spending increase while maintaining flat property tax levy at $146 million. Key initiatives include $6 million for a new sheriff's helicopter, $700,000 for a drone-as-first-responder emergency program, $300,000 for body-worn cameras for custody deputies, and continued funding for lead remediation ($1.23 million), early childhood services ($2.3 million), and school mental health programs.

title: Mets play Billy Joel’s ‘We Didn’t Start the Fire’ as brush burns. Turns out they did start the fire
summary: A brush fire was ignited by fireworks at NBT Bank Stadium on July 24, 2025, after a Syracuse Mets game. The fire was quickly extinguished by the Syracuse Fire Department with no injuries or property damage. This was the third fire incident in approximately 300 fireworks shows over 12 years, occurring during an unusually hot and dry July.

title: Micron gets ready to make its most visible move yet in Central NY
summary: Micron Technology obtained a permit to install an 8-foot by 40-foot corporate logo on One Lincoln Center downtown, replacing a Chase bank sign at a cost of $115,000. The sign installation represents a visible milestone in Micron's broader $100 billion chipmaking factory project in Clay that could employ 9,000 people and transform the Central New York economy. The company is simultaneously renovating office and welcome center space in the building, scheduled to open by early fall.
depts: Department of Permitting and Regulatory Services
Department of Economic Development and Planning

title: Micron impact: Syracuse area will grow ‘as fast as any place in the country,’ major bank CEO predicts
summary: KeyBank CEO Chris Gorman predicts Syracuse will experience rapid population and job growth comparable to Texas markets following Micron's $100B semiconductor factory investment in Clay. The project is expected to create 9,000 Micron jobs plus 40,000+ supply chain jobs, attract 50,000+ new residents (10% county increase), and drive demand for multifamily housing and ancillary economic development across the region.

title: Onondaga County hosting lead safety events this week for families, landlords and contractors
summary: Onondaga County is hosting multiple lead poisoning prevention events during National Lead Poisoning Prevention Week (Oct. 19-25, 2025) targeting families, landlords, and contractors. Key initiatives include a public information session on lead hazards, a contractor networking fair, participation in a refugee health fair, and a community event. The county reports ~10% of Syracuse children tested have elevated blood lead levels, with approximately 14,000 homes still using lead pipes for tap water.
entities: Onondaga County Health Department Lead Poisoning Prevention Program

title: Onondaga County plans to open a downtown drop-in center for city’s homeless
summary: Onondaga County plans to open a daytime drop-in center in downtown Syracuse by August 2025, funded by opioid settlement proceeds, to serve 30-50 homeless individuals daily. The center will offer security, mental health support, drug treatment, fentanyl testing, hygiene facilities, and food—designed to address street-level behavioral issues near the Rescue Mission while supporting downtown revitalization efforts.

title: Pioneer Homes residents get early chance to avoid noisy I-81 construction. How many will take it?
summary: Pioneer Homes residents are being offered early relocation through $5 million in HUD housing vouchers to avoid I-81 demolition construction beginning in 2026 and concurrent East Adams neighborhood renovation. So far 54 households plan to use vouchers and 32 prefer remaining in public housing elsewhere, with vouchers expiring at year-end 2025 and new apartments expected by March 2027.
depts: Department of Housing and Urban Development

title: Police presence will increase at Syracuse pools after fatal shooting in Strathmore
summary: A 15-year-old was fatally shot outside Upper Onondaga Park Pool on June 25, 2025, prompting Mayor Walsh to announce increased police presence at city pools. The suspect remains at large and the weapon unfound. Pool reopening timeline is undetermined, and Parks & Recreation staff are receiving support services.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Police union leader should lower the heat after combative Armory Square comments (Editorial Board Opinion)
summary: Editorial board criticizes Syracuse Police union leader Joseph Moran for publicly encouraging officers to disregard the department's use-of-force and de-escalation policies during increased Armory Square patrols. The opinion disputes Moran's characterization of the bar district as 'chaotic' and 'infiltrated' by gang members, noting that increased patrols are for nuisance behavior management, not violence response, and that firsthand observation showed no dangerous activity warranting such rhetoric.

title: Police warn Syracuse businesses of fraudulent zoning invoice scam
summary: Syracuse Police Department and Department of Neighborhood and Business Development issued a public warning about fraudulent zoning invoice scams targeting city businesses. Fake invoices requesting wire transfers for approval, submission, environmental impact, and review fees have targeted at least three businesses. The city clarified it never requests wire transfers for zoning fees and provided contact numbers for verification and reporting.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Ryan McMahon cites a ‘pipeline’ of housing projects, but it’s filled with uncertainty
summary: County Executive Ryan McMahon claims a 'pipeline' of 17,000+ housing units to address Onondaga County's projected need for 1,700 units annually through 2040, but only ~8,000 units are sufficiently defined for public listing. The remaining projects are conceptual or unpublicized, creating uncertainty about whether the county can meet housing demand driven by the Micron Technology facility. Current production (588 units in 2024) falls far short of targets.

title: School zone speeding fines raise concerns about equity, spending priorities (Your Letters)
summary: A South Side community resident raises concerns about school zone speed enforcement equity, citing disproportionate impact on low-income families and perceived disparities across neighborhoods. The letter calls for transparent budgeting, clarification on fine revenue allocation, and consistent policy application to balance student safety with fairness.

title: Senate OKs Trump’s plan to cancel millions for new Syracuse neighborhood
summary: Senate Republicans passed legislation canceling unspent grants from the $3.3 billion Neighborhood Access and Equity Program, eliminating Syracuse's $30 million federal grant for I-81 corridor revitalization. Syracuse had received only $300,000 of the award due to required environmental review timelines. The cancellation removes critical infrastructure funding for a neighborhood redevelopment project involving streets, parks, bike paths, and housing preparation.
depts: Office of the Chief Innovation Officer

title: Should police chase stolen cars? Two Central NY departments set opposite policies
summary: Syracuse Police Department and Onondaga County Sheriff's Office have adopted opposite policies on pursuing stolen vehicles. SPD implemented a no-chase policy for stolen cars unless connected to violent felonies, citing safety concerns and ineffectiveness at reducing theft rates (1,268 vehicles stolen in 2024, up 12% YoY). OCSO continues pursuing stolen vehicles, creating inconsistent enforcement across jurisdictions. National data shows police pursuits killed over 3,000 people (2017-2022), with majority for minor offenses.

title: Slow down or else! See where cameras will soon catch speeders near Syracuse schools
summary: Syracuse will deploy 34 speed cameras in school zones and 36 red-light cameras at intersections near schools, effective September 3, 2025. The $50-per-violation program aims to reduce speeding and improve pedestrian safety, with the city retaining 65% of fines ($3.24M projected annually based on Albany benchmarks) and private vendor Jenoptik receiving 35%. Speed cameras operate during school hours only; red-light cameras run continuously.
depts: Office of the Chief Innovation Officer

title: Some CNY towns could run out of water this weekend, county warns
summary: A ruptured water transmission line in Cicero has created a critical infrastructure crisis affecting six Onondaga and Madison County towns (Manlius, DeWitt, Pompey, Sullivan, Lenox, Lincoln). Storage tank levels have dropped to 18% capacity, with southern Manlius, northern Pompey, and parts of Fayetteville at imminent risk of losing water by Sunday without aggressive conservation reaching 3.5 million gallons/day usage. County and OCWA have activated emergency response protocols including temporary water lines, emergency shipments from alternative lakes, portable pumping systems, and standby tanker/bottled water distribution.
depts: Office of the Onondaga County Executive

title: Surprise move leaves Syracuse Housing Authority with new role in East Adams: Landlord
summary: The Syracuse Housing Authority (SHA) will assume ownership and management of 1,400 apartments in the East Adams redevelopment project, expanding its traditional role beyond its historical trajectory. This represents a strategic pivot from the original plan where private developer McCormack Baron Salazar would own and manage the properties post-development. The shift ensures SHA operational continuity and rental income stability but occurs amid significant leadership scrutiny from city officials and philanthropic partners.

title: Syracuse auditor: City’s ‘lifeless’ wage ordinance needs a serious overhaul
summary: City Auditor Alexander Marion released an audit finding Syracuse's 20-year-old living wage ordinance ineffective due to extensive loopholes, lack of enforcement mechanisms, and non-functional oversight. The ordinance—intended to ensure city contractors pay workers above poverty levels—has no documented compliance monitoring, an inactive advisory committee since 2014, and exemptions that exclude most contractors. Marion recommends comprehensive overhaul including eliminating exemptions, assigning enforcement to the Office of Management and Budget, and adopting a United Way-based wage formula reflecting local cost-of-living data.

title: Syracuse chief defends officers hitting man during arrest, but says some procedures violated
summary: Syracuse Police Chief Joe Cecile defended officers' use of force (three controlled strikes to head/face) during an arrest on South Avenue, stating the tactic was within department policy and state standards. However, the chief acknowledged that body-worn camera footage revealed unspecified policy and procedure violations that will result in discipline and retraining. The incident has generated public outcry via social media and raises questions about use-of-force oversight and transparency.

title: Syracuse Common Council lacks courage to fix unfair assessments (Editorial Board Opinion)
summary: Syracuse Common Council voted unanimously to cut $16M from Mayor Walsh's $348.4M budget, including elimination of a planned citywide property revaluation affecting 40,000 properties. The editorial criticizes this as political cowardice driven by mayoral candidates Pat Hogan and Chol Majok avoiding constituent backlash, arguing the revaluation is necessary to address decades of tax assessment inequities between neighborhoods.

title: Syracuse Common Council pushes proposal to build battery storage near city neighborhood into 2026
summary: Syracuse Common Council delayed a decision on New Leaf Energy's proposal to build a large-scale lithium-ion battery storage facility at 426 E. Brighton Ave. until 2026, pending legal review by the city corporation counsel. Fire Chief Michael Monds expressed safety concerns about fire risk and proximity to neighboring structures, while Councilor Hogan proposed a moratorium on battery storage permits. The decision reflects emerging tensions between state economic development incentives for battery infrastructure and local fire safety and neighborhood concerns.

title: Syracuse Common Council wants residents’ opinions on using drones for police calls
summary: Syracuse Common Council unanimously approved a public hearing (Sept. 15) on the Syracuse Police Department's proposal to deploy unmanned drones as first responders to 911 calls. The council is seeking community input on privacy and data-sharing concerns before voting on a $57,000 five-year licensing contract with Axon Enterprises for FAA remote operation clearance. The city previously approved $250,000 for drone equipment in November.

title: Syracuse considers offering amnesty for late fees on unpaid parking tickets
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh is seeking Common Council approval for a parking ticket amnesty program running November 3-21, 2025, allowing motorists to pay base fines plus a $5 state surcharge while avoiding accumulated late fees. The city has approximately 140,000 unpaid tickets totaling $5.3M in fines and $6M in late penalties; a prior 2020 amnesty generated ~$1M in revenue, though the city auditor previously recommended against future programs due to long-term moral hazard concerns.

title: Syracuse contractor donates $40,000 A/C overhaul so Mary Nelson’s center can reopen
summary: Local HVAC contractor Chad Terrinoni of Hummingbird Heating & Cooling donated a $40,000 air conditioning system overhaul to Mary Nelson's Youth Center, enabling the facility to reopen and resume serving 50-60 low-income children daily. The donation was prompted by a syracuse.com article about the broken A/C unit that disrupted the center's meal programs and community services; installation is scheduled for the following Tuesday.

title: Syracuse Finest no match for the Bravest in police vs. fire basketball game
summary: Syracuse Fire Department defeated Syracuse Police Department 92-88 in their charity basketball game at Corcoran High School on May 24, 2025, raising $2,500 for United Way of Central New York. The event marked the return of a decades-long inter-departmental rivalry after a five-year hiatus due to COVID-19, with approximately 250 attendees including Mayor Ben Walsh and SU Football Coach Fran Brown.
entities: United Way of Central New York

title: Syracuse has a buyer for shuttered fire station, but councilors have questions
summary: Mayor Walsh's administration proposed selling shuttered Fire Station 7 to JF Real Estate for $900,000 to fund a mixed-use redevelopment (4 apartments + restaurant). City councilors expressed concerns about fire protection gaps on the Near East Side, where population has surged due to student housing development. Fire Chief Monds indicated the building is structurally unsuitable for operations but the department plans to reopen Station 12 (July 2025) and pursue a new station on University Avenue as long-term solutions.

title: Syracuse lawmakers approve sale of former fire station where apartments, eatery planned
summary: Syracuse Common Council approved the $900,000 sale of shuttered Fire Station 7 to JF Real Estate for a $2.5M mixed-use redevelopment (ground-floor restaurant, 4 apartments). The project advances the city's Near East Side corridor redevelopment strategy tied to I-81 viaduct removal, with closing expected in 9-12 months and construction in ~12 months. Fire Department identified alternative University Avenue parcels for future station relocation.

title: Syracuse lawmakers finally endorse city’s plan to eliminate traffic fatalities
summary: The Syracuse Common Council unanimously approved Mayor Ben Walsh's Vision Zero Action Plan, a 30-year traffic safety initiative targeting zero fatal or serious injury crashes by 2055 with interim 35% reduction by 2035. The plan identifies high-crash intersections for prioritized infrastructure improvements and evaluates a city-wide 25 mph default speed limit. Council endorsement enables continuity across administrations and strengthens grant competitiveness without requiring immediate funding authorization.

title: Syracuse lawmakers OK settlement in case of drunk driver hitting recycling truck
summary: Syracuse Common Council approved a $110,000 settlement (6-3 vote) in a lawsuit filed by a passenger injured in a June 2020 crash involving a city recycling truck and a drunk driver. The city's legal department recommended settlement despite having 'moral high ground,' citing New York's joint liability law that could expose the city to greater financial risk at trial. Three councilors opposed the settlement, citing the drunk driver's primary culpability.

title: Syracuse lawmakers ‘disappointed’ with Walsh’s proposed tax hike, budget deficit
summary: Mayor Walsh presented a FY2025 budget with a $27M deficit and proposed 2% property tax increase to generate $7M in revenue. Common Council leaders, particularly Finance Chair Corey Williams and Councilor Pat Hogan, expressed disappointment and concern about deficit spending and the timing of tax increases. The council will conduct detailed budget review through April 29 public hearing, with final adoption due May 8.

title: Syracuse Mayor Walsh ready to accept some budget cuts; council leader not impressed
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh signaled willingness to accept $2.5M+ in budget cuts to eliminate a proposed 2% tax increase, but Syracuse Common Council leadership (Councilor Pat Hogan) rejected compromise and indicated they will override mayoral objections to their $16M reduction from the $348.4M budget proposal. The impasse centers on reserve fund usage ($27M proposed vs. council preference) and service impacts, with the council's final decision expected May 27.

title: Syracuse on Trump administration list of ‘sanctuary jurisdictions’
summary: Syracuse has been designated by the Trump administration's Department of Homeland Security as a 'sanctuary jurisdiction' on a list of 500+ localities alleged to obstruct federal immigration enforcement. The city joins 11 other NY municipalities, 15 counties, and NY State on the list. DHS has notified all listed jurisdictions of non-compliance and potential loss of federal funding, though specific penalties remain unspecified. City leadership maintains Syracuse's welcoming immigrant stance while claiming police do not enforce federal immigration law.

title: Syracuse police watchdog’s new leader abruptly resigns in latest CRB mess
summary: The chair of Syracuse's Citizen Review Board (CRB), Bryn Lovejoy-Grinnell, abruptly resigned on April 14, 2025, citing lack of resources, support, and independence from city hall and the Common Council. The resignation represents the latest crisis for the civilian police watchdog agency, which has struggled with internal conflicts, personnel turnover, and legitimacy issues. Council leadership disputes claims of interference and is exploring structural reforms through a proposed legislative update committee, though timing and implementation remain unclear.

title: Syracuse schools make progress despite some of the lowest test scores in New York
summary: Syracuse City School District ranked 647th of 658 NY school districts in ELA proficiency (24%) and shows low math proficiency (18%), though math proficiency has more than doubled since 2022 (from 8% to 18%). Despite remaining among the state's lowest-performing districts, SCSD demonstrated year-over-year gains in both ELA (+4 points) and math (+2 points), with third graders showing notably stronger performance (+8 points each). The article notes that standardized test scores alone do not fully capture student growth, particularly for English language learners and students requiring accommodations.

title: Syracuse to ask NY to cover millions feds canceled for East Adams housing
summary: Syracuse city officials are seeking $10 million in state funding to replace a $30 million federal grant canceled by Congress through President Trump's budget bill. The Neighborhood Access and Equity Program was eliminated, though Syracuse had only received $300,000 of its award before environmental reviews were completed. The funds were designated for East Adams neighborhood infrastructure (streets, parks, bike paths, sidewalks, lighting) supporting a major public housing redevelopment adjacent to Interstate 81.

title: Syracuse to show possible changes for streets near I-81, take public feedback
summary: The City of Syracuse is hosting two public open houses (Nov 12-13, 2025) to gather community feedback on the Reconnecting Communities project, which proposes streetscape improvements and a linear park along five South Side corridors affected by I-81 viaduct removal. A $500,000 federal grant funded design consultant Stantec to develop solutions enhancing walkability, biking, and transit connectivity across Colvin, Taylor, MLK, Adams, and Harrison streets.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs
entities: SUNY College of Environmental Science and Forestry

title: Syracuse University appoints Coast Guard veteran as new chief of campus safety
summary: Syracuse University appointed Michael J. Bunker, a U.S. Coast Guard veteran and experienced campus safety leader, as its new chief of campus safety and emergency services management, effective July 1, 2025. Bunker brings innovation in safety practices including mental health response teams, body cameras, and AI-enhanced security systems from his tenure at the University of Denver. His appointment represents a leadership transition as predecessor Craig Stone retires after 40 years in law enforcement.
depts: Syracuse University Campus Safety and Emergency Services Management

title: Syracuse University will not sign Trump’s higher education compact
summary: Syracuse University Chancellor Kent Syverud announced the institution will not sign President Trump's 'Compact for Academic Excellence in Higher Education' in its current form. The compact conditions include eliminating race, gender, and financial status from admissions; prohibiting criticism of conservative ideologies; and requiring institutional political neutrality. Syverud indicated willingness to reconsider only if federal modifications occur and after consultation with the Board of Trustees and university community.
entities: American Association of Colleges and Universities
SUNY College of Environmental Science and Forestry

title: Syracuse-area home building slumps when it needs to grow. Why are we stuck?
summary: Syracuse-area housing construction has slumped to 25-year lows (588 units in 2024, only 218 permits through July 2025) despite surging demand and the incoming Micron Technology chip fab project expected to bring new residents. County officials cite a pipeline of ~16,000 proposed units, but most are in early planning stages with uncertain timelines and funding; only 1,440 units are currently under construction. The CIO should monitor this as a critical infrastructure and economic-development challenge affecting workforce attraction and regional competitiveness.

title: Syracuse’s cheap municipal internet service now available in more neighborhoods
summary: Syracuse has expanded its municipal Surge Link broadband network to six additional neighborhoods following a $10.8M state grant, building on a pilot launched in October 2023. The service offers 100 Mbps at $14.99/month for low-income residents and $37/month market rate, positioning the city to reach 7,200+ households citywide as part of Mayor Walsh's poverty-reduction strategy.

title: Syracuse’s Columbus statue will outlast Ben Walsh, who promised 5 years ago to remove it
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh's 2020 promise to relocate Syracuse's 1934 Columbus statue remains unfulfilled as his term ends December 2025. Years of litigation, community debate, and advisory processes have yielded no final design for the proposed Heritage Park replacement. The next mayor will inherit an unresolved civic infrastructure and land-use decision complicated by preservationist opposition, Onondaga Nation advocacy, and potential Mellon Foundation grant involvement.

title: Syracuse’s water pipe woes: It’s going to take twice as long to replace lead lines, city admits
summary: Syracuse will take a decade or longer to replace all lead water pipes instead of the promised five years, after replacing only 700 of 3,000 targeted pipes in 2025. The city now plans 1,700 replacements for 2026 and faces another federal violation. Water Commissioner Robert Brandt cited logistical challenges in basement access and street coordination, though funding ($200M total project cost) is not the limiting factor.

title: Taking back $30 million I-81 neighborhood grant is ‘morally indefensible’ (Your Letters)
summary: Opinion letter advocates for restoration of a $30 million federal grant for 15th Ward neighborhood revitalization following I-81 viaduct removal, after House Republicans voted to rescind it. Writer frames the funding as essential to address historical community displacement and inequity, characterizing the rescission as 'morally indefensible' and calling for city leadership intervention.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Teen charged in killing at Syracuse pool was on probation at the time of the shooting
summary: A 15-year-old Syracuse resident was charged with second-degree murder in a fatal shooting at Upper Onondaga Park Pool on June 24, 2025. The suspect was on probation at the time of the incident and has been arraigned on violation of probation charges. Mayor Walsh announced increased police presence at city pools this summer in response.

title: Trump administration revokes visas of 3 Syracuse University students without warning
summary: The Trump administration has revoked visas for 3 Syracuse University international students without advance notice or explanation, part of a nationwide crackdown affecting 1,700+ students across 265+ institutions. SU (which enrolls ~3,300 international students) is working with affected students and coordinating with elected officials, though the university received no prior notification and has not publicly disclosed the students' identities or the selection rationale.

title: Trump administration sues New York City over ‘sanctuary city’ policies
summary: The Trump administration filed a federal lawsuit against New York City challenging its sanctuary city policies, arguing they obstruct immigration enforcement. The suit names Mayor Eric Adams, Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch, and other officials, citing policies that prevent local law enforcement from honoring federal immigration detainers. This action is part of a broader DOJ strategy targeting sanctuary policies nationwide and has direct implications for NYC's intergovernmental relations and public safety coordination.

title: Trump targets sanctuary cities: ‘We’re going to flood the zone,’ border czar says
summary: Federal immigration enforcement escalation targeting sanctuary cities, including Syracuse, which was listed as a 'sanctuary jurisdiction' by the Trump administration in May 2025. Border czar Tom Homan announced increased ICE presence in sanctuary cities. Syracuse Police Department and Onondaga County Sheriff maintain policies limiting immigration enforcement cooperation, though Mayor Walsh frames Syracuse as 'welcoming' rather than using sanctuary city terminology.

title: Update: Lawmakers withdraw proposal to delay fines for speeding in Syracuse school zones
summary: Syracuse City Councilor Amir Gethers withdrew a proposal to delay fines for school zone speed and red-light camera violations, allowing the city's traffic safety enforcement program to proceed with $50 fines starting November 3, 2025. The 60-day grace period ends after issuing approximately 60,000 warnings; projected fine revenue from violations would exceed $1.7 million.

title: Update: ‘Suspicious’ box strapped to tree near Syracuse City Hall belongs to state DOT (photo)
summary: A suspicious box found strapped to a tree near Syracuse City Hall on September 29, 2025, prompted evacuation and street closures. The device was identified as a state Department of Transportation sound measurement instrument, though city officials are investigating whether proper notification was provided. The incident highlights potential communication gaps between state and municipal agencies.
depts: New York State Department of Transportation

title: Walsh administration pushes to start biggest Syracuse housing development in decades
summary: Mayor Walsh's administration is advancing a $134M mixed-income housing development at the former Syracuse Developmental Center site, planning to break ground in 2026. The project would deliver 260 affordable apartments, 27 townhomes, and future phases including market-rate and senior housing—potentially Syracuse's largest housing development in 50+ years. The Common Council must approve financing mechanisms and infrastructure bonding today, though some council members express concerns about mixed-income sustainability.

title: Whole community must step up to care for homeless individuals (Your Letters)
summary: Opinion letter advocating for inclusive community planning regarding homeless services on Dickerson Street. Writer criticizes lack of neighborhood consultation during six-month planning process for a drop-in center near the Rescue Mission and calls for countywide responsibility-sharing for homeless care rather than displacement strategies.

title: With a party-line vote, Onondaga County lawmakers adopt 2026 budget
summary: Onondaga County Legislature passed the $1.6 billion 2026 budget on a party-line vote (12-4), with Republicans praising fiscal restraint (flat property tax levy, 11% tax rate cut) and Democrats criticizing insufficient funding for community services. The budget includes $3.3M for daycare, $1.2M for lead remediation, $6.75M for a sheriff's helicopter, and $700K for emergency response drones, but funded only 1 of 10 Democratic proposals for nonprofit community support.

title: ‘Draconian and dangerous’: Syracuse mayor blasts councilors for $16M in budget cuts
summary: Syracuse Common Council unanimously passed 80+ budget amendments cutting $16M in proposed spending without prior transparency or mayoral negotiation. Mayor Walsh characterized the process as 'unprecedented' in secrecy and called the cuts 'draconian and dangerous,' while Councilor Hogan defended the revisions as fiscally responsible with protections for police, fire, and city employment.

title: ‘Suspicious’ box strapped to tree near City Hall is a state noise monitor for I-81 project
summary: A noise monitoring device installed by NYSDOT contractors on a tree near City Hall was mistaken for a suspicious package, prompting evacuation and emergency response. The device is part of the $2.25 billion I-81 viaduct reconstruction project. While the city was notified of monitoring installations, this specific placement caught officials by surprise, though the device has been identified as safe and will be removed.
entities: New York State Department of Transportation

title: Developer shrinks Syracuse apartment project near I-81 for second time
summary: Developer Christopher Geiger has reduced the Gateway Apartments project near I-81 for the second time, cutting units from 291 to 259 (11% reduction) due to ineligibility for expected state brownfield tax credits on the eastern portion of the site. The seven-story project at East Fayette and Almond streets remains on track as the first development along the Almond Street corridor post-I-81 demolition, though no groundbreaking is expected in 2025 pending investor financing.

title: Syracuse chief defends officers hitting man during arrest, but says some procedures violated
summary: Syracuse Police Chief Joe Cecile defended officers' use of force (three controlled strikes to head/face) during an arrest on South Avenue, stating the tactic was within policy and state standards. However, Cecile acknowledged that body-worn camera footage revealed unspecified policy and procedure violations that will result in discipline and retraining. The incident has generated public outcry on social media.

title: Syracuse lawmakers ‘disappointed’ with Walsh’s proposed tax hike, budget deficit
summary: Syracuse Common Council leadership expressed disappointment with Mayor Walsh's proposed FY2025 budget, which includes a $27 million deficit and 2% tax rate increase. Council finance chair Corey Williams disputes the deficit calculation, viewing the true shortfall as $34 million when accounting for tax revenue. The council will conduct line-by-line budget review over the coming month, with final adoption due May 8.

title: NY should expand Kendra’s Law (Guest Opinion by Dr. Barry Perlman)
summary: Opinion piece by retired psychiatrist Dr. Barry Perlman advocates for expanding Kendra's Law (Assisted Outpatient Treatment) in New York, citing 2005 OMH research showing reductions in harmful behaviors, homelessness, and incarceration. Perlman critiques civil liberties organizations' opposition to AOT expansion, arguing their arguments do not address the significant cohort of seriously mentally ill patients who cannot engage in voluntary treatment. The piece reflects ongoing debate over involuntary mental health intervention policy at the state level.
entities: New York Lawyers for the Public Interest
New York State Office of Mental Health
Mental Health Association of New York State

title: City says Syracuse Housing Authority leaders are 'shooting holes in their own boat'
summary: City officials expressed frustration with SHA leadership over a $2 million environmental testing funding agreement for the East Adams neighborhood project. At a special SHA Board meeting, Executive Director Bill Simmons claimed the housing authority was excluded from negotiations. NBD Commissioner Michael Collins countered that SHA and its legal representatives participated. Project Director Joe Driscoll stated SHA was 'shooting holes in the bottom of the boat to sink the project.' The city withdrew the original proposal and arranged an alternative agreement directly with the developer, while discussing an additional $2.4 million SHA infrastructure investment.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Demolition starts at McKinney Manor, first step in Syracuse's $50M East Adams project
summary: Demolition commenced at the McKinney Manor Housing Project in Syracuse's 15th Ward, marking the first physical phase of the $50 million East Adams redevelopment. The project was announced in July 2024 when Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer revealed the $50 million HUD Choice Neighborhoods Initiative Program award. The plan involves redeveloping 600 public housing units into mixed-income housing. All former residents, relocated earlier in 2025, have been guaranteed the right to return. Development completion is scheduled for 2027. The article noted 'significant delays and little communication' preceding the demolition.

title: 2 Syracuse politicians want to cut all ties with license plate reader company over privacy concerns
summary: Two Syracuse Common Councilors (Jimmy Monto and Corey Williams) are seeking to revoke the city's $250,000 contract with Flock Safety for 26 license plate reader cameras, citing data-sharing privacy concerns and risks of misuse by immigration enforcement and other agencies. The proposal will be considered at the Common Council meeting on November 24, 2025. This reflects broader municipal reconsideration of Flock contracts nationwide.

title: 88-unit apartment building proposed for Syracuse’s University Hill
summary: Developer Norm Swanson proposes an 88-unit, five-story apartment building at 201 S. Crouse Ave. on University Hill, with plans to renovate an adjacent 1800s home into tenant event space. The mixed-use residential project targets both students and professionals; the Planning Commission review was pulled from the Dec. 15 agenda and will be rescheduled, with additional review required by the Landmark Preservation Board.

title: A cop’s trial is over, but Syracuse officials still can’t agree: Should they have settled?
summary: A Syracuse Common Council dispute over whether to settle a $825,000 lawsuit against Officer Kelsey Francemone (who fatally shot a man in 2016) has concluded with the trial verdict of only $10,000—but city officials remain divided on the settlement rejection decision. The city now faces $300,000–$500,000+ in accumulated legal fees from the three councilors' decision to block the settlement bond approval, raising questions about litigation cost management and risk tolerance in police liability cases.

title: A Syracuse group is out as ShoppingTown developer, putting project in doubt
summary: OHB Redev LLC has withdrawn as developer of the shuttered ShoppingTown Mall in DeWitt, a $400M mixed-use redevelopment project. County Executive McMahon negotiated the withdrawal to refocus the 65-acre site toward high-tech R&D and light manufacturing in response to Micron Technology's semiconductor complex announcement in Clay. The county's Industrial Development Agency will now seek new development proposals with expanded scope and total site control.

title: After 2 years, owner aims to rent apartments at the former Skyline Apartments within months
summary: Clear Investment Group is renovating two major apartment complexes in Syracuse (The Metropolitan, formerly Skyline Apartments with 370 units, and The Summit, formerly Vincent Apartments with 260 units) after acquiring them in 2023. Despite encountering $5.3M in unexpected costs including a major flood and security expenses, the company expects to begin leasing units by late 2025, offering below-median rents ($1,000-$1,520 at The Metropolitan). A city-company settlement established spring 2025 completion targets following litigation over renovation pace.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: AMR agrees to share staff and response data in first ambulance contract in Syracuse history
summary: Syracuse Common Council approved the city's first-ever ambulance service contract with AMR, establishing performance standards including 90% response time within 9 minutes and monthly data reporting. The no-cost agreement formalizes existing oversight but lacks enforcement penalties, drawing criticism from Councilor Majok for insufficient accountability mechanisms.

title: Bricks are falling from a smokestack at historic former Syracuse train station. City orders it razed
summary: A 100-foot smokestack at the historic former New York Central Railroad station (built 1936, listed on National Register of Historic Places in 2009) is deteriorating with bricks falling onto public areas near Burnet Avenue and Catherine Street. The City of Syracuse has ordered demolition after April warnings went unheeded, declaring it a major safety threat. The dispute involves property owner Tom Hornstein, who blames DOT vibrations from the Interstate 81 reconstruction project; DOT contests this and attributes deterioration to insufficient maintenance.
depts: New York State Department of Transportation

title: Carmelo Anthony visits Syracuse to launch his cannabis brand STAYME70
summary: Carmelo Anthony, Syracuse Orange legend and NBA Hall of Famer, launched his cannabis brand STAYME70 in downtown Syracuse on April 14, 2025, at Flynnstoned Cannabis Co. The brand supports community empowerment through NuProject (diverse entrepreneur funding) and Last Prisoner Project (cannabis criminal justice reform). This represents a high-profile private sector cannabis industry activity with social equity components.

title: City of Syracuse wins court order prohibiting illegal cannabis sales at North Side business
summary: City of Syracuse obtained a State Supreme Court injunction on June 18, 2025, shutting down The Herbal Center on North Salina Street for illegal cannabis sales without state licensure. Justice Murphy imposed $10,000 fines each on the business, owner John Vanfossen, and property owner, finding THC levels exceeded state limits and sales were 'knowing and defiant.' The defendant's counsel disputes the characterization and signals intent to appeal.

title: City workers moved out to make way for apartments. Syracuse still owns the empty building 2 years later
summary: Syracuse's sale of City Hall Commons to developers Hanover Real Estate Development has stalled two years after council approval. The city vacated the historic 1869 flat-iron building to One Park Place (costing $500K annually in rent) expecting a $13.2M mixed-income conversion into 39 apartments, but the $850K sale closed in March 2024 missed its November 2025 deadline due to complex state funding requirements. The deadline has been extended another year, but council expresses frustration over continued property ownership and rental obligations.

title: Crews will start removing dozens of trees this month at historic Syracuse park
summary: The City of Syracuse is undertaking a $320,000 improvement project at the James Pass Arboretum in Tipperary Hill, removing 50+ hazardous or invasive trees, pruning 40+ others, and planting 70 new trees in fall 2025. The project includes infrastructure upgrades (walkways, fencing, ADA accessibility) and commemorates the park's centennial, with community engagement occurring throughout the multi-phase initiative.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Downtown building owner sues to pause work on massive new Syracuse hotel
summary: A downtown building owner (Putnam Properties/Fred Davies) filed a lawsuit to invalidate city approvals for an $87 million, 10-story Onondaga Hotel project developed by Sun Development, seeking a more rigorous environmental impact study under State Environmental Quality Review Act. The lawsuit challenges Syracuse Industrial Development Agency and City Planning Commission decisions, citing concerns about obstruction of views, parking garage emissions/noise, and drainage impacts to the adjacent Lafayette Building. A court hearing is scheduled for February 11, 2026, and if successful, could delay the project by several months or more.

title: Dozens of new homes planned for vacant lots in poor Syracuse neighborhoods
summary: Syracuse secured $40M in state Block-by-Block grant funding to construct 25+ affordable homes on vacant lots in South Side and Near West Side neighborhoods. Nonprofits including Home HeadQuarters and Jubilee Homes are purchasing land bank parcels to build single- and two-family homes affordable to residents earning up to 60% of area median income ($56,880 for family of four). The program aligns with the city's 2020 housing initiative and new comprehensive housing strategy focused on infill development and neighborhood stabilization.
depts: Office of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Family of man fatally shot by Syracuse officer seeks new trial in lawsuit
summary: The family of Gary Porter, fatally shot by Syracuse Police Officer Kelsey Francemone in 2016, has requested a new trial, arguing the jury verdict was contradictory and that Black jurors were improperly excluded. The jury found excessive force but also agreed Porter had a gun, awarding only $10,000 in damages. The case now hinges on whether Francemone qualifies for judicial immunity protection.
entities: Chief U.S. District Judge Brenda Sannes

title: Family of man fatally shot by Syracuse officer seeks new trial in lawsuit
summary: The family of Gary Porter, fatally shot by Syracuse Police Officer Kelsey Francemone in 2016, has requested a new trial, arguing the jury verdict was contradictory and that Black potential jurors were improperly excluded. The jury found excessive force but also affirmed key aspects of the officer's account, awarding only $10,000 in damages. The case now hinges on whether Francemone qualifies for qualified immunity protection.

title: Feds award Centro $9 million to buy buses for new Syracuse rapid transit lines
summary: Centro received a $9.3 million federal grant to purchase nine custom compressed natural gas buses for two new Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) lines launching in 2028. The BRT system, forming an X-shaped route network across Syracuse, represents the centerpiece of a $35 million service redesign aimed at improving transit access for low-income residents and connecting them to employment hubs downtown, Destiny USA, colleges, and hospitals.

title: How ICE’s mostly unseen campaign in Syracuse breeds fear and resistance
summary: This Syracuse.com investigation documents ICE enforcement actions in Onondaga County during Trump's second term, focusing on detentions of immigrants in the voluntary immigration system, including students, hospital workers, and business owners. The article highlights community mobilization and advocacy response, raising implications for city services, immigrant integration, and social cohesion that may affect CIO priorities around digital inclusion, emergency communication systems, and vulnerable population data.

title: Hundreds of Syracuse teachers, staff get shorted on pay after $11M tech project screwup
summary: Syracuse City School District's $10.9M Oracle payroll system implementation (July 2025) has resulted in widespread pay errors affecting ~900 employees (nearly one-third of union members) in the first week of school, including missing checks, incorrect deductions, and missing raises. The Syracuse Teachers Association reports continued issues despite district and consultant efforts, with September cycles showing 900 errors initially, declining to 275 by month-end. The union has filed a grievance and is demanding increased consultant support and accountability.

title: If the Catholic Church can reappraise Columbus, so can we of Italian heritage (Your Letters)
summary: Opinion letter from We of Italian & Syracuse Heritage (WISH) CNY advocates for replacing the Columbus statue with a new 21st-century monument celebrating Italian-American contributions to Central New York. The letter argues that historical evidence of Columbus's harmful actions—enslavement, reward theft—justifies reconsideration, comparing the shift to the Catholic Church's abandonment of geocentric cosmology. Signatories call for community-grounded redesign honoring Italian heritage without Columbus association.
entities: We of Italian & Syracuse Heritage (WISH) CNY

title: Is Armory Square really so chaotic? We spent 4 hours there Friday, here’s what we saw
summary: Local news investigation examines safety perceptions in Armory Square following a viral Facebook post by police union claiming gang activity and chaos. Two reporters spent 4 hours observing the neighborhood on a Friday night to verify claims disputed by the police chief and deputy mayor, who attributed post-closing incidents to large gatherings rather than organized gang presence.

title: Is Armory Square really so chaotic? We spent 4 hours there Friday, here’s what we saw
summary: Syracuse.com reporters conducted a 4-hour observational study of Armory Square on July 18, 2025, to independently verify claims about neighborhood safety and chaos. The investigation was prompted by a viral Facebook post from the police union characterizing the area as infiltrated by "gangbangers," which city leadership disputed, attributing problems to post-closing hours gatherings rather than inherent daytime conditions.

title: Jury rules Syracuse officer used excessive force in Father’s Day shooting, but verdict likely to be overturned
summary: A federal jury ruled that Syracuse Police Officer Kelsey Francemone used excessive force in the 2016 fatal shooting of Gary Porter, awarding the family only $10,000 despite the city's prior $800,000+ settlement offer. However, the judge indicated qualified immunity will likely overturn the verdict after the jury found Porter was armed and posed a perceived threat, potentially eliminating civil liability for the officer.

title: Lawsuit dropped against big Armory Square building project, but the work is still on hold
summary: A lawsuit filed by the Landmark Theatre against the 401 S. Clinton St. apartment project in Armory Square has been dropped following an agreement between the theater and the city. However, the $18 million six-story, 88-unit development is now stalled due to a funding gap, with no construction timeline. The dispute centered on parking access for large trucks servicing the nearby theater, and both parties have committed to collaborative resolution.

title: Merchant: Syracuse can do better removing snow from downtown streets (Your Letters)
summary: Letter to editor from downtown merchant James Horan criticizing Syracuse's snow removal performance on side streets and parking lanes in the Downtown Business District. Writer acknowledges success of sidewalk clearing efforts but calls for enforcement of alternate-side parking rules and improved street snow removal to match the city's reputation, citing last year's poor performance that created hazardous ice conditions.

title: NY Attorney General ends whistleblower-prompted review of costly Syracuse IT project
summary: [Auto-generated] NY Attorney General ends whistleblower-prompted review of costly Syracuse IT project
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: NY puts off implementing law that would ban natural gas in new homes and buildings
summary: New York State has suspended implementation of the All-Electric Buildings Act, scheduled for January 1, 2025, pending resolution of federal litigation. The law would have banned natural gas and fossil fuel equipment in new buildings of seven stories or less. Gov. Hochul supports the climate initiative but delayed implementation to reduce regulatory uncertainty, drawing criticism from environmentalists who view the delay as abandoning state climate goals.
entities: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit

title: Onondaga Lake restoration is far from finished (Your Letters)
summary: Opinion letter criticizing County Executive McMahon's allocation of $2.5M in Honeywell remediation funds to the Inner Harbor aquarium instead of completing Onondaga Lake restoration. Letter highlights ongoing sewage management failures, worker safety issues at a $23M biosolids facility, and advocates for prioritizing lake cleanup and return of Maple Bay to the Onondaga Nation.

title: Owners of former Skyline Apartments in Syracuse hope to talk their way out of city’s lawsuit
summary: Clear Investment Group, owner of the former Skyline Apartments (now The Metropolitan), is in dispute with the City of Syracuse over building repairs and code compliance. The city filed a lawsuit Wednesday seeking a court-appointed receiver and $2 million in repairs after the developer failed to restore electricity or complete required inspections. Despite Thursday negotiations, city officials are proceeding with legal action amid deteriorating relations and ongoing housing vacancy.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Project connects old trails to form 1-mile loop in historic Syracuse park
summary: Thornden Park Association and city parks staff launched a pilot project to connect existing brick and paved pathways into a 1-mile loop using inexpensive mulched trails. Three trail sections are planned for 2025, with the first 500-foot section installed in July. The test approach allows the city to gauge public interest before investing in permanent infrastructure like lighting and signage.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Should Syracuse pay millions to fix historic steps? (Good Morning CNY for Sept. 29)
summary: Syracuse faces a $3 million decision on whether to repair a historic 178-step staircase in Westminster Park that has been closed since 2023 due to safety hazards. The cost has more than doubled from $1.2 million three years ago, prompting debate among city officials, lawmakers, and residents about resource allocation and historic preservation priorities.

title: Skyline Apartments owner promises to finish repairs soon in settlement with Syracuse
summary: Clear Investment Group has settled a lawsuit with the City of Syracuse regarding Skyline Apartments (to be renamed The Metropolitan), committing to complete renovations and make 350+ housing units ready for occupancy over seven months. The settlement includes weekly city inspections, phased certificates of occupancy, and drops the city's receivership action. This resolves a dispute over renovation delays attributed to fire/water damage and represents a critical housing supply recovery.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse airport isn’t showing Kristi Noem video that blames Democrats for shutdown
summary: Syracuse Hancock International Airport declined to air a Department of Homeland Security video featuring Secretary Kristi Noem blaming Democrats for federal government shutdown impacts, citing potential violations of Hatch Act restrictions on political activity by federal employees. Multiple airports nationwide (including Buffalo and Westchester County, NY) made similar decisions, while TSA workers remain on duty without pay during the ongoing shutdown.

title: Syracuse can’t afford to forgo millions of dollars in speeding tickets (Your Letters)
summary: Opinion letter criticizes the city's decision to implement a grace period for school zone speeding violations, arguing it forgoes $3 million in needed revenue and undermines traffic safety deterrence. Writer advocates for enforcement fines over warnings and questions fiscal impact through November 2025.

title: Syracuse closes large city-owned downtown garage due to safety concerns
summary: The City of Syracuse closed the Fayette Street Parking Garage (300 E. Fayette St.) on November 6, 2025, due to structural safety concerns discovered during routine engineering inspections. The 41-year-old, 550-space facility shows significant deterioration, particularly shifted concrete columns and exterior panels on the northwest corner. The city is conducting further testing to determine if the structure can be repaired or requires demolition, with revenue implications of approximately $700,000 annually.
depts: Department of Code Enforcement and Zoning Administration

title: Syracuse furniture retailer Dunk & Bright expanding with new store in Henrietta
summary: Dunk & Bright, a fourth-generation Syracuse furniture retailer founded in 1927, is expanding beyond Onondaga County with a new 72,000-sq-ft store opening April/May 2025 at South Town Plaza in Henrietta (near Rochester). The $4M investment includes plaza infrastructure upgrades and is expected to create 20-30 jobs, marking the company's third location and first outside the county.

title: Syracuse launches online search tool of its vacant lots for sale
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced the launch of City-Owned Property Search (syr.gov/city-owned-property), an interactive online tool enabling residents to discover and bid on 167 city-owned vacant lots by neighborhood, address, or map navigation. The tool streamlines property acquisition by providing direct links to purchase applications, with final sales requiring Common Council approval after interdepartmental review.

title: Syracuse neighbors fear commercial battery storage site could be dangerous
summary: South Side residents are opposing a proposed 426 E. Brighton Ave. commercial lithium-ion battery storage facility by New Leaf Energy, citing fire and environmental contamination risks. Syracuse Fire Chief Michael Monds refused to approve the current design as non-compliant with fire codes due to proximity to residences. The Economic Development Committee postponed the special-use permit decision pending public hearing and potential zoning code updates to restrict such facilities to industrial areas.

title: Syracuse plans ‘clanker system’ to stop trucks from hitting Park Street bridge
summary: Syracuse is installing a 'clanker system'—heavy rubber buoys suspended at bridge height—ahead of the Park Street CSX railroad bridge to alert drivers of over-height trucks before impact. The $500,000 project begins mid-2026 and will be fully reimbursed through NY State's CHIPS program. Bridge strikes have increased significantly (6 in 2025 YTD, 13 in 2024), prompting this infrastructure safety intervention modeled on successful Delaware deployments.
depts: New York State Department of Transportation

title: Syracuse sues company that was supposed to save Skyline Apartments, says they ‘failed to invest’
summary: Syracuse Mayor Ben Walsh's administration filed a lawsuit against Clear Investment Group, a Chicago-based developer who acquired the Skyline Apartments in 2023 with promises to renovate the 352-unit building. Clear has failed to execute its $4.6M renovation plan, and the property has deteriorated significantly including a catastrophic water leak in March 2025 that released an estimated 2 million gallons. The city seeks appointment of a receiver, $2M in repair deposits, and enforcement of $355K in accumulated code violations.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse’s largest apartment complex has spiraled downward. Tenants have had enough
summary: Nob Hill Apartments, Syracuse's largest residential complex with 760 units, faces severe deterioration including non-functional elevators, inadequate heating, water damage, and broken locks. The city filed a lawsuit in April seeking $340,000 in fines for code violations; a new management company (Destra Multifamily) was hired in March to remediate, while tenants have organized demanding repairs by October 1 and rent freeze through 2026.

title: Syracuse’s new snowplows have been on the road for weeks. Now they have their names
summary: Syracuse Mayor Ben Walsh announced the names of three newly acquired snowplows following a public naming contest that generated over 700 submissions. The winning names—'You're Killing Me, Squalls,' 'Syracuse Saltshaker,' and 'Snow Big Deal'—were selected from entries emphasizing pun-based creativity. This represents the city's second snowplow naming initiative, reflecting public engagement in municipal asset management.

title: Syracuse’s painful employment gap for Blacks and Hispanics: What can be done?
summary: Syracuse faces severe racial employment disparities, with Hispanic workers at 11.5% unemployment (11th-worst nationally) and Black workers at 13% (22nd-worst), gaps of 7.5 and 9 percentage points respectively above white unemployment. The article examines systemic and structural causes including segregation, generational poverty, and underresourced urban schools, while highlighting the Micron Technology project as an opportunity to address inequities through inclusive economic growth.

title: The City of Syracuse wants to sell a prime site worth millions. What’s taking so long?
summary: The City of Syracuse has delayed the sale of a prime 2+ acre property at 725 Harrison Street (a century-old school building) for over a year despite chronic budget deficits. Two main bidders—SUNY Upstate Medical University and California developer Ascent Property Co. (offering $10M + 400 market-rate apartments)—are in negotiations with no clear timeline. Political tension exists over tax-base recovery and housing development priorities.

title: The Syracuse Prize: $10K award will go to one resident who has enriched the city
summary: The City of Syracuse has launched the inaugural Syracuse Prize, a $10,000 award recognizing residents who have enriched the city's cultural vibrancy over the past five years. The application deadline is January 25, 2026, with finalists announced March 6, 2026. The prize is funded through a private donor-advised fund managed by Central New York Community Foundation and administered by CNY Arts, positioning cultural investment as a civic priority.

title: Two projects would bring over 125 apartments to one of Syracuse’s poorest areas
summary: Two housing development projects totaling 126 affordable apartments are advancing in Syracuse's Southwest corridor, with combined state and local support including $1M in Downtown Revitalization Initiative funding and property tax relief requests. Projects target low-to-moderate income residents and seniors, representing one of the largest new housing additions to the economically disadvantaged area in decades, with construction targeted to begin mid-2026.

title: Vacant Syracuse houses burn down at alarming rate. Can city predict and prevent them?
summary: City Auditor Alexander Marion released a report finding that 29% of Syracuse's 87 structure fires in 2024 occurred in vacant buildings, costing taxpayers $1.1M+ in property damage and $540K in fire department and demolition costs. The auditor recommends an early-warning predictive system using six criteria (proximity to past fires, inactive utilities, tax/water arrears, code violations) to flag high-risk properties for intervention, potentially identifying 50-100 properties at risk before fires occur.

title: Van Robinson, long-time Syracuse politician and advocate for city’s Black community, dies at 87
summary: Van Robinson, former Syracuse Common Council president and first Black politician to hold that position, died at 87. Robinson was a 57-year-old Syracuse resident and longtime NAACP leader who championed the I-81 viaduct removal project and community reconnection initiatives. His legacy includes the Van B. Robinson Common Council Chambers naming and significant advocacy for Syracuse's African American community.

title: Why did Syracuse’s airport reject Kristi Noem’s video? A no-politics policy, it seems
summary: Syracuse Hancock International Airport rejected a video message from U.S. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem citing its Terminal Advertising Policy prohibition on political messaging. The video blamed Democrats for a government shutdown, which airport officials classified as political advertising rather than permitted government public service messaging. The decision highlights tension between content neutrality policies and federal agency communications in public facilities.
entities: Syracuse Hancock International Airport / Syracuse Regional Airport Authority

title: Former Sears Building to be site of state's first workforce innovation hub
summary: Governor Hochul announced selection of the former Sears Building at 1300 South Salina Street as New York State's first ON-RAMP Workforce Innovation Hub, a $200M state initiative to connect skilled workers with manufacturing careers.
