The City of Syracuse and CenterState CEO are leading broader revitalization efforts, with $8.5M in Empire State Development funding approved for the flagship center. Initial training will focus on career exploration, ESOL, financial empowerment, and construction/manufacturing skills.

title: Residents worried about property tax increase due to $1.8 million plan
summary: Syracuse City officials proposed an $1.8 million, three-year property reassessment project affecting 41,000+ properties with anticipated 70%+ valuation increases. Common Council delayed voting on the project due to governance concerns; residents expressed significant anxiety about resulting property tax increases. The project, managed by Buffalo-based G.A.R. Associates, requires council approval before spring launch.

title: Syracuse maintains neglected St. Anthony's Convent for absent property owner
summary: St. Anthony's Convent on Court Street, owned by absentee property owner Mark Congel, caught fire on March 21, 2025, leaving Syracuse taxpayers to fund maintenance costs ($15K boarding, $23K maintenance/debris removal). Despite 15 accumulated code violations and city citations, Congel remains unresponsive; city action is constrained by an active Home Leasing development contract. Elected officials are calling for stronger enforcement mechanisms and potential property seizure.

title: Impending demolition of former northside convent elicits memories of its long history
summary: A nearly 130-year-old former Maria Regina convent on Syracuse's north side was heavily damaged by a suspicious fire on March 21, 2025, prompting the city to order emergency demolition due to immediate public safety risks. The building, which held significant cultural and religious importance to the community, will be demolished this week by owner Mark Congel, affecting 26 of 180 planned senior housing units on the campus. The property sale to Home Leasing should complete by fall.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Landowner's absence fuels frustration at town meeting after convent condemnation
summary: A historic convent (Motherhouse) in Syracuse was condemned for demolition following a March 21 fire that severely damaged the 100+ year old structure. A town meeting revealed frustration from neighbors and city council over the absent property owner Mark Congel's years of neglect (15 code violations issued), lack of external expert consultation in the demolition decision, and potential loss of salvageable artifacts including antique wood and stained glass windows. The city plans to determine what is salvageable while pursuing senior apartments development on the site.

title: Your Stories Q&A: Dispute over repairing potholes leads to DeWitt, Salina road being closed
summary: A dispute over road maintenance responsibility between Town of Salina, Town of DeWitt, and NYSDOT regarding a deteriorated 0.1-mile stretch of Townline Road has resulted in Salina closing and abandoning the road. The incident highlights intergovernmental coordination failures and unclear jurisdiction over boundary-area infrastructure, with potential implications for similar edge-case municipal assets.
entities: New York State Department of Transportation

title: Tensions boil over handling of Maria Regina property between Syracuse Mayor, councilors
summary: Tensions escalated between Syracuse Mayor Ben Walsh and Common Councilor Marty Nave over the city's delayed enforcement action against Maria Regina property owner Mark Congel. Following a March 21 fire that destroyed St. Anthony's Convent, the Division of Code Enforcement ordered demolition, prompting Nave to question why enforcement hadn't occurred years earlier. Mayor Walsh raised his voice at Nave during a follow-up encounter, leading Council President Pro Tempore Pat Hogan to publicly call for a formal apology.
depts: Division of Code Enforcement and Zoning Administration

title: Syracuse asks court to replace current redeveloper of Skyline Apartments with receiver
summary: Syracuse is petitioning NY State Supreme Court to replace Clear Investment Group with a court-appointed receiver for the Skyline Apartments on James Street. The 352-unit building has deteriorated despite Clear's 2024 promises of $4.5M in upgrades; the city seeks $355K in fines, $2M in escrow for repairs, and code violation corrections. This action addresses a critical housing shortage amid documented property failures including fire, flooding, and squatter occupation.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse seeks court order to remove Skyline Apartments owner
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh is petitioning State Supreme Court to remove Clear Investment Group as owner of Skyline Apartments and install a court-appointed receiver. The city alleges the property owner has neglected critical maintenance and repairs, creating health and safety hazards including unaddressed flooding and fire code violations amid Syracuse's housing crisis.

title: Syracuse landlord argues property on Euclid Ave. should not be consider historic
summary: A Syracuse landlord is challenging the historic designation of a property on Euclid Avenue, arguing it should not receive historic preservation status. The article references an anecdotal comment about notable figures visiting the site, suggesting debate over the property's historical significance and its implications for the owner.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Clear Investment says it's still committed to redeveloping dilapidated Skyline Apartments
summary: The City of Syracuse is pursuing legal action to appoint a receiver to replace Clear Investment Group as developer of the deteriorating Skyline Apartments on James Street, citing ongoing structural failures including a major water pipe burst. Clear Investment Group disputes the characterization and claims commitment to continued renovations, while housing advocates express skepticism about both the developer's capacity and the receivership remedy's effectiveness.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse councilors Hogan & Majok oppose proposed tax hike amid reserve surplus
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh proposed a $348.4M 2026 budget with 2% property tax and water bill increases, but Councilors Pat Hogan and Chol Majok oppose the hike citing the city's $120M reserve fund surplus—which has doubled since Walsh took office. City auditors note the unassigned fund balance is unprecedented at ~45-50% of annual expenditure versus the typical 15-20% benchmark, raising questions about fiscal necessity.

title: Consultants, waivers, lack of bids relied on during incomplete overhaul of Syracuse I.T.
summary: A Syracuse I.T. payroll modernization project faced significant governance and procurement failures, including use of waivers to bypass competitive bidding, reliance on multiple consultants, and allegations that Ernst & Young used Advanced IT as a pass-through vendor. A whistleblower complaint and subsequent Common Council audit have exposed incomplete project oversight and documentation discrepancies by city leadership, with the Finance Commissioner admitting project delays impacted critical audit work.

title: Syracuse leaders propose updates to municipal tree ordinance
summary: Syracuse Division of Forestry is modernizing the municipal tree ordinance for the first time since 1983, with City Arborist Stephen Harris leading efforts to update rules governing public and private tree management. The initiative aims to increase canopy cover from 28% to 35% city-wide and ensure equitable 30% minimum coverage across all neighborhoods regardless of income level, capitalizing on anticipated land gains from I-81 viaduct removal.
entities: SUNY ESF Department of Landscape Architecture

title: Police Chief: Officers will face discipline in viral South Avenue arrests
summary: Syracuse Police Chief Joseph Cecile announced that officers involved in a forceful arrest of two individuals on South Avenue on April 9, 2025 will face discipline and retraining for policy violations. The incident, captured in a viral Facebook video, resulted in one officer punching a suspect in the head. Body camera footage revealed procedure violations; the full investigation remains ongoing.

title: Syracuse Police drone program green-lit amid privacy concerns
summary: The Syracuse Police Department's Drone First Response Program has been approved by the Common Council for a one-year trial despite constituent privacy concerns. The program will deploy drones without audio capabilities in response to specific calls only, with deployment tracking available publicly. Key stakeholders expressed caution about long-term adoption and the difficulty of reversing the technology once implemented.

title: Lafayette Country Club turning into a lifestyle community
summary: Lafayette Country Club is being redeveloped into a lifestyle community with amenities including a dog park and walking paths. A commenter expresses skepticism about the project's viability based on a previous failed tech company proposal for the same site, questioning whether the development will materialize or default to low-cost condominiums.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Village of North Syracuse give little notice for taking away trash cans
summary: The Village of North Syracuse switched trash removal contractors from Syracuse Haulers to Butler Disposal with minimal resident notification. Trash cans were removed this week with only a door note as notice, leaving residents without bins until June 5th when Butler Disposal begins distribution. Residents report frustration over lack of communication and concerns about exposed trash attracting wildlife in this animal-populated area.

title: Community activist talks about rash of violent crime, young suspects in Syracuse
summary: Community activist Clifford Ryan, founder of OG's Against Violence, discusses a recent surge in violent crimes and stolen vehicle incidents in Syracuse involving teenage suspects. NY State criminal justice data shows over 4,000 police contacts with youth under 18 between 2019-2023, prompting community deterrence efforts and calls for youth to consider consequences of criminal actions during summer months.
depts: New York State Division of Criminal Justice Services

title: Blueprint 15 Executive Director resigns as Children Rising Center project remains paused
summary: Blueprint 15 Executive Director has resigned while the Children Rising Center project (valued at $35 million) remains paused. The resignation raises questions about project leadership capacity and oversight of concurrent responsibilities. This represents a significant leadership change affecting a major capital initiative in Syracuse.

title: Onondaga County, Syracuse invest in unprecedented efforts to tackle lead contamination
summary: Onondaga County and Syracuse are undertaking a lead contamination remediation initiative with unprecedented resource investment. Progress is modest: three homes completed with nine more expected, prompting public criticism about government project delays, cost overruns, and delivery challenges.

title: New baseball-themed splashpad opens at Cummings Field
summary: Syracuse Parks, Recreation & Youth Programs opened a new baseball-themed splashpad at Cummings Field on July 24, 2025, replacing decommissioned baseball fields that suffered drainage issues. The splashpad was recommended by the Eastwood Association in 2021 and features baseball-themed design elements including baseball-adorned sprinkler and water systems. Commissioner Syeisha Bird indicated strong community engagement and future park development plans.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Light comedy and a catch phrase to help stop Parkway bridge strikes?
summary: New York State Department of Transportation launched the "Check Your Height, Know It's Right" public awareness campaign across 16 states and D.C. to reduce bridge strikes, particularly on the Onondaga Lake Parkway. The multi-modal campaign combines humorous video ads with increased State Police patrols and infrastructure modifications (warning signs, lane narrowing, ramp closure) to address the 350 bridge strikes that occurred in NY State in 2024, following the deadly 2010 Megabus incident.
depts: New York State Department of Transportation
entities: New York State Department of Transportation

title: Recurring harmful algal blooms -- is a long term fix on the horizon?
summary: Harmful algal blooms (HABs) are causing recurring beach closures across Central New York lakes, with Cayuga Lake and Taughannock Falls State Park among the hardest hit. Environmental advocates argue that current monitoring by state and local agencies does not address root causes—excess phosphorus and nitrate pollution from agricultural runoff—and propose riparian buffer planting as a cost-effective long-term solution.

title: Talks collapse over early childhood center at former St. Ann's Church
summary: Negotiations have collapsed regarding the conversion of the former St. Ann's Church into an early childhood center. The article references missed opportunities for state funding and mentions involvement from a local school district, but the actual content available is limited to a social media comment criticizing the project leadership rather than comprehensive reporting.

title: Sisters reflect on the demolition of the Maria Regina Motherhouse
summary: Article discusses the demolition of Maria Regina Motherhouse with community commentary on religious institutions. The piece appears to be a news story with reader comments reflecting divergent views on the Catholic Church and religious life, relevant to Syracuse's historic preservation and facilities management considerations.

title: Historic Convent Complex chapel's fate uncertain as convent deconstruction is concluding
summary: Article reports on the uncertain future of a historic convent complex chapel as deconstruction of the convent concludes. A proposed housing project at the site has generated community concern, with a commenter expressing opposition to the development and broader political commentary on housing policy.
depts: Department of Neighborhood and Business Development

title: Syracuse report urges steps to curb vacant building fires
summary: Syracuse City Auditor Alexander Marion released a 100+ page report titled 'Following the Fire' analyzing 87 structure fires in 2024, of which 25 (approximately one-third) occurred in vacant buildings. The report recommends immediate actions including enhanced property inspections, FEMA-standard labeling of unsafe structures with red 'X' signs, and pattern identification to enable early interventions and prevent future fires that endanger both residents and first responders.

title: Syracuse schools brace for cuts from recently passed spending bill
summary: Syracuse City School District, one of New York's 'big five' districts, faces a potential $7 million funding gap from a recently passed federal spending bill. Superintendent Anthony Davis is developing contingency plans and emphasizes that school funding impacts the broader community through effects on SNAP, Medicaid, and student services. The district's $600 million budget may require difficult decisions if federal cuts materialize.

title: Mary Nelson Youth Center hosts annual back-to-school giveaway
summary: The Mary Nelson Youth Center held its 2025 Back-to-School Supply Giveaway on August 16, 2025 at Meachem Park, distributing grade-appropriate school supplies and backpacks to Syracuse children. Now in its 24th year since 2002, the event serves as a community engagement and youth support initiative addressing educational equity and family financial burden during back-to-school season.

title: Long-planned development project on Syracuse’s south side gains momentum
summary: The Southside Renaissance Project, a $41 million mixed-use development on Syracuse's South Ave, is advancing after four years of planning with groundbreaking expected in 2027 and completion in 2029. The project includes a business incubator for restaurateurs of color and 75 units of senior affordable housing, developed by the Women's Economic Institute and St. Nicks Alliance with funding from private sources, state loans, grants, and tax credits.
entities: New York State Office of the Local Initiatives Support Corporation (LISC)

title: Onondaga County expands hotel room tax to include short-term rentals
summary: Onondaga County expanded its hotel room occupancy tax to include short-term rental properties booked through platforms like Airbnb and VRBO, effective immediately. The measure requires a county short-term rental registry and is projected to generate $1 million in annual revenue. Tax collection will be automated through booking platforms for most owners, with non-platform users required to remit taxes manually.
depts: Office of the County Fiscal Officer

title: Parents feel extra safe with the new school zone spedometers
summary: Syracuse has installed speed-detection signs in school zones to enhance pedestrian safety as the school year begins. The devices display real-time speed warnings to drivers exceeding 25 MPH limits, with CNY Central's field test finding 60% of drivers speeding. After a 60-day grace period starting Wednesday, violations will incur $50 fines.

title: Syracuse officials expect deer management pilot program to continue
summary: Syracuse is continuing a deer management pilot program involving trapping and euthanasia in high-density residential areas where firearm use is unsafe. The program killed 55 of 137 deer this year and donated 4,000 pounds of venison to the Food Bank. Chief Policy Officer Greg Loh cites overpopulation, Lyme disease transmission, and property damage as justification, though residents like Nancy Larson oppose the approach and advocate for alternative solutions.

title: Syracuse City School District looks to pay outgoing Superintendent $125,000 for consulting
summary: Syracuse City School District is proposing a $125,000 consulting contract with TD22 Consulting LLC, owned by outgoing Superintendent Anthony Davis, to provide transition support and coaching through October 31, 2025. The contract aims to support strategic plan implementation during the leadership transition to Deputy Superintendent Pamela Odom.

title: Syracuse City Schools spend 50% more per student yet rank near bottom in state test scores
summary: Syracuse City School District spends $31,333 per student (50% above national average of $20,000) yet ranks 644th of 658 districts in ELA proficiency and 549th of 550 in math proficiency. Leadership transition underway with Deputy Superintendent Pamela Odom assuming superintendent role; district considering $125,000 consulting contract with outgoing Superintendent Anthony Davis's company.
entities: New York State Department of Education

title: Calvin Corriders Sr. has left his position as chair of the Syracuse Housing Authority
summary: Calvin Corriders Sr. has departed his role as chair of the Syracuse Housing Authority. The article provides limited substantive detail beyond the announcement of his departure, with a single community comment suggesting frustration with leadership tenure.

title: ICE comments on Syracuse business owner’s self-deportation amid visa controversy
summary: ICE detained a French business owner in Syracuse operating on an expired K-1 visa, leading to community intervention and his self-deportation. ICE stated the situation was avoidable and referenced available compliance tools including voluntary departure assistance, but the case highlights tensions between immigration enforcement and local business community protection.

title: Syracuse’s north side residents air their frustrations the fire-damaged building on Maria Regina campus
summary: North side residents held a public forum to address concerns about the fire-damaged Maria Regina College Motherhouse building. City officials including Fire Chief Michael Monds attended to discuss safety protocols, demolition timeline, and future campus redevelopment. The incident highlights community attachment to the historic Franciscan property and resident demands for stronger accountability of private property owners.

title: Mayor Walsh announces hiring events
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced two city hiring events to fill vacancies across city government. The Department of Water will host a specialized recruiting event on April 29 with on-the-spot job offers for watershed inspectors, water system construction engineers, and maintenance workers. A broader multi-department job fair follows on May 12, indicating organized workforce development efforts across city operations.

title: Leader says Common Council will ‘probably’ override Syracuse mayor’s requests to restore budget cuts
summary: The Syracuse Common Council President Pro-Tempore Pat Hogan announced the council will likely override all 45 budget vetoes issued by Mayor Ben Walsh, indicating a significant breakdown in executive-legislative budget alignment. This signals potential conflict over spending priorities and resource allocation decisions.

title: STEAM High School to open this September, Syracuse City School District says
summary: Syracuse City School District is launching STEAM High School in September 2025 with an initial cohort of 250 ninth-grade students, expanding to include tenth grade in subsequent years. The school will offer programs in civil engineering, robotics, data analytics, and performing arts, representing a significant initiative in workforce development and STEM education aligned with economic development priorities.

title: Low-Income internet provider expands coverage to more than 14,000 Syracuse residents
summary: Surge Link, Syracuse's affordable high-speed internet provider, expanded coverage to over 14,000 residents on July 8, 2025, up from initial coverage of 1,000+ people. The service offers 100 Mbps internet for $14.99/month to low-income households qualifying for assistance programs like Medicare or SNAP. Mayor Ben Walsh announced the expansion at a press conference.

title: Firefighters manage brush fire by NBT Bank Stadium
summary: Syracuse Fire Department responded to a brush fire behind NBT Bank Stadium on July 24, 2025 at approximately 9 p.m. Firefighters quickly brought the fire under control. The cause remains under investigation. This incident demonstrates emergency response capacity in the city.

title: Syracuse dropped its carbon emissions over decade by wide margin, exceeding goals
summary: Syracuse released its 2024 Greenhouse Gas Emission Inventory showing the city reduced municipal emissions by 67% and community emissions by 29% over 14 years (2010-2024), significantly exceeding original goals of 40% and 7% respectively. This achievement demonstrates substantial progress on the city's sustainability and climate action commitments.
depts: Office of the Chief Innovation Officer

title: Syracuse mayor’s final community briefing highlights public safety and housing crisis
summary: Syracuse Mayor Ben Walsh delivered his final community briefing before his December 31 term end, highlighting public safety achievements with overall crime down over 20%, including significant decreases in both violent and property crime year-over-year. The briefing also addressed housing crisis concerns, signaling administration priorities in public safety and housing for the incoming leadership transition.

title: OCWA still seeking water service line information from 48,000 customers
summary: Onondaga County Water Authority (OCWA) is conducting a mandatory EPA-mandated inventory of 106,000 water service lines to identify lead and galvanized steel pipes requiring replacement by 2027. With 48,000 customers still unreported, OCWA is mailing reminders and seeking customer cooperation through photo submission and home visits to build an accurate predictive model and avoid costly yard excavations.

title: Homepage
summary: WAER homepage aggregates regional news including Syracuse University athletics leadership changes, New York state climate and insurance policy developments, and local Central New York infrastructure updates. Limited direct relevance to Syracuse city government operations; primarily covers university, state-level governance, and sports coverage.

title: Syracuse basketball finalizing deal to hire Gerry McNamara as next head coach
summary: Syracuse University is finalizing a deal to hire Gerry McNamara as head coach of its basketball program. McNamara, a Syracuse alumnus and 2003 NCAA championship player, comes from a successful tenure at Siena College and will inherit a program that has missed the NCAA Tournament for five consecutive years. University leadership has committed to increased funding to restore competitiveness within the ACC.

title: Boeheim on McNamara: ‘I think they got the best coach available’
summary: Jim Boeheim, former Syracuse University basketball coach, publicly endorsed Gerry McNamara as 'the best coach available' following McNamara's hiring. Boeheim previously coached McNamara as a player and later employed him on his SU staff. This is a sports-related personnel announcement with no direct CIO governance implications.

title: Thousands of Upstate NY businesses face utility shutoffs sparked by hefty power bills
summary: Upstate NY businesses face significant utility shutoff risk due to unpaid National Grid bills, with January-February 2026 showing a threefold increase in at-risk customers compared to the prior year. This economic hardship trend may signal broader business cash flow challenges affecting the regional economy and potentially tax revenue generation for Syracuse.

title: SU hires McNamara to become next head coach
summary: Syracuse University has hired Gerry McNamara as the ninth head coach of its basketball program. This is a leadership appointment at a major regional research institution with potential implications for community partnerships and economic development related to university athletics.

title: Man stabbed multiple times on Syracuse’s East Side
summary: A 50-year-old man was stabbed multiple times on Syracuse's East Side on March 25, 2026, with police responding to the 100 block of Devine Street just after midnight. This incident represents a public safety event in the East Side neighborhood requiring police investigation and potential follow-up on crime data and community safety initiatives.

title: Syracuse University to axe nine majors as cost-cutting at the school continues
summary: Syracuse University announced plans to eliminate nine humanities-based majors as part of ongoing cost-cutting measures to maintain financial stability. While this is a university decision rather than municipal policy, it may have indirect impacts on workforce development pipelines and community education partnerships that the City should monitor.

title: ‘Mean Girls’ musical coming to Syracuse University
summary: Syracuse University's First Year Players will present a spring production of 'Mean Girls' musical with three performances beginning April 2 at Schine Student Center. This is a cultural and student engagement announcement with no direct municipal policy or service implications.

title: Syracuse will officially introduce Gerry McNamara at special event on Monday
summary: Syracuse University announces a public event on Monday to officially introduce Gerry McNamara as its new men's basketball coach. This is a university athletics leadership announcement unrelated to city government operations or the Chief Innovation Officer's mandate.

title: Upstate NY mayor arrested again on child rape, sexual abuse charges
summary: An Upstate New York mayor, Frank W. Kovacs of Laurens, was arrested on charges of child sexual abuse and rape allegedly committed between 2008-2011 in Clifton Park. The victim was under 17 years old at the time, with assaults reportedly beginning when the child was 13. This article documents criminal charges against a municipal leader and may have implications for local government oversight and institutional accountability.

title: Plans revealed for second Micron business park in Clay: 10 buildings, more than 100 acres
summary: Onondaga County is advancing plans for a second business park in Clay across from the White Pine Commerce Park, where Micron Technology is constructing a major semiconductor factory. The new park will feature 10 buildings on more than 100 acres, representing significant economic development and infrastructure expansion in the region.

title: Popular Syracuse University building to be featured in new FOX Nation series
summary: Syracuse University's architecture is being featured in an upcoming FOX Nation television series. This represents positive media exposure for the institution and the Syracuse region, though the article provides limited detail about the specific building or series content.

title: Traffic alert: Heavy traffic expected near dome Saturday for Syracuse lacrosse doubleheader
summary: Syracuse Police Department issued a traffic alert for heavy congestion expected around JMA Wireless Dome on Saturday due to Syracuse University hosting a lacrosse doubleheader (women's vs. Pittsburgh at 10 a.m., men's vs. Duke at 2 p.m.). The alert advises drivers and fans to plan ahead and arrive early to mitigate delays.

title: Federal judge orders ICE, border patrol to release records sought by Syracuse group
summary: A federal judge ruled in favor of TRAC (Transactional Records Access Clearinghouse), a Syracuse University-based research group, ordering ICE and U.S. Customs and Border Protection to release detailed enforcement data through a FOIA lawsuit. The ruling enables greater transparency into federal immigration enforcement operations and demonstrates successful use of public records mechanisms.

title: Cicero-North Syracuse grad nets game-winning OT goal for Le Moyne women’s lacrosse
summary: Local news article covering Le Moyne College women's lacrosse victory featuring two Cicero-North Syracuse high school graduates. Not directly relevant to City of Syracuse municipal operations, innovation, or policy.

title: Syracuse lacrosse couldn’t play defense vs. Duke but found another ‘D’ its been seeking
summary: Sports article about Syracuse University men's lacrosse team's performance against Duke. Not relevant to City of Syracuse municipal operations, innovation initiatives, or civic infrastructure.

title: Le Moyne men’s lacrosse falls to Robert Morris in NEC matchup
summary: Le Moyne College men's lacrosse team lost 19-4 to Robert Morris University in Northeast Conference play, falling to 2-9 overall and 1-2 in conference. This article reports on a collegiate sports event with no direct relevance to City of Syracuse municipal operations or Chief Innovation Officer priorities.

title: Department Of Engineering To Host Public Meeting For South Ave Bridge
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Engineering will host a public meeting on March 24, 2026 to gather community feedback on the planned rehabilitation of the South Avenue Bridge over Onondaga Creek. The $-amount-undisclosed project includes replacing bearings, rebuilding sidewalks, repairing masonry, and resealing the deck; online comments accepted through April 10.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Engineering

title: Dept. of Engineering Opens Comment Period for Stormwater Management
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Engineering has opened a public comment period (through March 25, 2026) for its Stormwater Management Plan (SWMP) and MS4 Annual Report, both required by NYS DEC's SPDES General Permit. The SWMP outlines stormwater pollution reduction efforts and best management practices; the Annual Report reviews 2025 progress and sets 2026 goals. This is a compliance-driven initiative to reduce sediment and pollution entering surface and groundwater.
entities: New York State Department of Environmental Conservation

title: Mayor, Council and Auditor Thank Senate For One-House Budget Proposal
summary: Mayor Sharon F. Owens and city leadership expressed gratitude to the New York State Senate for a One-House budget proposal that includes $30 million in additional aid for Syracuse—double the Governor's executive budget allocation. Northbound and southbound traffic will be detoured via Van Rensselaer St, Spencer St, and Solar St, while pedestrian access to the Creekwalk remains unrestricted.

title: Public Meeting on Regional Market Area Mobility Improvements March 5
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Engineering is hosting a public meeting on March 5, 2026 to present the Regional Transportation Center and Regional Market Area Mobility Improvement project. The initiative proposes multi-modal transportation improvements including sidewalk/curb replacements, ADA-compliant ramps, traffic signal upgrades, and shared-use paths across Park Street, NBT Bank Parkway, Tex Simone Drive, and Hiawatha Boulevard to enhance pedestrian and bicycle safety.

title: Reconnecting Communities to Unveil Designs for Five Key Corridors
summary: The City of Syracuse will host a Reconnecting Communities Open House on March 4, 2026, to present final design concepts for five key corridors (Colvin, Taylor, MLK, Adams, Harrison streets, and a proposed Linear Park). The initiative addresses historic transportation barriers while advancing Vision Zero goals and complementing the I-81 Viaduct Project, with online engagement options for public feedback.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: City of Syracuse Launches Lead Water Educational Materials
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water launched public educational materials on lead water safety in coordination with EPA and NYSDOH. Materials address health risks of lead exposure and mitigation steps, available online, in-person at City Hall and libraries, and by phone. The City is treating water with orthophosphate and actively replacing lead service lines ahead of EPA deadlines.
entities: New York State Department of Health

title: Mayor Owens Announces Release of 2026 City Services Guide
summary: Mayor Sharon Owens announced the 2026 City Services Guide will be mailed to residents starting January 26, 2026. The guide, produced by the Department of Public Works, consolidates service information, schedules, and contact details across city departments. The announcement emphasizes digital accessibility through SYRCityline (mobile app and web portal) for service requests and real-time updates.

title: DPW to Begin Overnight Leaf and Snow Removal Operations
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Public Works will launch overnight leaf and snow removal operations beginning November 2, 2025, to prepare for winter while maintaining fall leaf collection. Mayor Ben Walsh and DPW Commissioner Jeremy Robinson emphasize the operational efficiency of this dual-purpose shift, while requesting residents bag leaves and place them between sidewalk and curb to prevent infrastructure damage and flooding.

title: DPW to Convert Traffic Signals at Two Intersections to Flashing Lights
summary: DPW will convert traffic signals at two intersections (S. Geddes & W. Onondaga Street; Grant Boulevard & Lemoyne Avenue) to flashing lights from November 1, 2025 through April 1, 2026. This seasonal safety measure alternates northbound/eastbound flashing yellow with southbound/westbound flashing red to improve vehicle and pedestrian safety during winter conditions.

title: City of Syracuse Announces Delay in Trash Collection on Southside.
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Public Works announced a one-day delay in trash collection on Thursday, October 23, 2025, affecting 11 streets on the Southside. Residents are instructed to keep carts out; remaining pickups will occur Friday, October 24. Updates will be posted via @SYRCityline social media.

title: City of Syracuse Shares School Zone Traffic Safety Warning Data
summary: Syracuse launched a red light and speed camera enforcement program in school zones, recording over 60,000 warnings in the first two weeks (59,950 speeding, 430 red light violations). The city is implementing a 60-day warning period before full enforcement begins November 2, with fines of $50 per violation plus $25 late penalties. This initiative aligns with the city's Vision Zero traffic safety commitment to protect students.

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Launch of Redlight and Speed Cameras
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh launched the School Zone Traffic Enforcement Program on September 3, 2025, deploying red light and speed cameras near schools citywide as part of Vision Zero safety initiatives. A 60-day warning period precedes full enforcement with $50 fines per violation, supported by the Police Department and School District leadership.

title: Department of Water to Begin Mosley Pump Station Project Sep. 2
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water will begin the Mosley Pump Station replacement project on September 2, 2025, near Forest Hill Drive and James Street. The project includes pump station replacement, water main upgrades, and lead service line remediation for approximately 120 homes in the Eastwood area, with partial road closures expected but no water service disruptions anticipated.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: Traffic Advisory Citrus in the City
summary: Syracuse University's 'Citrus in the City' freshman orientation event on August 23-24, 2025 will require street closures and traffic modifications in downtown Clinton Square. Erie Boulevard West and S. Clinton Street will be fully closed from 8am Saturday through early Sunday morning, with West Water Street converted to temporary two-way traffic and parking restrictions in effect.

title: Water Department Reports 1st Half 2025 Sampling Meet EPA Lead Standard
summary: Syracuse Water Department reports that first-half 2025 tap water sampling from 121 homes with lead service lines meets EPA standards at 9 ppb (action level: 15 ppb), and already complies with EPA's stricter 2027 requirement of 10 ppb. Testing was conducted by third-party contractor 120Water with samples analyzed by independent laboratory, validated by state and county regulators. The results demonstrate effectiveness of the city's orthophosphate corrosion control treatment program.

title: City of Syracuse Releases 2025 Outdoor Pool Schedule
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Parks, Recreation & Youth Programs announced the 2025 outdoor pool season opening on a rolling basis starting June 18, with all pools operational by end of June through mid-August. Free admission open swim, lap swimming, swim lessons ($3/session), competitive swim team, and youth aquathon events are available across multiple city pools with varying hour schedules and registration periods.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation & Youth Programs

title: Department of Parks to Restore Hanover Square Fountain
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Parks will begin rehabilitating the historic Hanover Square Fountain starting June 16, 2025, through late fall 2025. The project includes restoration of the 1981 fountain structure, new lighting and mechanical infrastructure, enhanced landscaping, and accessibility improvements. This represents a capital investment in downtown revitalization and public space quality.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation & Youth Programs

title: Mayor Announces Completion of Phase 1 of Creekwalk Security Cameras
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced completion of Phase 1 of the Creekwalk Security Camera Initiative, deploying 20 new cameras at strategic locations along the Creekwalk trail from Onondaga Lake to Downtown Syracuse. Cameras are mounted on existing light poles and will be operated by the Syracuse Police Department to enhance public safety and deter unlawful activities. The project is part of a broader multi-phase effort to improve security infrastructure along the recreational corridor.

title: Mayor and Superintendent Announce Public Meetings on Phase III of JSCB
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh and Superintendent Anthony Davis announced public meetings for Phase III of the Joint Schools Construction Board (JSCB) project, covering ten Syracuse City School District schools. Three community sessions are scheduled (June 3, 5, and 10) to gather resident feedback on facility improvements and the Comprehensive Plan.

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Elmwood Park Sediment Removal Project
summary: Mayor Walsh announced a sediment removal and restoration project for Elmwood Park's Furnace Brook, running June–October 2025, funded by FEMA and NYS emergency grants. The project aims to restore the brook to pre-Tropical Storm Fred (2021) conditions through dredging, channel wall repair, and turf restoration. The park will be closed during construction, with moderate traffic impacts on Glenwood Avenue.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs
entities: NYS Department of Homeland Security and Emergency Services

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Opening of Kirk Park Canoe and Kayak Launch
summary: Mayor Walsh announced the opening of the Kirk Park Canoe and Kayak Launch on May 21, 2025, a new water access point on Onondaga Creek near Hunt Avenue. The Department of Engineering completed significant upgrades including an ADA-compliant launch ramp, safety rails, lockable gate, and a stilling basin. This is part of Syracuse's broader initiative to develop outdoor recreational spaces and improve water access infrastructure.

title: Mayor Announces Public Meetings for Phase 3 of Creekwalk Extension
summary: Mayor Walsh announced two public meetings (May 13 & 15, 2025) to gather community input on Phase 3 of the Onondaga Creekwalk Extension, which will extend the existing 4.8-mile trail south three miles from West Colvin Street to Dorwin Avenue. The project aims to provide safe pedestrian and bicycle access through Southside and Valley neighborhoods, with preliminary design underway and interactive mapping stations for resident feedback.

title: Mayor Announces Syracuse Wins Award for Trash & Recycling Education
summary: Syracuse won the Citibot Premier Community Award for its trash and recycling cart rollout education initiative, achieving 99.98% compliance through targeted public engagement campaigns and advanced enforcement technology. Mayor Ben Walsh highlighted the success as a model for effective municipal communication and community collaboration in service delivery innovation.

title: Dept. of Engineering Announces Open Comment Period for SWMP and MS4
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Engineering opened a public comment period (through March 28, 2025) for the Stormwater Management Plan (SWMP) and MS4 Annual Report, which outline stormwater pollution reduction efforts required by NYSDEC's SPDES General Permit. These documents detail best management practices and progress toward reducing sediment and pollution entering surface and groundwater systems.
entities: New York State Department of Environmental Conservation

title: Department of Water Working to Increase Water Pressure Overnight
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is addressing low water pressure across multiple city areas caused by a combination of factors: supply constraints from Skaneateles Lake, two restarted eastside pumps following power outages, and depleted reservoir levels due to multiple water main breaks this year, including a major break on the north side today. Pressure improvements are anticipated overnight.

title: City Department of Water Responding to Low Pressure in Water System
summary: The City Department of Water is addressing low pressure affecting high-elevation areas and high-rise buildings, caused by a water main break in the 100 block of 7th North Street and temporary supply reduction due to high winds on Skaneateles Lake. Service disruptions are expected until repairs complete this evening. This represents an active infrastructure incident requiring CIO awareness for potential IT/SCADA system monitoring and public communications coordination.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: City Department of Water Responding to Low Pressure in Water System
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is responding to low water pressure affecting high-elevation areas and high-rise buildings. The department is investigating water loss sources, requesting resident reports of leaks, and increasing water supply to restore pressure.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: Mayor Announces Open Houses for Vision Zero Traffic Safety Initiative
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced four Vision Zero Initiative open houses scheduled throughout February 2025 across Syracuse's four quadrants to engage residents on traffic safety goals and the Vision Zero Action Plan. The initiative aims to eliminate all traffic fatalities and severe injuries through infrastructure improvements including sidewalk programs, automated school zone enforcement, speed cushions, and bicycle infrastructure.

title: City to Host Open House for Kirk Park Pedestrian Bridge Project
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Engineering will host a public open house on February 11, 2025 to gather community feedback on the Kirk Park Pedestrian Bridge Project, which will replace an outdated bridge over Onondaga Creek. The project aims to enhance functionality, safety, and connectivity between the Creekwalk and Kirk Park, with public input accepted through February 28.

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Multiple Water Main Breaks
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is actively repairing multiple water main breaks across the city caused by recent temperature fluctuations. Five active break locations are identified with expected traffic rerouting and potential water service disruptions in affected areas; residents and businesses can contact the Department of Water for updates.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Break on Sunnycrest Road
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break on the 100 block of Sunnycrest Road, causing service disruptions for households and businesses in the area and low pressure in Eastwood. Sunnycrest Road between Shotwell Park and Hickok Avenue is closed to vehicle traffic; sidewalks remain open with detours in place.

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Break on East Genesee Street
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break on the 1800 block of East Genesee Street on January 8, 2025. Residents and businesses will experience water service disruptions through the evening, with East Genesee Street between Bassett Street and Columbus Avenue closed to vehicle traffic during repairs. Sidewalks remain open with traffic detours in place.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: City of Syracuse Announces Delays in Trash and Recycling Collection
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Public Works announced delays in trash and recycling collection services for Friday, January 4, 2025, due to recent snow events. Residents are advised to keep carts out and expect Sunday pickup for remaining households, with updates to be posted on @SYRCityline social media channels.

title: City of Syracuse to Begin Issuing Citations for Cart Violations
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Public Works will begin issuing $100 citations on January 6, 2025, for improper trash and recycling cart setouts, ending a grace period that began with the Fall 2024 uniform cart rollout. Property owners have 24 hours to correct violations before fines are assessed; common violations include placing trash in recycling bins, bagging recyclables, and failing to break down cardboard.

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Break on E Washington St
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break on the 100 block of East Washington Street, with service disruptions expected until late morning on December 29, 2024. Two street segments will be closed to vehicle traffic during repairs, though sidewalks remain accessible with detours in place.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: City of Syracuse to Collect Christmas Trees in January
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Public Works will conduct curbside collection of natural Christmas trees throughout January 2025 using a quadrant-based pickup schedule (Northeast Jan. 4-5, Southeast Jan. 11-12, Southwest Jan. 18-19, Northwest Jan. 25-26). Residents must remove all decorations and non-organic materials; artificial trees should be disposed of via regular trash service or bulk pickup. Alternative drop-off locations are available at OCRRA compost sites.

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Release of 2025 City Services Guide
summary: Mayor Walsh announced the 2025 City Services Guide will be mailed to Syracuse residents starting December 30, 2024. The guide, produced by the Department of Public Works, provides residents with service schedules, contact information, and instructions for trash/recycling, sidewalk maintenance, and sewer services. The city is promoting Cityline as the primary digital service request platform for non-emergency requests.

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Break on Pulaski Street
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break on the 100 block of Pulaski Street, resulting in water service disruptions for nearby households and businesses, pressure issues on the Northside, and a full closure of Pulaski Street between North Geddes Street and Giminski Drive. Residents and businesses can contact the Department of Water at 315-448-8360 for questions.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Break on E Washington St
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break on the 200 block of East Washington Street, causing water service disruptions to nearby businesses and a vehicle traffic closure between South Warren and Montgomery Streets through completion of repairs. Sidewalks remain accessible and traffic detours are in place.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: City of Syracuse to Close Offices for Christmas and New Years Holiday
summary: City of Syracuse administrative offices will close December 25 and January 1 for Christmas and New Years holidays. Trash and recycling pickup will be delayed one day during both holiday weeks; metered parking will be free on both dates. Emergency public works services remain available, and residents can submit service requests via SYRCityline app or website.

title: Mayor Walsh Issues Snow Safety Alert for Upcoming Winter Storm
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh issued a snow safety alert for a winter storm affecting Syracuse from Wednesday evening through Friday morning, December 4-6, 2024. The alert directs residents to follow parking restrictions, clear sidewalks and fire hydrants, and prepare for hazardous road conditions. The City will prioritize plowing primary and service roads while encouraging residents to monitor real-time conditions via the Snow Plow Map and social media.

title: City and County Share Progress on Water Filter Distribution Program
summary: City of Syracuse and Onondaga County launched a water filter distribution program targeting households with pregnant women and children under six to address lead exposure from lead service lines. Over 1,175 filters distributed in first three days through WIC program; subsequent phases will expand through Salvation Army Christmas Bureau and Syracuse City School District Pre-K through first grade enrollment.

title: County and City Announce Details of Free Water Filter Distribution
summary: Onondaga County and City of Syracuse are distributing free Brita Elite water filters to households with pregnant women and children under six due to potential lead contamination from lead service lines. Distribution begins November 25 at WIC program and Salvation Army sites, with school-based distribution planned for December. This addresses public health concerns stemming from earlier sampling protocol errors.

title: City Releases Sampling Results That Show Water Meets EPA Lead Standard
summary: Syracuse released water sampling results from August-October 2024 showing drinking water meets EPA lead standards at 12 ppb (action level: 15 ppb). The City is accelerating lead service line replacements, targeting over 3,000 lines in the next year using an equity-based prioritization model. Prior sampling protocol violations by Water Department staff are under investigation.

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Break on East Raynor Avenue
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break on the 800 block of East Raynor Avenue on November 21, 2024. Service disruptions and vehicle traffic closures are expected until repairs complete, with sidewalks and detours remaining available. This represents routine infrastructure maintenance with managed public impact.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Road Reconstruction Project on Milton Avenue
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced a road reconstruction project on Milton Avenue starting November 4, 2024, involving milling and paving from West Genesee Street to the city line. Work will occur 6 a.m. to 6 p.m. daily with an expected one-week completion timeline, weather permitting, while maintaining road access with potential traffic delays.

title: Mayor Walsh Announces DPW to Begin Overnight Leaf and Snow Removal
summary: Mayor Walsh announced that the Department of Public Works will begin overnight operations starting November 3, 2024, deploying snow-plowing crews for leaf collection during non-snow days. The city urges residents to bag leaves and keep them off roadways to prevent flooding and sewer backup issues.

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Break on East Fayette Street
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break on East Fayette Street (2000 block), causing water service disruptions and street closures between Croly Street and Westmoreland Avenue. Vehicle traffic is redirected; sidewalks remain open. Residents and businesses can contact the Department of Water for updates.

title: Department of Water Releases Citywide Water Service Inventory
summary: The Department of Water released a citywide water service line inventory compliant with EPA regulations, allowing residents to identify their service line material. Recent lead sampling results (10 ppb at 90th percentile) showed improvement from August findings, with exceedances in only 5 of 115 sampled homes. The City committed to replacing over 3,000 lead service lines in the coming year using $22.8M in state funding.
entities: New York State Department of Health

title: Mayor Walsh Appoints Robert Brandt to Commissioner of Water
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced the permanent appointment of Robert Brandt as Commissioner of the Department of Water, effective October 7, 2024. Brandt transitioned from Interim Commissioner (since June 2024) to permanent role, bringing 30+ years of municipal service experience. The appointment signals organizational stability in water infrastructure management and oversight.

title: Syracuse Water Crews to Repair Water Main Leak on West Street on Sep 8
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water will repair a water main transmission line on West Street near Franklin Square on September 8, 2024. The repair will require street closures between Fayette and Washington Street, potentially causing water service disruptions and traffic delays in the area.

title: DPW Announces End of Blue Bin Distribution for Recycling in the City
summary: The Syracuse Department of Public Works announced the discontinuation of blue bin distribution for recycling, aligning with Onondaga County Resource Recovery Agency's countywide policy. Starting October 7, residents will receive new 96-gallon covered recycling carts, a service improvement that builds on 2023 trash cart implementation and is expected to increase recycling participation and improve worker safety.

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Leak on West Street
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main leak on West Street between Erie Boulevard and West Fayette Street. The repair may cause water service disruptions and traffic delays in the area, with the Erie Boulevard exit closed until work is completed. Residents and businesses can contact the Department of Water at (315) 448-8360 for questions.

title: Water Department Closes S Warren St for Fire Service Repair
summary: The Syracuse Water Department is closing Warren Street between Jefferson and Fayette Streets to repair a fire service line. No water service disruptions are expected for residents and businesses; vehicle traffic is detoured while sidewalks remain open and parking garage exits are permitted.

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Road Reconstruction Project on Butternut Street
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced a road reconstruction project on Butternut Street beginning July 20, 2024, involving milling and paving from Lodi to North Salina Street. Work will occur 6 a.m. to 6 p.m. with single-lane traffic reductions and one-week estimated timeline, weather permitting. Sidewalks remain accessible and alternate routes are advised.
entities: City of Syracuse Office of Communications

title: Water Department Public Meeting for Westminster Avenue Area Neighbors
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water will host a public meeting on June 26, 2024, to inform Westminster Avenue area residents about the Westminster Pump Station project, which includes replacement of water mains and lead-containing service lines at no cost to property owners. The project affects approximately 50 homes in the Westcott neighborhood and is part of the Water Department's long-term infrastructure plan to improve water quality and maintain safe pressure.

title: Fire and Water Department Warn: Opening Hydrants Risks Public Safety
summary: The Syracuse Fire Department and Department of Water issued a public safety warning against opening fire hydrants during heat waves, citing risks to water pressure, emergency response capability, and public safety. Violators face civil penalties up to $500. Residents are directed to city pools and spray features as alternatives.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Hydrant Branch on Locksley Road
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a hydrant branch on the 200 block of Locksley Road, requiring shutdown of Guildford Pump Station and temporary water service disruption through afternoon of June 17, 2024. Locksley Road between William Drive and Euclid Avenue is closed to vehicle traffic; sidewalks remain open with detours in place.

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Break on North Geddes Street
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break on the 500 block of North Geddes Street. Vehicle traffic on North Geddes Street between West Genesee Street and West Belden Avenue will be closed until repairs are complete, with sidewalks remaining open and detours in place. Residents can contact the Department of Water at (315) 448-8360 for questions.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Road Reconstruction Project on Peat Street
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced a road reconstruction project on Peat Street beginning May 25, 2024, involving milling and paving operations from Burnet Avenue to Erie Boulevard. The road will close daily from 6 a.m. to 6 p.m. for approximately one week, with traffic detours in place and businesses remaining accessible via Erie Boulevard.

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Leak on Midland Avenue
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is actively repairing a water main leak at the intersection of West Colvin Street and Midland Avenue. Service disruptions are expected in the area during repairs, and traffic impacts are compounded by an ongoing Onondaga County infrastructure project. The CIO should note potential coordination needs between city water infrastructure systems and county projects.

title: Water Crews Continue Repairs to Water Main Break on Shonnard Street
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is conducting repairs on a water main break located on the 100 block of Shonnard Street. Service disruptions are expected in the area, with Shonnard Street between West Street and Niagara Street closed to vehicle traffic during repairs. Sidewalks remain accessible and traffic detours are in place.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: Water Crews Continue Repairs to Water Main Break on Velasko Road
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is conducting repairs on a water main break located on the 400 block of Velasko Road. Water service disruptions are expected throughout the day, with Velasko Road between West Onondaga Street and Bellevue Avenue closed to vehicle traffic until repairs are completed. Sidewalks remain accessible and traffic detours are in place.

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Break on Velasko Road
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break on the 400 block of Velasko Road. Velasko Road between West Onondaga Street and Bellevue Avenue is closed to vehicle traffic; residents and businesses may experience water service disruptions. Crews are actively maintaining water services while completing repairs.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: City of Syracuse Announces Water Department Public Meeting on April 4
summary: Syracuse Water Department will host a public information meeting on April 4, 2024 at Nottingham High School to inform residents about the Brookford Pump Station project. The project will replace water mains and lead-containing service lines at no cost to property owners in the Meadowbrook neighborhood, affecting approximately 750 homes across multiple streets.

title: Mayor Walsh Announces the Return of Full Fleet of Veo
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced the return of Veo's full micromobility fleet to Syracuse for the 2024 season following winter reduction. The program demonstrated strong growth in 2023 with 433,300 rides and nearly 1,000 scooters/e-bikes available, replacing over 100,000 car trips annually and supporting the city's sustainable transportation and equity goals.

title: Mayor Walsh Announces 2024 Road Reconstruction Plans for the City
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced Syracuse's 2024 road reconstruction plan involving nearly 23 miles of repaving and 30 miles of seal-and-resurface work across all city quadrants, funded through local, state, and federal sources. The City DPW and Department of Engineering will execute the projects using data-driven assessment methods including SMTC road condition ratings, with two major Dig-Once infrastructure coordination projects concluding this season.
depts: Office of the Chief Operating Officer

title: Water Crews Working to Repair Water Main Break on North Clinton Street
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break at North Clinton Street and West Division Street. Water service disruption affects the 600 block of North Clinton Street with expected restoration by 8 pm; the intersection will remain closed to vehicle traffic during repairs.
entities: City of Syracuse Department of Water

title: Sewer Department to Complete Repair Work on S Clinton St on Feb 18
summary: Syracuse Sewer Department will complete repair work on South Clinton Street (between West Fayette and Walton Streets) on Sunday, February 18, 2024, starting at 7 am. The road will be closed to vehicle traffic during repairs, with sidewalks remaining open and traffic detours in place. Residents can contact Cityline for questions.

title: Mayor Walsh Appoints Robert Brandt to Deputy Commissioner of Water
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh appointed Robert Brandt as Deputy Commissioner in the Department of Water, effective February 9, 2024. Brandt brings 30 years of municipal service experience, including roles in sanitation, sewer and water maintenance, and most recently as Assistant Superintendent of Street Cleaning/Snow and Ice. He will oversee day-to-day operations, program development, and personnel management within the water department.

title: City of Syracuse to Collect Christmas Trees in January
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Public Works will collect natural Christmas trees curbside in January 2024 using a quadrant-based pickup schedule (Jan. 6-7, 13-14, 20-21, 27-28). Residents must remove all decorations and non-organic materials; artificial trees should be disposed of via regular trash service or bulk pickup. Alternative drop-off locations at OCRRA compost sites are available Jan. 2-12.

title: Home for the Holidays Tree Lighting Street Closures
summary: The City of Syracuse will close nine street segments in downtown Clinton Square on Friday, November 24, 2023, from 4-8 p.m. for the "Home for the Holidays" Tree Lighting event. The closure affects major thoroughfares including Clinton Street, Erie Boulevard, Water Street, Salina Street, Genesee Street, and James Street in the downtown core.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Downtown Syracuse Overnight Parking Restrictions In Effect
summary: The City of Syracuse has implemented seasonal overnight parking restrictions in downtown areas effective November 1 through April 1 to facilitate snow removal operations. Odd-even alternating parking on designated blocks between 10 p.m. and 6 a.m. improves street clearance efficiency and pedestrian/vehicle safety. Violations subject to ticketing and towing; signage and parking alternatives are available through the Downtown Committee.

title: Water Crews Working To Repair Water Main Break On West Fayette Street
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a water main break at South Clinton and West Fayette Street. Service disruption affects the 200 block of West Fayette Street with expected restoration by 6 pm; the intersection remains closed to vehicle traffic with sidewalks open and detours in place.

title: I-81 to Close Overnight Between Exits 16A & 17
summary: The New York State Department of Transportation will close I-81 between exits 16A and 17 in Syracuse during overnight hours (6 p.m. to 6 a.m.) beginning November 15, 2023, to remove steel bridge beams for the I-481 southbound ramp bridge demolition as part of the I-81 Viaduct Project. Signed detours will direct traffic via I-481 North, Brighton Avenue, and Route 11; additional closures dependent on weather and work progress will be announced Friday.
entities: New York State Department of Transportation

title: City Of Syracuse To Begin Issuing Citations For Trash Cart Violations
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Public Works will begin issuing $100 citations on November 1, 2023, for improper trash cart setout violations. After a 7-week grace period following the September citywide expansion of the sanitation cart program, DPW will enforce requirements including bagging waste, closing lids, proper placement at collection time, and storing carts off-street when not in use.

title: Franklin Square Fountain to be Temporarily Removed for Restoration
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Parks & Recreation is removing the cast metal fountain in Franklin Square Park (601 Plum Street) for restoration, beginning October 31, 2023. Robinson Iron will dismantle and transport the fountain to Alabama for stripping, repair, painting, and corrosion prevention, with completion expected by mid-2024. The fountain, originally fabricated in 1991, has been a neighborhood landmark since the late 1980s redevelopment.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: City Administrative Offices Closed Monday, October 9 for Columbus Day
summary: City of Syracuse administrative offices will close Monday, October 9, 2023 for Columbus Day federal holiday. Trash/recycling collection continues on regular schedule; metered parking is free; Department of Public Works yard waste drop-off site closes; emergency services remain available at 315-448-8360.

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Final Stage of West Genesee Street Project
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced the final stage of the West Genesee Street 'Dig Once' infrastructure project, with repaving beginning October 9 on a 2.3-mile stretch from South Salina to Myrtle Street. The project coordinates utility replacements (water, sewer, telecom, gas) to minimize future excavations, with paving operations expected to complete by end of November while keeping the road open to traffic.
entities: City of Syracuse Office of Communications

title: Annemarie Deegan Named First Deputy Commissioner of Finance
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh appointed Annemarie Deegan as First Deputy Commissioner of Finance, the newly created position. Deegan will oversee centralized financial operations, payroll, capital management, risk mitigation, and modernization initiatives across City departments. She brings 15 years of City service, including roles in Parks and the Office of Management and Budget, and is pursuing an MBA.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Syracuse Wins Transportation Project of the Year
summary: Syracuse DPW received the Transportation Project of the Year award from the Upstate Chapter of the Institute of Transportation Engineers for its data-driven Speed Hump Pilot Program. Since 2021, the City has installed over a dozen traffic-calming speed humps and cushions designed collaboratively with the Fire Department to support the Mayor's Vision Zero initiative aimed at eliminating traffic fatalities and serious injuries.

title: Street Closing Information for 2023 Festa Italiana
summary: Festa Italiana festival will operate September 15-17, 2023 in front of City Hall with multi-stage street closures beginning September 14 through September 18. Setup in the KeyBank Building adjacent lot and surrounding streets (East Washington, Montgomery, Market, East Water Streets) will be sequentially closed starting at 9 a.m., 1 p.m., and 5 p.m. Thursday through Monday morning reopening.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Repaving West Genesee Street Near Downtown
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced a West Genesee Street repaving project near downtown Syracuse beginning September 11, 2023, involving milling and paving from North Clinton Street to West Street. The project is part of the "Dig Once" initiative coordinating with utility providers to replace underground infrastructure and minimize future excavations, with work expected to complete in one week during daytime hours while keeping roads open.

title: City of Syracuse Warns of Traffic Delays on East Colvin Street
summary: The City of Syracuse is warning of traffic delays on East Colvin Street between I-81 and Jamesville Avenue during September 7-14, 2023 due to milling and paving work. The project includes full reconstruction with new pavement, bike lanes, sidewalks, water mains, sewer rehabilitation, and landscaping, expected to complete by end of October. One-lane traffic with flaggers will manage flow; evening reopening to two-way traffic occurs when work halts.

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Road Reconstruction on Martin Luther King East
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced a road reconstruction project on Martin Luther King East between South Salina Street and Renwick Avenue, beginning September 7, 2023. The project involves milling and paving with road closures to through traffic except at South State Street and Oakwood Avenue intersections, contingent on favorable weather conditions.

title: Mayor Walsh Urges Employers to Offer Centro Services to Employees
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced a three-year agreement making Centro bus service free to City of Syracuse employees as an employee benefit, approved unanimously by Common Council. The initiative aims to improve employee recruitment and retention while reducing transportation costs, with service launching later in September 2023.

title: 2023 Irish Festival Street Closing Information
summary: The City of Syracuse announces street closures in Clinton Square from September 7-10, 2023 for the Irish Festival (September 8-9). Three streets will be fully or partially closed: Clinton Street (Genesee to Washington), Erie Boulevard West (Clinton to Franklin), and West Water Street (Clinton to Franklin, converted to two-way with parking restrictions). This is a standard traffic management notice for a recurring cultural event.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs
Department of Transportation and Infrastructure Services

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Distribution of 36,500 New Trash Carts
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced the citywide distribution of 36,500 new covered trash carts to all Syracuse households, with delivery completed by mid-September. The semi-automated pickup system uses tipper-arm equipped trucks to reduce worker injuries and improve street cleanliness. This infrastructure modernization represents a significant operational change requiring resident compliance with new setout rules and storage requirements.

title: Street Closing Information for 2023 Mary Nelson's Youth Day
summary: The City of Syracuse announced street closures around Sankofa Park on August 19, 2023, for Mary Nelson's Youth Day and Annual School Supply Giveaway event. Three streets (S. Salina Street, E. Colvin Street, and Wood Ave) will be closed from 8 a.m. to 6-7 p.m. to facilitate the community event.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Street Closing Information for 2023 Arts Week Downtown
summary: Downtown Syracuse will implement street closures July 27-30, 2023 for two major events: the Northeast Jazz & Wine Festival at Clinton Square and the 52nd Annual Syracuse Arts & Crafts Festival at Columbus Circle. Multiple street segments will be fully or partially closed during these dates to accommodate event setup and operations.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: West Fayette Street Closed for Sink Hole Repair
summary: The City of Syracuse Department of Water is repairing a sinkhole on the 100-block of West Fayette Street between South Salina and Clinton Streets. The street is closed to vehicular traffic beginning June 22, 2023, with no disruption to water service. Businesses remain accessible via sidewalk, and residents can contact Cityline for questions.

title: Road Closures for 2023 Jazz Fest
summary: The City of Syracuse announced street closures in downtown areas (Clinton Street, Erie Boulevard West, West Water Street, and East Water Street) from June 22-25, 2023 to support the Syracuse Jazz Fest event at Hanover Square and Clinton Square. Multiple blocks will be fully closed or converted to two-way traffic with parking restrictions beginning at 6:30-7:00 a.m. on Thursday, June 22.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: Road Closures for 2023 Juneteenth Festival
summary: The City of Syracuse announced street closures in the downtown Clinton Square area from June 15-18, 2023 to accommodate the annual Juneteenth Festival. Multiple streets including Clinton Street and Erie Boulevard West will be fully closed, while West Water Street will operate as two-way traffic. The festival parade runs Saturday, June 17, 11 a.m.–12 p.m., with expected delays on South State and South Salina streets.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: City Announces Lane Restrictions for Greenspace Maintenance on West St
summary: City of Syracuse announced a greenspace maintenance project on West Street South (200-300 block of West Fayette Street) scheduled for Saturday, June 10, 2023. Two right lanes will be closed from 6 a.m. to 1 p.m. for shrub removal and soil preparation; wildflower seeds will be planted later. The left lane remains open during work.
depts: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs

title: City Invites Public Input on South Side Infrastructure Project
summary: The City of Syracuse Engineering Department is soliciting public input on the Midland Avenue Paving Project on the South Side through June 20, 2023. The $1.3M+ infrastructure initiative includes road resurfacing, new sidewalk construction, traffic signal replacement, and ADA accessibility upgrades, with construction scheduled to begin March 2025. A public meeting was held June 6 at Cecile Community Center.

title: New Covered Trash Carts Begin Arriving at Phase One Homes
summary: Syracuse is launching Phase One of a citywide sanitation modernization program, deploying 6,800 covered trash carts to ~20% of residential properties starting May 30, 2023, with mandatory use beginning June 5. The semi-automated pickup system aims to reduce litter, improve worker safety, and streamline service; full citywide rollout of remaining 33,000 properties planned for later in 2023, with recycling carts in 2024.
depts: Department of Sanitation and Solid Waste

title: Mayor Walsh Announces Release of Trash Cart Rollout Fact Sheet
summary: Mayor Ben Walsh announced a phased rollout of 95-gallon lidded trash carts for semi-automated sanitation pickup, beginning summer 2023 with 20% of routes before full citywide implementation. The initiative prioritizes worker safety, sanitation, and operational efficiency, with recycling carts planned for 2024. Common Council ordinance changes are under consideration to enable the program.

title: Mayor Owens Opens Public Commenting Period for Climate Action Plan
summary: Mayor Sharon F. Owens announced the opening of a public commenting period for Syracuse's draft inaugural Climate Action Plan, which addresses greenhouse gas emissions and climate resilience. The plan was developed through community engagement and will remain open for feedback until April 21, 2026, before final adoption by the Common Council. The initiative is funded in part by New York State's Climate Smart Communities Grant Program.
entities: New York State Department of Environmental Conservation

title: DPW To Convert Flashing Traffic Signal To Standard Signals This Spring
summary: DPW will convert two intersections (S. Geddes & W. Onondaga Streets; Grant Boulevard & Lemoyne Avenue) from flashing yellow/red signals to standard three-color traffic signals on April 1, 2026. This bi-annual seasonal conversion supports spring/summer traffic flow after winter weather protocols end; the reverse conversion occurs in November.
